topics.title: Determination of Field Capacity, PWP and Water Holding Capacity of Soil
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAGL408 - SOIL SCIENCE & MECHANICS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Determination of Field Capacity, PWP and Water Holding Capacity of Soil # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Soil Water — The Big Picture Soil is not just a pile of mineral particles. It is a **three-phase system** consisting of solid particles (minerals + organic matter), liquid (soil water + dissolved solutes), and gas (soil air). The **relative proportion of these phases** dictates nearly every behavior we study in Soil Mechanics & Agronomy — from compaction to irrigation scheduling. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — PCAGL408):** > Soil water is classified into different categories based on the **energy status** (matric suction/pressure potential) at which it is held. The three most operationally important categories for an Agronomy/Civil lab are **Field Capacity (FC), Permanent Wilting Point (PWP), and Water Holding Capacity (WHC)**. ## 1.2 Formal Definitions (KTU Standard Wording) | Term | Formal Definition | Energy Reference | |---|---|---| | **Field Capacity (FC)** | The amount of water held in the soil after excess gravitational water has drained away and the rate of downward movement has materially ceased. | ~ **33 kPa (1/3 bar)** suction | | **Permanent Wilting Point (PWP)** | The soil moisture content at which plants can no longer extract sufficient water to recover from wilting, even when transpiration is stopped. | ~ **1500 kPa (15 bar)** suction | | **Water Holding Capacity (WHC)** | The maximum amount of water a soil can retain against gravity, including both capillary and hygroscopic water (often measured at near-saturation after free drainage). | ~ **0 to 10 kPa** suction | | **Available Water Capacity (AWC)** | The portion of soil water that is actually usable by plants. | **AWC = FC − PWP** | ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Soil as a Sponge Apartment" Imagine the soil as a **high-rise sponge building**: - **Ground floor apartments** (large pores / macropores) — When you soak the sponge, water rushes out instantly through the ground floor (these are **gravity drains**). This water is *gravitational water* — held too loosely for plants. - **Middle-floor apartments** (medium pores / mesopores) — Water that stays here after the gravity drains have emptied is what a plant can sip through a straw. This is **Field Capacity** — the *bank account* of plant-available water. - **Penthouse apartments** (micropores) — Water here is held so tightly by surface tension (matric forces) that plant roots cannot generate enough suction to pull it out. This is the **Permanent Wilting Point** — locked away. - **The basement safe** (bound water / hygroscopic water) — Chemically adsorbed to clay particle surfaces; even oven drying at 105°C removes only part of it. This water is **never available**. > [!NOTE] > **Why does this matter?** > An irrigation engineer or agronomist needs to know how much water to *apply* and how much the *crop can drink*. The difference FC − PWP gives the **plant-available water reservoir**. Sandy soils have small reservoirs; clay soils have large but mostly locked reservoirs. Loams are the sweet spot. ## 1.4 The Soil Water Continuum (Energy Hierarchy) As matric suction increases, water becomes progressively harder to extract: $$\text{Saturation} \;>\; \text{WHC} \;>\; \text{Field Capacity} \;>\; \text{ capillary water} \;>\; \text{PWP} \;>\; \text{Hygroscopic Coefficient} \;>\; \text{Oven Dry}$$ **Bold constants used in KTU labs:** - Oven drying temperature: **105–110 °C** for 24 hours. - PWP standard suction: **1500 kPa** (≈ **15 bar**). - FC standard suction: **33 kPa** (≈ **1/3 bar**). - 1 bar = **100 kPa** = **1020 cm of water column** ≈ **10.2 m H₂O**. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Conceptual Framework — The Three Energy States Soil water is governed by the **soil water potential** concept. Total water potential is: $$\psi_{total} = \psi_{matric} + \psi_{osmotic} + \psi_{gravitational} + \psi_{pressure}$$ For a **saturated soil at the bottom of a freshly irrigated field**, gravity dominates → water drains (gravitational water). Once drainage ceases, the remaining water is held by **matric potential** (capillary + adsorptive forces) against gravity. - **FC** corresponds to a matric potential of approximately **−1/3 bar (≈ −33 kPa)** in most agricultural soils. - **PWP** corresponds to a matric potential of approximately **−15 bar (≈ −1500 kPa)**. - **WHC** is empirically measured at **near-zero suction** (often at −0.1 to −0.3 bar depending on soil texture). ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic: Why these Definitions? 1. **Saturation** = All pores water-filled. Porosity (n) ≈ volumetric water content at saturation. 2. **Drainage phase** = Gravity empties macropores (> 50 µm equivalent diameter). 3. **Equilibrium reached** = Soil water held only by matric forces at FC suction → **Field Capacity**. 4. **Plants extract water** by building up root suction. Once plant suction (~ 15 bar) matches soil suction, no more water uptake → **Wilting Point**. 5. **Beyond PWP** → Water exists as thin films on particle surfaces (hygroscopic). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Gravimetric Water Content (θg) | $\theta_g = \dfrac{M_w - M_d}{M_d} \times 100$ | % | $M_w$ = wet mass; $M_d$ = oven-dry mass | | 2 | Volumetric Water Content (θv) | $\theta_v = \theta_g \times \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_w}$ | % or m³/m³ | $\rho_b$ = bulk density; $\rho_w$ = 1.0 g/cm³ | | 3 | Available Water Capacity | $AWC = FC - PWP$ | % (gravimetric) | Plant-usable water per 100 g dry soil | | 4 | Available Water per unit depth | $AW_d = AWC \times \rho_b \times D$ | mm | D = root zone depth | | 5 | Water Holding Capacity (WHC) | $WHC = \dfrac{M_{sat} - M_d}{M_d} \times 100$ | % | $M_{sat}$ = mass after saturation + free drainage | | 6 | Bulk Density | $\rho_b = \dfrac{M_d}{V_t}$ | g/cm³ | $V_t$ = total (field) volume | | 7 | Saturation % | $S = \dfrac{V_w}{V_v} \times 100$ | % | $V_v$ = volume of voids | | 8 | pF value | $pF = \log_{10}(h)$ | dimensionless | h = suction head in cm water | | 9 | Convert suction (bar) → pF | $pF = \log_{10}(h_{cm})$ where $h_{cm} = bar \times 1020$ | dimensionless | FC → pF ≈ 2.53; PWP → pF ≈ 4.18 | | 10 | Depth of water in root zone | $d = \theta_v \times D$ | mm | For irrigation scheduling | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL for KTU lab report:** Always report **gravimetric** water content unless specifically asked for volumetric. Mention the **oven temperature (105 °C)** and **drying duration (24 h)** explicitly — examiners reward this. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | Parameter Used | |---|---| | **Irrigation scheduling** (drip / sprinkler design) | FC, PWP, AWC | | **Drought-tolerant crop selection** | AWC vs crop demand curve | | **Landfill cover design** (geo-environmental) | WHC to predict leachate | | **Earthen dam / embankment design** | FC vs optimum moisture for compaction | | **Climate-smart agriculture & DSSAT/FAO models** | FC, PWP, AWC as primary input | | **Soil classification (USDA)** | Available water capacity differentiates silt loams vs sandy loams | # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Available Water Capacity (AWC) The plant-available water per unit mass of soil is the algebraic difference between the upper and lower plant-extractable limits. **Step 1:** Define FC in gravimetric form. $$FC = \frac{M_{w,FC} - M_d}{M_d} \times 100 \quad \text{(\% by mass)}$$ **Step 2:** Define PWP in gravimetric form. $$PWP = \frac{M_{w,PWP} - M_d}{M_d} \times 100 \quad \text{(\% by mass)}$$ **Step 3:** Subtract PWP from FC (since PWP < FC numerically). $$\begin{aligned} AWC &= FC - PWP \\ &= \frac{(M_{w,FC} - M_d) - (M_{w,PWP} - M_d)}{M_d} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{M_{w,FC} - M_{w,PWP}}{M_d} \times 100 \end{aligned}$$ **Conversion logic:** The dry mass $M_d$ cancels between the two limits because the *same* soil sample (or same bulk density zone) is referenced. This means AWC can be measured from the **mass of water released between FC and PWP** per unit dry soil mass. **Step 4:** Convert gravimetric AWC to **volumetric form** using bulk density. $$\theta_{v,AWC} = \frac{(FC - PWP) \times \rho_b}{100}$$ **Step 5:** Express as **depth of water** in root zone of depth D (mm). $$\text{Available Water (mm)} = \frac{(FC - PWP)}{100} \times \rho_b \times D \times 10$$ The factor 10 converts cm to mm if D is in cm. If D is in mm, omit the factor. ## 3.2 Derivation of Water Holding Capacity (WHC) — Laboratory Procedure **Step 1:** Take a clean dry perforated metallic box (or a funnel with Whatman No. 42 filter paper). Record empty mass = $M_b$. **Step 2:** Fill with air-dry soil. Record mass = $M_b + M_s$ (where $M_s$ = mass of air-dry soil). **Step 3:** Saturate by placing the box in a tray of water and letting it imbibe from below for 24 h (capillary saturation avoids trapped air). **Step 4:** Lift, allow gravity drainage for ~ 30 minutes. Wipe external moisture. Record mass = $M_{b} + M_{s,sat}$. **Step 5:** Oven-dry at 105 °C for 24 h. Cool in desiccator. Record mass = $M_b + M_d$. **Step 6:** Compute: $$WHC = \frac{(M_b + M_{s,sat}) - (M_b + M_d)}{(M_b + M_d) - M_b} \times 100 = \frac{M_{s,sat} - M_d}{M_d} \times 100$$ The **box mass cancels**, leaving a clean ratio. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Lab Record Style) **Problem:** A soil sample of bulk density **1.35 g/cm³** has gravimetric **FC = 28 %** and **PWP = 12 %**. The effective root zone depth is **60 cm**. Calculate: 1. Available water capacity (gravimetric). 2. Available water capacity (volumetric). 3. Total available water in mm in the root zone. 4. The pF value at PWP. **Solution:** **(1) Gravimetric AWC:** $$AWC_g = FC - PWP = 28 - 12 = 16\%$$ **(2) Volumetric AWC:** $$\theta_{v,AWC} = \frac{(FC - PWP) \times \rho_b}{100} = \frac{16 \times 1.35}{100} = 0.216 \; m^3/m^3 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 21.6\%$$ **(3) Depth of available water (D in cm):** $$AW_d = \theta_{v,AWC} \times D = 0.216 \times 60 \;\text{cm} = 12.96 \;\text{cm} = 129.6 \;\text{mm}$$ **(4) pF at PWP:** $$h_{cm} = 15 \;\text{bar} \times 1020 = 15300 \;\text{cm}$$ $$pF = \log_{10}(15300) = 4.185$$ ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Method | Parameter | Lab Method | Field Method | Time Required | Accuracy | |---|---|---|---|---| | Field Capacity | Pressure plate at 1/3 bar | Ponded irrigation + sampling after 48 h | Lab: 3 d; Field: 3 d | Lab > Field | | PWP | Pressure plate at 15 bar | Sunflower / biological indicator | Lab: 5 d; Field: 7–10 d | Lab > Biological | | WHC | Perforated box / funnel | — | Lab: 2 d | High | | Saturation % | Saturation paste | Standing water table | < 1 h | High | | Hygroscopic coeff. | H₂SO₄ desiccator (10% RH) | — | 7–14 d | Moderate | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Soil Water Continuum & Suction Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD Sat[Saturation
0 kPa suction
All pores water-filled] --> Grav[Free Drainage
Gravity drains macropores
over 2 to 3 days] Grav --> FC[Field Capacity FC
minus 33 kPa suction
Upper plant limit] FC --> Plant[Plant Uptake Zone
Roots extract water
matric suction rises] Plant --> PWP[Permanent Wilting Point PWP
minus 1500 kPa suction
Lower plant limit] PWP --> Hyg[Hygroscopic Water
Adsorbed films on clay
below minus 31 bar] Hyg --> Oven[Oven Dry State
105 degree C for 24 h
Reference zero] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Experimental Workflow (Lab Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Step 1 Collect soil sample
Air dry and sieve 2 mm] --> B[Step 2 Weigh sample
Record wet mass Mw] B --> C[Step 3 Oven dry
105 degree C for 24 h] C --> D[Step 4 Cool in desiccator
30 minutes] D --> E[Step 5 Weigh oven dry
Record dry mass Md] E --> F[Compute theta g
Mw minus Md over Md times 100] F --> G{Which parameter?} G -->|Saturation drainage| H[Report as FC] G -->|1500 kPa extraction| I[Report as PWP] G -->|Free drainage after saturation| J[Report as WHC] H --> K[Compute AWC equals FC minus PWP] I --> K J --> L[Report WHC percent] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Available vs Unavailable Water Partition ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph TotalWater[Total Soil Water Content] direction TB Grav2[Gravitational Water
Drains in 2 to 3 days
Not available]:::grey Avail[Available Water
FC minus PWP
Uptake by roots]:::green Unav[Unavailable Water
Below PWP
Held at minus 1500 kPa]:::red end classDef grey fill:#d3d3d3,stroke:#555,color:#000 classDef green fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 classDef red fill:#f08080,stroke:#8b0000,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Apparatus Reference Table | Apparatus | Purpose | Specification | |---|---|---| | Perforated soil box (Brass) | WHC determination | Volume ~ 100 cm³, perforations 1 mm | | Whatman No. 42 filter paper | Hold soil during WHC test | Slow flow, fine retention | | Moisture cans (Aluminium) | Hold samples during oven drying | Pre-weighed with lid | | Hot-air oven | Drying | 105 ± 2 °C, forced convection | | Desiccator with silica gel | Cooling without moisture regain | 30 min cool-down | | Analytical balance | Mass measurement | ± 0.01 g precision | | Pressure plate apparatus | FC & PWP at controlled suction | 1/3 bar & 15 bar ceramic plate | | Graduated cylinder | Saturation volume measurement | ± 1 mL | # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define Field Capacity (FC) and Permanent Wilting Point (PWP). State the standard suction values at which each is conventionally defined.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Field Capacity (FC)** — *1 mark* The moisture content of soil after excess gravitational water has drained away and downward movement has ceased, typically 2–3 days after saturation/irrigation. - **Permanent Wilting Point (PWP)** — *1 mark* The soil moisture content below which plants cannot recover turgidity even when transpiration is stopped; plants wilt permanently. - **Standard suction values** — *1 mark* FC ≡ **33 kPa (1/3 bar)** and PWP ≡ **1500 kPa (15 bar)**. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Distinguish between Gravimetric and Volumetric water content. Give the relationship between them.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Gravimetric (θg) — *1 mark* | Volumetric (θv) — *1 mark* | |---|---| | Mass of water per unit mass of dry soil. $\theta_g = \dfrac{M_w - M_d}{M_d} \times 100$ in %. | Volume of water per unit bulk volume of soil. $\theta_v = \dfrac{V_w}{V_t}$ in m³/m³ or %. | **Relationship** — *1 mark*: $$\theta_v = \theta_g \times \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_w}$$ where $\rho_b$ = bulk density of soil (g/cm³) and $\rho_w$ = density of water = 1.0 g/cm³. --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. Students often **confuse WHC with FC** — remember WHC is measured *immediately* after saturation + free drainage, while FC uses 2–3 day drainage or 1/3 bar suction. > 2. **Always cancel the container mass** when computing % water content — forgetting this loses 1–2 marks. > 3. Units matter: KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark for each **missing unit**. State θ in %, AWC in mm. > 4. In derivation questions, **show every algebraic step** — skipping transitions loses full credit. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a)** Define Available Water Capacity (AWC). Derive an expression for the depth of water available to crops in a given root zone depth in terms of FC, PWP, and bulk density. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the laboratory procedure for the determination of Field Capacity and Water Holding Capacity of a given soil sample. State any two precautions. **(7 marks)** --- **Solution:** **(a) Derivation [7 marks]:** - **Definition [1 mark]:** Available Water Capacity is the portion of soil water between Field Capacity (FC) and Permanent Wilting Point (PWP) that can be absorbed by plant roots. $$AWC = FC - PWP$$ - **Step 1** — Gravimetric AWC: $$AWC_g = FC - PWP \quad \text{(\% by mass)}$$ - **Step 2** — Convert to volumetric using bulk density $\rho_b$ and water density $\rho_w$: $$\theta_{v,AWC} = \frac{(FC - PWP)}{100} \times \frac{\rho_b}{\rho_w}$$ Since $\rho_w = 1$ g/cm³ for practical purposes: $\theta_{v,AWC} = \dfrac{(FC - PWP) \times \rho_b}{100}$ — *[2 marks for the conversion step]* - **Step 3** — Depth of water available in root zone of depth D (in mm): *[4 marks]* $$\begin{aligned} AW_d \;(\text{mm}) &= \theta_{v,AWC} \times D \;(\text{in mm}) \\ AW_d &= \frac{(FC - PWP) \times \rho_b \times D}{100} \end{aligned}$$ For D in cm: $AW_d = \dfrac{(FC - PWP) \times \rho_b \times D \times 10}{100}$ (mm). **(b) Laboratory Procedure [7 marks]:** **Field Capacity (3.5 marks):** 1. Collect a representative soil sample in a moisture box; weigh immediately to record **wet mass** $M_w$. 2. Place in hot-air oven at **105 °C** for 24 hours. 3. Cool in desiccator for 30 min; record **oven-dry mass** $M_d$. 4. Compute: $$FC \; (\%) = \frac{M_w - M_d}{M_d} \times 100$$ *(Alternative: use Pressure Plate Apparatus at 1/3 bar suction — describe soil saturation, equilibration on ceramic plate under pressure, and gravimetric measurement of equilibrated moisture content.)* **Water Holding Capacity (3.5 marks):** 1. Take a perforated metallic box; record empty mass $M_b$. 2. Fill with air-dry soil (~ 50 g); record total mass $(M_b + M_s)$. 3. Place in a water tray for **24 h** for capillary saturation from below (avoids trapped air). 4. Lift, drain for 30 min, wipe external moisture, weigh → $(M_b + M_{s,sat})$. 5. Oven-dry at 105 °C for 24 h, cool in desiccator, weigh → $(M_b + M_d)$. 6. Compute: $$WHC \; (\%) = \frac{M_{s,sat} - M_d}{M_d} \times 100$$ **Precautions [included above; mention 2 explicitly]:** - Saturate from below to avoid air entrapment. - Cool in desiccator before weighing; otherwise hot samples register lighter mass falsely. - Filter paper must be changed between samples. - Take triplicate readings and report the **mean**. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(a)** Explain the soil water continuum from saturation to oven-dry state with neat energy values (pF / suction). List the categories of soil water based on availability to plants. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A sandy loam soil has gravimetric FC = **22 %** and PWP = **9 %**. The bulk density is **1.40 g/cm³** and effective root zone depth is **50 cm**. Calculate (i) AWC gravimetric, (ii) AWC volumetric, (iii) Total available water in mm, and (iv) pF at field capacity. **(7 marks)** --- **Solution:** **(a) Soil Water Continuum [7 marks]:** The following categories exist as matric suction increases (and water becomes progressively less available): | Stage | Suction | pF | Availability | |---|---|---|---| | Saturation | 0 kPa | 0 | Free water drains by gravity | | Gravitational water | < 33 kPa | < 2.5 | Drains in 2–3 days; not available | | **Field Capacity** | **−33 kPa** | **2.53** | Upper plant-available limit | | Capillary water | 33 to 1500 kPa | 2.53 to 4.18 | **Plant-available water** | | **Permanent Wilting Point** | **−1500 kPa** | **4.18** | Lower plant-available limit | | Hygroscopic water | 1500 to 31000 kPa | 4.18 to 5.5 | Unavailable | | Oven-dry water | > 31 000 kPa | > 5.5 | Removed at 105 °C | **Categories based on plant availability [3 marks]:** - Gravitational water (unavailable — drains) - Available / capillary water (FC − PWP) - Unavailable water (below PWP; held in hygroscopic + bound form) *(Energy values and pF table — 4 marks)* **(b) Numerical Problem [7 marks]:** **Given:** FC = 22 %, PWP = 9 %, $\rho_b = 1.40$ g/cm³, D = 50 cm. **(i) Gravimetric AWC [1.5 marks]:** $$AWC_g = FC - PWP = 22 - 9 = \mathbf{13\%}$$ **(ii) Volumetric AWC [1.5 marks]:** $$\theta_{v,AWC} = \frac{(FC - PWP) \times \rho_b}{100} = \frac{13 \times 1.40}{100} = \mathbf{0.182 \;\; m^3/m^3 \;\; (18.2\%)}$$ **(iii) Total available water in mm [2 marks]:** $$\begin{aligned} AW_d &= \theta_{v,AWC} \times D \;\; (\text{cm}) \times 10 \\ &= 0.182 \times 50 \times 10 \\ &= \mathbf{91 \;\; mm} \end{aligned}$$ **(iv) pF at Field Capacity [2 marks]:** $$h_{cm} \;(\text{at FC}) = \frac{1}{3} \times 1020 = 340 \;\text{cm}$$ $$pF = \log_{10}(340) = \mathbf{2.53}$$ --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **FC = upper plant-available limit** at **−33 kPa (1/3 bar)**; measure 2–3 days after saturation. - ✅ **PWP = lower plant-available limit** at **−1500 kPa (15 bar)**; plant cannot recover turgor. - ✅ **WHC ≠ FC** — WHC is the *total* moisture held after saturation + free drainage (includes more water than FC because gravity has not yet finished draining). - ✅ **AWC = FC − PWP** → the **plant-available water reservoir** (in %). - ✅ Depth form: $AW_d = \dfrac{(FC - PWP) \times \rho_b \times D}{100}$ (D in cm) → result in **mm**. - ✅ **pF** = $\log_{10}(h_{cm})$; FC → pF ≈ 2.53; PWP → pF ≈ 4.18. - ✅ Always **report in % for gravimetric** and **m³/m³ or % for volumetric**. - ✅ Always **cool in desiccator** before weighing; oven temperature = **105 °C**; drying time = **24 h**. - ✅ **Saturate from below** during WHC test to prevent air entrapment. - ✅ Container mass must be **tared/cancelled** when computing % water content. - ✅ Sandy soils → **low AWC**; clay soils → **high AWC but mostly unavailable**; loams → best for crops. - ✅ Triplicate readings → **report mean ± standard deviation** for full credit. - ✅ pF 2.53 = 1/3 bar; pF 4.18 = 15 bar — these two constants appear in **every** KTU numerical. - ✅ In lab report, mention **apparatus, principle, procedure, observation table, calculation, result, precautions, sources of error**. > [!NOTE] > **Final Study Tip:** When you sit for the KTU lab exam, expect the examiner to test (a) definitions + standard suctions, (b) one derivation linking θg, θv, ρb, and D, and (c) one numerical with at least 3 sub-parts. Memorize the **two anchor suctions (33 kPa & 1500 kPa)** and the **formula $AW_d = \theta_{v,AWC} \times D$** — they unlock 80% of marks.

topics.title: Determination of pH and EC of Soil
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL408 - SOIL SCIENCE & MECHANICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of pH and EC of Soil

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Soil pH — Formal Definition

**Soil pH** is defined as the negative logarithm (base 10) of the hydrogen ion activity $\left[\text{H}^+\right]$ in a soil-water suspension. It quantifies the **acidity** or **alkalinity** of the soil solution and is a master variable governing nutrient availability, microbial activity, and structural stability of the soil.

$$\text{pH} = -\log_{10}\left[\text{H}^+\right] = \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{[\text{H}^+]}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — pH Scale Range for Soils**
> Soils generally range from **pH 3 (extremely acidic)** to **pH 10 (strongly alkaline)**. The ideal agricultural range is **pH 6.0 – 7.5**. At KTU lab level, soil pH is reported to **one decimal place** (e.g., pH = 6.8).

### The pH Scale Interpretation

| pH Range | Soil Reaction | Indicator |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| < 4.5 | Extremely Acidic | Likely toxic Al³⁺, Fe²⁺ |
| 4.5 – 5.5 | Strongly Acidic | Lime required |
| 5.5 – 6.5 | Moderately Acidic | Suitable for tea, pineapple |
| 6.5 – 7.5 | Neutral | Ideal for most crops |
| 7.5 – 8.5 | Moderately Alkaline | Gypsum may be needed |
| > 8.5 | Strongly Alkaline | Sodicity hazard |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — pH as a "Sourness Meter"**
> Imagine soil water as a glass of lemonade. Adding lemon juice (H⁺ ions) makes it **sour (acidic, low pH)**. Adding baking soda (OH⁻ ions) makes it **bitter (alkaline, high pH)**. Pure water sits in the middle (**neutral, pH 7**). The pH meter is just an electronic "taste tester" that measures how much sourness or bitterness is dissolved in the soil water.

---

## 1.2 Soil Electrical Conductivity (EC) — Formal Definition

**Electrical Conductivity (EC)** of soil is the measure of the soil's ability to conduct an electric current, which is directly proportional to the concentration of **soluble salts** (ions such as $\text{Na}^+$, $\text{Cl}^-$, $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$, $\text{HCO}_3^-$) present in the soil solution. It is the reciprocal of electrical resistivity.

$$\text{EC} = \frac{1}{\text{Resistivity}} = \frac{L}{R \cdot A} \quad \text{[SI unit: S/m]}$$

The standard unit used in soil science is **dS/m** (deciSiemens per metre), which is **numerically equal** to **mS/cm** (milliSiemens per centimetre).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — EC Units & Conversion**
> $1 \text{ dS/m} = 1 \text{ mS/cm} = 1000 \text{ μS/cm} = 0.001 \text{ S/m}$. Total Dissolved Solids (TDS in mg/L) $\approx$ **EC (dS/m) × 640**.

### Soil Salinity Classification (USDA)

| EC (dS/m) | Salinity Class | Crop Impact |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 0 – 2 | Non-saline | No restriction |
| 2 – 4 | Slightly saline | Sensitive crops affected |
| 4 – 8 | Moderately saline | Yield reduction in most crops |
| 8 – 16 | Strongly saline | Only tolerant crops survive |
| > 16 | Very strongly saline | Few plants grow |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — EC as a "Salt Traffic Meter"**
> Think of EC as a **"busy road"**. When salt ions dissolve in soil water, they become tiny charged "buses" that carry electricity. The more salts, the more buses on the road, the higher the traffic (EC). Distilled water is an **empty road** (EC ≈ 0), while sea water is a **rush-hour highway** (EC ≈ 50 dS/m). An EC meter simply counts the traffic of charged particles between two electrodes.

---

## 1.3 Significance in Agriculture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why pH and EC Together?**
> They form the **"Twin Health Indicators"** of any soil. pH tells the **chemical character** (acidic/alkaline), while EC tells the **salt load**. Both are measured together in a single lab session using the same soil-water suspension — making this experiment one of the most economical and informative in soil science.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principle of pH Measurement

The measurement of pH is performed using a **potentiometric method** based on a **glass electrode** and a **reference electrode** immersed in the soil suspension. The glass electrode develops a potential proportional to the H⁺ ion activity as per the **Nernst Equation**.

$$E = E^0 - \frac{2.303 \, R \, T}{F} \cdot \text{pH}$$

Where:
- $E$ = measured cell potential (mV)
- $E^0$ = standard potential of the reference electrode
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)
- $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96485 C/mol**

At **25 °C (298 K)**, the slope simplifies to:
$$\frac{2.303 \, R \, T}{F} = 0.05916 \text{ V/decade} \approx 59.16 \text{ mV per pH unit}$$

## 2.2 Principle of EC Measurement

EC is measured using a **conductivity cell** (Kohlrausch cell) consisting of two platinum electrodes of defined geometry. An alternating current (AC) is applied to prevent polarization, and the resistance $R$ of the solution is measured. The **cell constant** $K$ converts resistance to conductivity.

$$\text{EC} = K \times \frac{1}{R} = K \times G$$

Where:
- $K$ = cell constant (cm⁻¹), typically **1.0 cm⁻¹**
- $R$ = resistance (ohms, $\Omega$)
- $G$ = conductance (siemens, S)

## 2.3 Sample Preparation Standards

| Parameter | pH Determination | EC Determination |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| **Soil : Water Ratio** | 1 : 2.5 (most common) | 1 : 2 (most common) |
| **Suspension Type** | Soil : Distilled Water | Soil : Distilled Water |
| **Equilibration Time** | 30 minutes (stirring) | 30 minutes (settling) |
| **Reading Mode** | Direct mV → pH | Direct cell constant × G |
| **Temperature** | Recorded & compensated to **25 °C** | Recorded & compensated to **25 °C** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a 1:2 or 1:2.5 ratio and not saturated paste?**
> The **saturated paste extract (1:1 by saturation)** is the most accurate but laborious. The 1:2 or 1:2.5 suspension is **routinely used in KTU labs** for speed and reproducibility. Conversion factors are applied if comparison with saturation extract is needed.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Unit / Constant | KTU Exam Relevance |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Definition of pH | $\text{pH} = -\log_{10}[\text{H}^+]$ | Dimensionless | Direct 3-mark question |
| 2 | Nernst Slope at 25 °C | $S = 0.05916 \text{ V/pH}$ | V per pH unit | Conceptual 3-mark |
| 3 | EC from Conductance | $\text{EC} = K \cdot G$ | dS/m | Calculation problem |
| 4 | Conductance from Resistance | $G = 1/R$ | Siemens (S) | Conceptual |
| 5 | Cell Constant | $K = L / A$ | cm⁻¹ | KCl calibration |
| 6 | TDS from EC | $\text{TDS} \approx \text{EC} \times 640$ | mg/L | Numerical problem |
| 7 | EC conversion (1:2 → Saturated Paste) | $\text{EC}_{e} = \text{EC}_{1:2} \times 2.5$ | dS/m | Numerical |
| 8 | pH Temperature correction | $\text{pH}_{25} = \text{pH}_T + 0.01 \times (T - 25)$ | — | Lab note |
| 9 | Calibration buffers | pH 4.0, 7.0, 9.2 | — | Mandatory step |
| 10 | EC standard (KCl 0.01 M) | **EC = 1.413 dS/m at 25 °C** | dS/m | Standard solution |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility**
> - pH guides **lime/gypsum requirement** in fertilizer recommendations.
> - EC guides **irrigation water suitability** and decides reclamation strategy for **saline/sodic soils**.
> - Used in precision agriculture for **variable-rate fertilizer application** via GPS-mapped sensors.

## 2.5 Boundary Conditions and Validity

- pH meter must be **buffered with at least two standard solutions** bracketing the expected pH (commonly pH 4 and pH 7 for acidic soils; pH 7 and pH 9.2 for alkaline soils).
- EC cell must be **calibrated with 0.01 M KCl** giving **1.413 dS/m at 25 °C** before each session.
- All readings must be **temperature-compensated to 25 °C** (most modern meters do this automatically using a built-in ATC probe).
- Suspension should be **stirred gently before pH reading** and **allowed to settle for supernatant** EC reading.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedure, Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Apparatus Required

- **Digital pH meter** with glass electrode and reference electrode (combined type)
- **Digital conductivity meter** with dip-type conductivity cell
- **Buffer solutions** pH 4.0, 7.0, and 9.2
- **Standard KCl solution (0.01 M)** for EC calibration
- **Beakers** (100 mL), **stirring rods**, **wash bottle** with distilled water
- **Analytical balance** (0.01 g accuracy)
- **Whatman No. 1 filter paper** (only for EC supernatant if required)
- **Thermometer** (0–100 °C, 0.1 °C accuracy)

## 3.2 Reagent Preparation

### A. Buffer Solution pH 4.0 (Potassium Hydrogen Phthalate)
- Dissolve **10.21 g of potassium hydrogen phthalate** in distilled water.
- Dilute to **1000 mL** in a volumetric flask.

### B. Buffer Solution pH 7.0 (Potassium Dihydrogen Phosphate + Disodium Hydrogen Phosphate)
- Dissolve **3.39 g KH₂PO₄** and **3.53 g Na₂HPO₄** in distilled water.
- Dilute to **1000 mL**.

### C. Buffer Solution pH 9.2 (Sodium Borate / Borax)
- Dissolve **3.81 g Na₂B₄O₇·10H₂O** in distilled water.
- Dilute to **1000 mL**.

### D. 0.01 M KCl Standard (for EC calibration)
- Dry AR-grade KCl at **60 °C for 2 hours**.
- Weigh **0.7456 g** and dissolve in distilled water to make **1000 mL**.
- This gives **EC = 1.413 dS/m at 25 °C**.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Procedure for pH Determination

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 lab manual prescribes the **1:2.5 soil:water suspension method** using an electrometric pH meter.

**Step 1 — Sampling and Preparation**
- Collect representative air-dried soil sample. Crush and sieve through a **2 mm sieve**.
- Weigh **20 g** of soil into a **100 mL beaker**.

**Step 2 — Suspension Preparation**
- Add **50 mL of distilled water** (soil:water = 1:2.5).
- Stir thoroughly for **30 seconds** using a glass rod.
- Allow the suspension to **stand for 30 minutes** with intermittent stirring.

**Step 3 — Calibration of pH Meter**
- Switch on the pH meter and allow it to **warm up for 15 minutes**.
- Rinse the electrode with distilled water and gently blot dry.
- Immerse in **pH 7.0 buffer** and adjust the **CAL/STD knob** until display reads **7.0**.
- Rinse and immerse in **pH 4.0 buffer** (or **pH 9.2** for alkaline range), adjust the **SLOPE knob** until the correct value is displayed.
- Repeat the calibration until readings are **stable to ± 0.05 pH unit**.

**Step 4 — Measurement**
- Rinse the electrode with distilled water.
- Lower the electrode into the soil suspension. Ensure the **glass bulb is fully immersed** in the liquid portion (avoid touching soil particles at the bottom).
- Stir gently and read the **stable pH value** to one decimal place.
- Record the **suspension temperature**.

**Step 5 — Cleaning**
- Rinse electrode with distilled water, **never wipe** (to avoid static charge).
- Store the electrode in **storage solution (3 M KCl)** — never in distilled water.

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Procedure for EC Determination

**Step 1 — Suspension Preparation**
- Weigh **20 g of air-dried soil** into a **100 mL beaker**.
- Add **40 mL of distilled water** (soil:water = 1:2).
- Stir for **1 minute** and let the suspension **stand for 30 minutes** to allow equilibration.

**Step 2 — Calibration of Conductivity Meter**
- Switch on the EC meter and allow it to stabilize.
- Rinse the conductivity cell with distilled water.
- Immerse in **0.01 M KCl** (EC = 1.413 dS/m at 25 °C).
- Adjust the **CAL knob** until display reads **1.41 dS/m**.
- Rinse again with distilled water.

**Step 3 — Measurement**
- Lower the conductivity cell into the clear **supernatant** of the soil suspension (do not disturb the sediment).
- Allow reading to stabilize (typically 15–30 seconds).
- Record EC value in **dS/m** to two decimal places.
- Record **suspension temperature**.

**Step 4 — Temperature Compensation**
- Most modern meters have **Automatic Temperature Compensation (ATC)**; if not, use the correction:
$$\text{EC}_{25} = \text{EC}_{T} \times \left[1 + 0.019 \times (T - 25)\right]$$

Where $T$ = measured temperature (°C) and 0.019 is the temperature coefficient for KCl-like solutions.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Examples (KTU Style)

### Example 1 — pH from Hydrogen Ion Concentration

A soil solution has $[\text{H}^+] = 2.5 \times 10^{-6}$ mol/L. Calculate the soil pH and comment on the soil reaction.

**Solution:**

$$\text{pH} = -\log_{10}(2.5 \times 10^{-6})$$

$$\text{pH} = -\left[\log_{10}(2.5) + \log_{10}(10^{-6})\right]$$

$$\text{pH} = -[0.3979 - 6]$$

$$\text{pH} = -(-5.6021)$$

$$\boxed{\text{pH} = 5.60}$$

**Interpretation:** pH 5.60 falls in the **moderately acidic** range (5.5–6.5). Suitable for crops like tea, maize; liming may be beneficial for legumes.

---

### Example 2 — EC Calculation from Conductance

A conductivity cell with cell constant **$K = 1.05$ cm⁻¹** measures a resistance of **820 Ω** in a soil extract at 25 °C. Calculate the EC in dS/m.

**Solution:**

$$G = \frac{1}{R} = \frac{1}{820} = 1.2195 \times 10^{-3} \text{ S}$$

$$\text{EC} = K \times G = 1.05 \times 1.2195 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\text{EC} = 1.280 \times 10^{-3} \text{ S/cm}$$

Converting to dS/m:
$$1 \text{ S/cm} = 100 \text{ dS/m} \implies \text{EC} = 1.280 \times 10^{-3} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\text{EC} = 0.128 \text{ dS/m}}$$

**Interpretation:** EC < 2 dS/m → **non-saline soil**, no salinity hazard.

---

### Example 3 — Conversion of EC (1:2 to Saturated Paste)

EC of a 1:2 soil-water suspension = **1.20 dS/m** at 25 °C. Estimate the EC of the saturated paste extract.

**Solution:**

$$\text{EC}_{e} \approx \text{EC}_{1:2} \times 2.5$$

$$\text{EC}_{e} = 1.20 \times 2.5 = 3.00 \text{ dS/m}$$

**Interpretation:** Saturated paste EC = 3.0 dS/m → **slightly saline**, sensitive crops may be affected.

---

### Example 4 — TDS from EC

A soil extract has EC = **2.4 dS/m**. Calculate the approximate Total Dissolved Solids (TDS) in mg/L.

**Solution:**

$$\text{TDS} \approx \text{EC} \times 640$$

$$\text{TDS} = 2.4 \times 640$$

$$\boxed{\text{TDS} = 1536 \text{ mg/L} \approx 1.54 \text{ g/L}}$$

---

## 3.6 Calibration Verification — KCl Standard

**Derivation of EC for 0.01 M KCl:**

$$\text{KCl} \rightarrow \text{K}^+ + \text{Cl}^- \quad \text{(concentration of each ion} = 0.01 \text{ M)}$$

The molar conductivity at infinite dilution:
$$\Lambda^0(\text{K}^+) = 73.5 \text{ S·cm}^2/\text{mol}, \quad \Lambda^0(\text{Cl}^-) = 76.4 \text{ S·cm}^2/\text{mol}$$

$$\Lambda^0_{\text{KCl}} = 73.5 + 76.4 = 149.9 \text{ S·cm}^2/\text{mol}$$

$$\kappa = \Lambda \times C = 149.9 \times 0.01 = 1.499 \text{ mS/cm}$$

At 25 °C, the accepted reference value is:
$$\boxed{\kappa_{0.01\,M\,\text{KCl}} = 1.413 \text{ dS/m at 25 °C}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This 1.413 dS/m standard is the **cornerstone of EC calibration** in all KTU undergraduate labs and is asked as a direct 3-mark question in viva.

## 3.7 Precautions (KTU Record-Writing Mandate)

- Always use **freshly prepared distilled water** with EC < 0.01 dS/m.
- Buffer and KCl standards must be **stored in polyethylene bottles**, never glass, to avoid ion exchange.
- **Air bubbles trapped** on the EC cell electrodes cause low readings — gently tap the cell.
- pH electrode must be kept **moist** in storage solution, never allowed to dry out.
- For alkaline soils, calibrate with **pH 7 and pH 9.2** buffers; for acidic, use **pH 4 and pH 7**.
- Record **two independent readings** that agree within **± 0.1 pH unit** or **± 5 % EC**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — pH Meter Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Soil Suspension 1:2.5] --> B[Glass Electrode]
    B --> C[High Impedance Amplifier]
    C --> D[ADC Converter]
    D --> E[Microcontroller Unit]
    E --> F[LCD Display pH Value]
    G[Reference Electrode KCl] --> C
    H[Buffer pH 4 and 7] --> I[Calibration Memory]
    I --> E
    J[Temperature Sensor ATC] --> E
```

**Explanation of Blocks:**
- **Glass Electrode** develops a potential proportional to H⁺ activity.
- **Reference Electrode** provides a stable reference potential.
- **High Impedance Amplifier** amplifies the tiny mV signal.
- **ADC** converts analog mV to digital.
- **Microcontroller** applies Nernst equation and temperature correction.
- **LCD** displays the final pH value compensated to 25 °C.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — EC Meter Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[AC Oscillator 1 kHz to 1 MHz] --> B[Current Driver]
    B --> C[Conductivity Cell 2 Pt Electrodes]
    C --> D[Soil Suspension 1:2]
    D --> C
    C --> E[Resistance Measurement Bridge]
    E --> F[Signal Conditioner]
    F --> G[Microcontroller]
    G --> H[LCD Display EC dS per m]
    I[ATC Temperature Sensor] --> G
    J[KCl 0.01 M Standard 1.413 dS per m] --> K[Cell Constant Calibration]
    K --> G
```

**Explanation of Blocks:**
- **AC Oscillator** prevents ionic polarization at electrodes.
- **Conductivity Cell** with defined cell constant $K$ measures solution resistance.
- **Bridge Circuit** determines $R$ precisely.
- **Microcontroller** computes $\text{EC} = K / R$ and applies ATC.
- **Display** shows the final EC value in dS/m at 25 °C.

## 4.3 Process Flowchart — Complete Experiment Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Air dry and sieve soil 2 mm] --> S2[Step 2: Weigh 20 g soil in 100 mL beaker]
    S2 --> S3{Choose ratio}
    S3 -->|pH test| S4[Add 50 mL distilled water 1:2.5]
    S3 -->|EC test| S5[Add 40 mL distilled water 1:2]
    S4 --> S6[Stir and equilibrate 30 min]
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7[Calibrate pH meter with pH 4 and 7]
    S6 --> S8[Calibrate EC meter with 0.01 M KCl]
    S7 --> S9[Immerse pH electrode in suspension]
    S8 --> S10[Immerse EC cell in supernatant]
    S9 --> S11[Record pH and temperature]
    S10 --> S12[Record EC and temperature]
    S11 --> S13[Apply ATC and report final values]
    S12 --> S13
    S13 --> S14[Classify soil: pH reaction and EC salinity]
```

## 4.4 Electrode Polarization Concept Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DC[Direct Current DC applied to cell]
        P1[Positive ion migration to cathode] --> Pol1[Build-up of charges on electrodes]
        Pol1 --> Err1[Erroneous low EC reading]
    end
    subgraph AC[Alternating Current AC applied to cell]
        P2[Reversal of polarity every cycle] --> Pol2[No net charge accumulation]
        Pol2 --> OK[Accurate EC reading]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why AC and not DC for EC measurement?**
> If DC current is used, ions migrate to the electrodes and form a **polarization layer**, drastically lowering the measured conductivity. AC at 1 kHz–1 MHz constantly reverses polarity, **preventing ion build-up** and giving the true conductance.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
**Define soil pH. Mention the ideal pH range for agricultural soils and the consequences of soil acidity.**

**Model Answer:**

Soil pH is the **negative logarithm of hydrogen ion concentration** in a soil-water suspension:
$$\text{pH} = -\log_{10}[\text{H}^+]$$

The **ideal pH range for most agricultural crops is 6.0 to 7.5** (slightly acidic to neutral).

**Consequences of soil acidity (pH < 5.5):**
- Reduced availability of **P, Ca, Mg, Mo**.
- Increased solubility of **Al³⁺, Mn²⁺, Fe²⁺** leading to toxicity.
- Suppression of **microbial activity** and nitrogen fixation.
- Poor soil structure and reduced crop yield.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is electrical conductivity of soil? State its unit and the standard KCl solution used for calibration.**

**Model Answer:**

Electrical conductivity (EC) is a measure of the soil's ability to conduct electric current, which depends on the **concentration of soluble salts** in the soil solution.

$$\text{EC} = \frac{1}{\text{Resistivity}} = K \cdot G \quad \text{[S/m]}$$

**Unit:** dS/m (deciSiemens per metre) or equivalently mS/cm.

**Standard calibration solution:** **0.01 M KCl** at 25 °C, which gives an EC of **1.413 dS/m**.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)
> *(Internal Choice — Answer ANY ONE)*

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **potentiometric method** of soil pH determination with a neat labelled diagram of a digital pH meter. List the buffer solutions used for calibration and the precautions to be taken during measurement.

**(b) [7 Marks]** The following data were obtained in a soil science lab:
- Soil sample weight = 20 g
- Distilled water added = 50 mL (1:2.5 suspension)
- pH meter reading = 6.42
- Suspension temperature = 28 °C

Calculate the **H⁺ ion concentration** and the **pH corrected to 25 °C**. Comment on the soil reaction.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **principle and procedure** for the determination of electrical conductivity of a soil sample using a conductivity meter. Mention the role of the **cell constant** and the **standard KCl solution**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A soil suspension (1:2) gave an EC reading of **0.85 dS/m at 30 °C**. The cell constant of the conductivity cell is **1.10 cm⁻¹**. Calculate the **EC corrected to 25 °C** and the **approximate TDS in mg/L**. Classify the soil salinity status.

---

## COMPLETE MODEL SOLUTIONS

### Question A — Solution

#### Part (a) [7 Marks] — Potentiometric pH Method

**Principle:**
The pH meter measures the **electromotive force (EMF)** developed between a **glass electrode** (sensitive to H⁺) and a **reference electrode** (calomel or Ag/AgCl) immersed in the soil suspension. The EMF is related to pH via the **Nernst equation**:
$$E = E^0 - \frac{0.05916 \text{ V}}{1} \cdot \text{pH} \quad \text{(at 25 °C)}$$

**Apparatus:** Combined glass-reference electrode, digital pH meter, buffer solutions (pH 4.0, 7.0, 9.2), beakers, distilled water, stirrer, thermometer.

**Procedure:**
1. Air-dry and sieve the soil sample through a 2 mm sieve.
2. Weigh 20 g of soil into a 100 mL beaker.
3. Add 50 mL of distilled water to give a 1:2.5 suspension.
4. Stir for 30 seconds and let stand for 30 minutes.
5. **Calibrate** the pH meter with **pH 7 buffer** (set STD) and **pH 4 buffer** (set SLOPE).
6. Immerse the electrode in the suspension, stir gently, and record the stable reading.
7. Rinse the electrode with distilled water between samples.

**Precautions:**
- Electrode must not touch soil particles at the bottom.
- Buffer solutions must be fresh and stored in plastic bottles.
- Temperature must be recorded and compensated.
- Do not wipe the electrode — blot dry only.

#### Part (b) [7 Marks] — Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- pH at 28 °C = 6.42
- Temperature correction factor: **+0.01 pH per °C** above 25 °C (for soil systems)

**Step 1: H⁺ concentration from observed pH**
$$[\text{H}^+] = 10^{-6.42}$$

$$[\text{H}^+] = 3.80 \times 10^{-7} \text{ mol/L}$$

**Step 2: Temperature correction**
$$\text{pH}_{25} = \text{pH}_{28} - 0.01 \times (28 - 25)$$

$$\text{pH}_{25} = 6.42 - 0.03 = 6.39$$

**Step 3: Recalculate H⁺ at 25 °C**
$$[\text{H}^+]_{25} = 10^{-6.39} = 4.07 \times 10^{-7} \text{ mol/L}$$

**Comment on soil reaction:** pH 6.39 lies in the **slightly acidic to neutral** range (6.5–7.0 borderline). The soil is **suitable for most field crops** with no immediate lime requirement.

---

### Question B — Solution

#### Part (a) [7 Marks] — EC Determination Principle & Procedure

**Principle:**
EC is determined by measuring the **electrical resistance** of a soil-water suspension using a **conductivity cell** containing two platinum electrodes of fixed geometry. AC current is used to prevent polarization. The cell constant $K$ converts measured resistance to conductivity:
$$\text{EC} = K \cdot G = \frac{K}{R}$$

**Cell Constant:** $K = L/A$, where $L$ = distance between electrodes and $A$ = area of electrodes. Determined by calibration with **0.01 M KCl** (known EC = 1.413 dS/m at 25 °C).

**Procedure:**
1. Take 20 g of air-dried, sieved soil in a 100 mL beaker.
2. Add 40 mL of distilled water (1:2 ratio).
3. Stir for 1 minute and allow to stand for 30 minutes.
4. **Calibrate** EC meter with 0.01 M KCl → set to 1.41 dS/m.
5. Immerse the conductivity cell in the clear supernatant.
6. Record the stable EC reading in dS/m and the temperature.
7. Apply ATC correction if not automatic.

**Role of Cell Constant:** Converts the measured conductance (which depends on electrode geometry) into a **standardized conductivity value** independent of cell shape, enabling comparison between instruments.

**Role of KCl Standard:** Provides a **known reference EC** to verify the cell constant and ensure instrument accuracy.

#### Part (b) [7 Marks] — Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- $\text{EC}_{30} = 0.85$ dS/m
- Cell constant $K = 1.10$ cm⁻¹
- Temperature coefficient = 0.019 per °C

**Step 1: Temperature correction to 25 °C**
$$\text{EC}_{25} = \text{EC}_{30} \times \left[1 + 0.019 \times (30 - 25)\right]^{-1}$$

$$\text{EC}_{25} = \frac{0.85}{1 + 0.019 \times 5} = \frac{0.85}{1.095}$$

$$\text{EC}_{25} = 0.776 \text{ dS/m}$$

**Step 2: TDS calculation**
$$\text{TDS} = \text{EC}_{25} \times 640$$

$$\text{TDS} = 0.776 \times 640 = 496.6 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 3: Salinity classification**
Since $\text{EC}_{25} = 0.776$ dS/m $< 2$ dS/m, the soil is classified as **NON-SALINE** (USDA). No yield reduction expected due to salinity; no special management required.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Forgetting that 1 dS/m = 1 mS/cm is numerically equal but S/m ≠ dS/m. Always write the final unit.
> 2. **Sign error in temperature correction:** A positive temperature gives a lower EC at 25 °C (cooling effect). Use the **division formula** correctly: $\text{EC}_{25} = \text{EC}_T / [1 + 0.019(T-25)]$.
> 3. **Soil:water ratio confusion:** pH uses **1:2.5**; EC uses **1:2**. Mixing these up loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Buffer error:** Calibration with only **one buffer** is incomplete; minimum **two** are required, one on either side of the expected pH.
> 5. **Neglecting temperature effect:** All EC/pH readings must be **temperature-compensated** to 25 °C for KTU reporting.
> 6. **TDS factor:** The factor **640** applies for EC in dS/m; for mS/cm the factor is the same since units are equal. Do not use 1000.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 Important Things to Remember Before Exam**

- **pH definition:** $\text{pH} = -\log[\text{H}^+]$; range 0–14; soils usually 4–9.
- **EC definition:** Conductance per unit length; **unit dS/m**; range 0–>16 for soils.
- **Soil:Water ratio:** **1:2.5 for pH**, **1:2 for EC** (KTU standard).
- **Buffer standards:** pH 4.0, 7.0, 9.2 — calibrate with **at least two** bracketing the expected range.
- **KCl standard:** **0.01 M KCl = 1.413 dS/m at 25 °C** — the universal EC calibration reference.
- **Cell constant:** $K = L/A$, typically ~1.0 cm⁻¹; verified using KCl standard.
- **Nernst slope:** 59.16 mV per pH unit at 25 °C — direct exam value.
- **TDS conversion:** $\text{TDS (mg/L)} \approx \text{EC (dS/m)} \times 640$.
- **Temperature coefficient:** 0.019 per °C for EC correction.
- **Salinity classes:** Non-saline (<2), Slightly (2–4), Moderately (4–8), Strongly (8–16), Very strongly (>16) dS/m.
- **EC method:** AC current essential to **avoid polarization**.
- **pH method:** Potentiometric, glass electrode + reference electrode.
- **Agricultural optimum:** pH 6.0–7.5; EC < 2 dS/m for most crops.
- **EC of soil water** is **higher** than EC of irrigation water (concentration effect on drying).
- **Saturated paste EC** is roughly **2.0–2.5 times** the 1:2 suspension EC.
- **Storage of electrode:** Keep pH glass electrode **moist in 3 M KCl**, never in distilled water.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Field Density of Soil Using Core Cutter Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL408 - SOIL SCIENCE & MECHANICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Field Density of Soil Using Core Cutter Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Field Density (In-situ Density / Bulk Density):** The mass of soil per unit volume measured at its natural, undisturbed state in the field, expressed in **g/cc** (grams per cubic centimetre) or **kN/m³** (kiloNewtons per cubic metre).

The **Field Density of Soil ($\rho$ or $\gamma$)** is a critical geotechnical parameter that quantifies how tightly the soil particles are packed together under actual field conditions. Unlike laboratory density tests (such as the Proctor compaction test), the field density test measures the soil **in its natural state** without any artificial compaction or remoulding.

### The Core Cutter Method (IS 2720: Part XXIX – 1975, Reaffirmed 2015)

The **Core Cutter Method** is a standard, direct, and highly reliable procedure used to determine the in-situ (field) density of **fine-grained, cohesive, and soft soils** — particularly soils that can be readily cut and trimmed without fracturing. The method is standardised by the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** under the code **IS 2720 (Part XXIX)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Cutter Method — Definition:** A destructive in-situ density test in which a steel cylindrical core cutter of known dimensions is driven into the ground to extract an undisturbed soil sample, which is then weighed to determine the bulk density. The moisture content of the extracted soil is determined separately to compute the **dry density**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are making a **cake** 🎂. You mix flour, sugar, eggs, and butter into a batter. The batter has a certain density — a measure of how much "stuff" is packed into a given space. Now, after baking, the cake settles and becomes denser.

In geotechnical engineering, soil in the field behaves similarly — but **we cannot just scoop it into a measuring cup** because it is locked in the ground! To find out how dense the soil is in its natural condition, engineers push a **hollow steel tube (the core cutter)** into the soil, much like pressing a **cookie cutter into dough** 🍪. The soil trapped inside the cutter represents an exact, undisturbed slice of the ground. By weighing this slice and knowing the cutter's exact volume, we can precisely calculate the field density.

### Why is Field Density Critical?

- **Compaction Quality Control:** Verifies whether field compaction meets design specifications for **embankments, road subgrades, dam cores, and airfield pavements**.
- **Relative Compaction Calculation:** Compared against **Maximum Dry Density (MDD)** from Proctor tests to compute the **Relative Compaction (%)**.
- **Bearing Capacity & Settlement:** Directly influences a soil's load-bearing capacity and consolidation behaviour.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Use Case:** During highway construction, every compacted soil layer must achieve ≥ **95%–98% of MDD** as per **MoRTH (Ministry of Road Transport and Highways) specifications**. The Core Cutter test is the field verification tool used at every 500 m² to 1000 m² interval.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Reference |
|-----------|---------------|-----------|
| Acceleration due to gravity | **9.81 m/s²** | IS Code General |
| Density of water | **1 g/cc** or **1000 kg/m³** | Universal |
| Unit weight of water | **9.81 kN/m³** | IS 2720 |
| Standard core cutter volume | **1000 cc** | IS 2720 Part XXIX |
| Standard core cutter height | **130 mm** | IS 2720 Part XXIX |
| Standard core cutter diameter | **100 mm** | IS 2720 Part XXIX |

# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Underlying Scientific Principle

The Core Cutter Method rests on a single, elegant principle: **Mass per Unit Volume**.

When a steel cylinder of precisely known internal volume is driven into the ground, the soil that fills the cylinder represents a true, undisturbed, in-situ sample. By measuring the **mass of this contained soil** and dividing it by the **calibrated internal volume of the cutter**, we obtain the **bulk density** ($\rho$). Combined with the **moisture content** ($w$), we can derive the **dry density** ($\rho_d$) — the parameter actually used for engineering design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:** **Bulk Density** includes the weight of water + solids + air, while **Dry Density** represents only the solid particles per unit volume. Dry density is the **design parameter** for compaction control.

## 2.2 Apparatus Required

- **Core Cutter** — Seamless steel tube, dimensions as per IS 2720 Part XXIX.
- **Steel Dolly (Driving Cap)** — Placed on top of the cutter to distribute hammer impact evenly and prevent edge damage.
- **Rammer / Hammer** — Standard weight as per specifications.
- **Steel Rule** — For measuring cutter depth.
- **Palette Knife / Trimming Knife** — To trim excess soil flush with cutter ends.
- **Balances** — Accuracy of **1 g** for mass measurement.
- **Moisture Content Containers** — Clean, airtight metal cans.
- **Desiccator, Oven** — Maintained at **105°C–110°C** for 24 hours.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logical Theory

The theoretical workflow follows a strict, ordered sequence:

- **Step 1 — Site Preparation:** Clear and level a small area of the soil surface at the test location. The surface should be free of loose debris, vegetation, or organic matter.

- **Step 2 — Cutter Calibration:** Before use, the internal volume ($V$) of the core cutter is either:
  - **Calculated** from measured internal diameter and height, OR
  - **Verified** by filling with water of known mass and computing volume from the water's mass and density.

- **Step 3 — Driving the Cutter:** The core cutter, with the dolly placed on top, is driven into the soil using a rammer. The cutter must penetrate **to a depth equal to its full height** without rocking or tilting. Any voids at the surface must be backfilled with loose soil.

- **Step 4 — Excavation:** Soil surrounding the cutter is carefully dug out using a spade/hoe. The cutter with the contained soil is lifted out, with surplus soil carefully trimmed off from the ends using a palette knife to make it perfectly flush with the cutter walls.

- **Step 5 — Mass Determination:** The cutter + soil is weighed ($M_1$). The cutter is cleaned, dried, and weighed empty ($M_2$). The **mass of soil alone** is:
$$M_{soil} = M_1 - M_2$$

- **Step 6 — Moisture Determination:** A representative soil sample (at least **20 g** for fine soils, **50 g** for coarse soils) is extracted from inside the cutter for oven-drying to determine the **moisture content ($w$)** as a percentage.

- **Step 7 — Density Computation:** Using the known volume $V$, calculate bulk density and dry density using the governing equations.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — KTU BOARD EXAM CRITICAL**

| # | Formula Name | Governing Equation | Variable Description | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Volume of Core Cutter (calculated)** | $V = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^2 \cdot H}{4}$ | $D$ = Internal Diameter, $H$ = Internal Height | cm³ or m³ |
| 2 | **Volume (water calibration method)** | $V = \dfrac{M_w}{\rho_w}$ | $M_w$ = Mass of water filled, $\rho_w$ = 1 g/cc | cm³ |
| 3 | **Mass of Wet Soil in Cutter** | $M = M_1 - M_2$ | $M_1$ = Cutter + wet soil, $M_2$ = Empty cutter | grams |
| 4 | **Bulk Density (Field Density)** | $\rho = \dfrac{M}{V}$ | Mass of wet soil / Total volume | g/cc |
| 5 | **Moisture Content** | $w = \dfrac{M_w - M_d}{M_d} \times 100$ | $M_w$ = wet mass, $M_d$ = dry mass | % |
| 6 | **Dry Density (Master Equation)** | $\rho_d = \dfrac{\rho}{1 + w}$ | $\rho$ = Bulk density, $w$ = moisture (decimal) | g/cc |
| 7 | **Relative Compaction** | $RC = \dfrac{\rho_d (\text{field})}{\rho_d (\text{max, Proctor})} \times 100$ | Field dry density / MDD × 100 | % |
| 8 | **Unit Weight Conversion** | $\gamma = \rho \cdot g$ | $\rho$ in kg/m³, $g$ = 9.81 m/s² | kN/m³ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit Consistency Rule:** When $\rho$ is in **g/cc**, then $w$ must be expressed as a **decimal** (e.g., $15\% = 0.15$) inside the dry density equation. Mixing units (e.g., g/cc with %) is the **#1 source of mark loss** in KTU board exams.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

The Core Cutter Method is the **backbone of earthwork quality assurance** in India. It is mandatory for:
- **Highway & Expressway Projects** (per IRC and MoRTH guidelines).
- **Railway Embankments** (per RDSO specifications).
- **Earthen Dam Construction** (per IS 8826 and CWC guidelines).
- **Airport Runway Subgrade** verification.

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Master Density Equation

The bulk (field) density is fundamentally derived from the **definition of density**:

$$\rho = \frac{\text{Mass of substance}}{\text{Volume occupied by substance}}$$

For soil contained in the core cutter:

$$\rho = \frac{M_{soil}}{V_{cutter}}$$

Since $M_{soil}$ is measured directly (subtracting empty cutter mass from full cutter mass) and $V_{cutter}$ is calibrated before the test, the bulk density is computed directly.

### Derivation of Dry Density from Bulk Density

**Starting Point — Phase Relationship:**
For a soil mass of volume $V$, the total mass $M$ is the sum of **solid mass** ($M_s$) and **water mass** ($M_w$):

$$M = M_s + M_w$$

By the **definition of moisture content** ($w$):

$$w = \frac{M_w}{M_s}$$

Therefore:

$$M_w = w \cdot M_s$$

Substituting into the total mass equation:

$$M = M_s + w \cdot M_s = M_s(1 + w)$$

Rearranging for solid mass:

$$M_s = \frac{M}{1 + w}$$

**Bulk Density:**

$$\rho = \frac{M}{V} = \frac{M_s(1 + w)}{V}$$

**Dry Density (mass of solids per unit total volume):**

$$\rho_d = \frac{M_s}{V} = \frac{M}{V(1 + w)} = \frac{\rho}{1 + w}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The Master Equation:** $\rho_d = \dfrac{\rho}{1+w}$ is the most important formula in this experiment and is worth **3–4 marks** standalone in KTU board examinations.

## 3.2 Volume Derivation of a Cylindrical Core Cutter

A core cutter is a right circular cylinder. Its volume is derived from the area of a circle multiplied by the height:

**Area of internal cross-section:**

$$A = \frac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4}$$

where $D$ is the **internal diameter** of the cutter.

**Volume of the cylinder:**

$$V = A \times H = \frac{\pi \cdot D^2 \cdot H}{4}$$

where $H$ is the **internal height** of the cutter.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Numerical Example

> [!TIP]
> **This is a KTU-style problem. Solve it step-by-step as you would write in the answer booklet.**

**Problem Statement:**
A core cutter of internal diameter **10 cm** and internal height **15 cm** was used to determine the field density of a soil sample. The following observations were recorded:

- Mass of core cutter + wet soil = **3245 g**
- Mass of empty core cutter = **1245 g**
- Mass of wet soil sample for moisture content = **120 g**
- Mass of oven-dry soil sample = **108 g**

Calculate: **(a)** Bulk density, **(b)** Moisture content, **(c)** Dry density, and **(d)** Relative compaction if MDD = **1.65 g/cc**.

---

### **Solution — Complete Step-by-Step**

#### **Step A — Volume of the Core Cutter**

Given: $D = 10$ cm, $H = 15$ cm.

$$V = \frac{\pi \cdot D^2 \cdot H}{4}$$

$$V = \frac{\pi \cdot (10)^2 \cdot 15}{4}$$

$$V = \frac{\pi \cdot 100 \cdot 15}{4}$$

$$V = \frac{1500 \pi}{4}$$

$$V = 375\pi$$

$$V = 375 \times 3.14159 = 1178.10 \text{ cm}^3$$

$$\boxed{V \approx 1178.10 \text{ cc}}$$

#### **Step B — Mass of Wet Soil in Cutter**

$$M_{soil} = M_1 - M_2$$

$$M_{soil} = 3245 - 1245$$

$$\boxed{M_{soil} = 2000 \text{ g}}$$

#### **Step C — Bulk Density (Field Density)**

$$\rho = \frac{M_{soil}}{V}$$

$$\rho = \frac{2000}{1178.10}$$

$$\boxed{\rho \approx 1.698 \text{ g/cc}}$$

#### **Step D — Moisture Content**

$$w = \frac{M_w - M_d}{M_d} \times 100$$

where:
- $M_w = 120$ g (wet soil sample)
- $M_d = 108$ g (oven-dry soil sample)

$$w = \frac{120 - 108}{108} \times 100$$

$$w = \frac{12}{108} \times 100$$

$$w = 0.1111 \times 100$$

$$\boxed{w = 11.11\%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion for Dry Density Formula:** $w = 11.11\% = 0.1111$ (as decimal).

#### **Step E — Dry Density**

$$\rho_d = \frac{\rho}{1 + w}$$

$$\rho_d = \frac{1.698}{1 + 0.1111}$$

$$\rho_d = \frac{1.698}{1.1111}$$

$$\rho_d = 1.528 \text{ g/cc}$$

$$\boxed{\rho_d \approx 1.528 \text{ g/cc}}$$

#### **Step F — Relative Compaction**

$$RC = \frac{\rho_d(\text{field})}{\rho_d(\text{max})} \times 100$$

$$RC = \frac{1.528}{1.65} \times 100$$

$$RC = 0.9261 \times 100$$

$$\boxed{RC \approx 92.61\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A relative compaction of **92.61%** is **below the typical MoRTH specification of 95%**. The field compaction is **NOT acceptable** and the contractor must re-compact and re-test the layer.

---

## 3.4 Final Tabulated Result Sheet

| Parameter | Symbol | Computed Value | Unit |
|-----------|--------|----------------|------|
| Volume of cutter | $V$ | 1178.10 | cm³ |
| Mass of wet soil | $M$ | 2000 | g |
| Bulk density | $\rho$ | 1.698 | g/cc |
| Moisture content | $w$ | 11.11 | % |
| Dry density | $\rho_d$ | 1.528 | g/cc |
| Maximum dry density | MDD | 1.65 | g/cc |
| Relative compaction | $RC$ | 92.61 | % |

## 4.4 Limitation & Applicability Matrix

| Soil Type | Core Cutter Suitability | Alternative Test |
|-----------|-------------------------|------------------|
| Soft to medium stiff **clay** | ✅ Highly Suitable | — |
| **Silty** soils | ✅ Suitable | — |
| Loose sandy soils | ⚠️ Marginal (sample may collapse) | Sand Replacement Method |
| Gravelly / stony soils | ❌ Not Suitable | Sand Replacement Method |
| Hard, dense soils | ⚠️ Difficult to drive cutter | Sand Replacement / Rubber Balloon |
| Organic / peat soils | ❌ Not Suitable | Specialised methods |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Point to Remember:** The Core Cutter Method is suitable for **fine-grained, cohesive soils**. For **granular / coarse-grained soils**, the **Sand Replacement Method** (IS 2720 Part XXVIII) must be used.

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the term "Field Density of Soil" and state the principle of the Core Cutter Method.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Field Density of Soil** (also called in-situ density or bulk density) is defined as the mass of soil per unit volume measured in its natural, undisturbed state in the field. It is typically expressed in **g/cc** or **kN/m³**.

The **Core Cutter Method** is based on the principle that when a steel cylindrical cutter of known volume is driven into the ground, it traps an undisturbed soil sample. By measuring the mass of this contained soil and dividing it by the cutter's calibrated volume, the bulk density is obtained. The moisture content is determined separately by oven-drying, and the **dry density** is then calculated using:

$$\rho_d = \frac{\rho}{1 + w}$$

The method is standardised as per **IS 2720 (Part XXIX) – 1975**.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What are the limitations of the Core Cutter Method? In which type of soil is it most suitable?**

**Model Answer:**
The **Core Cutter Method** has the following limitations:

- Not suitable for **gravelly, stony, or rocky soils** because the cutter cannot penetrate or may get damaged.
- Not suitable for **hard, dense soils** as driving the cutter becomes very difficult.
- Unsuitable for **loose, cohesionless sandy soils** as the sample may collapse on extraction.
- Slight **disturbance** of soil may occur during driving.
- Provides a **single-point measurement** and may not represent the entire compacted area.

The method is **most suitable for soft to medium stiff, cohesive soils such as clays and silts**, where the soil can be cleanly cut and the sample retained within the cutter.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

**Describe the procedure for determining the field density of soil using the Core Cutter Method with a neat sketch. List the apparatus used and explain the calculations involved. (14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a) — Apparatus and Procedure (7 Marks)**

**Apparatus Required:**
1. Core cutter (steel cylinder of known internal volume — typically 1000 cc)
2. Steel dolly (driving cap)
3. Rammer or hammer
4. Steel rule
5. Palette knife / trimming knife
6. Spade and pickaxe for excavation
7. Balance (accuracy 1 g)
8. Moisture content containers
9. Thermostatically controlled oven (105°C–110°C)
10. Desiccator

**Step-by-Step Procedure:**

1. **Site Preparation:** Clean and level a small area of the soil surface at the test location. Remove all loose material, vegetation, and organic debris.

2. **Calibrate Core Cutter:** Measure the internal diameter ($D$) and internal height ($H$) of the cutter using a vernier caliper. Calculate the volume using:
$$V = \frac{\pi \cdot D^2 \cdot H}{4}$$

   Alternatively, verify the volume by filling the cutter with water of known mass and density.

3. **Drive the Cutter:** Place the core cutter vertically on the prepared surface. Position the steel dolly on top and drive the cutter into the soil using the rammer until its **full height** is embedded.

4. **Excavate:** Carefully dig around the cutter using a spade, ensuring the soil column inside remains undisturbed. Lift the cutter out cleanly.

5. **Trim:** Using a palette knife, trim the soil flush with both ends of the cutter. Remove any soil sticking to the outer surface.

6. **Weigh:** Weigh the cutter + wet soil ($M_1$). Empty, clean, dry the cutter, and weigh it empty ($M_2$).

7. **Moisture Sample:** Extract a representative soil sample (~20 g for fine soils) from inside the cutter, place in a moisture can, weigh, oven-dry at 105°C–110°C for 24 hours, and reweigh.

8. **Calculations:** Compute bulk density, moisture content, and dry density.

---

#### **Part (b) — Calculations and Result (7 Marks)**

The following formulas are used:

| Step | Formula |
|------|---------|
| Mass of wet soil | $M = M_1 - M_2$ |
| Bulk density | $\rho = M / V$ |
| Moisture content | $w = (M_w - M_d) / M_d \times 100$ |
| Dry density | $\rho_d = \rho / (1 + w)$ |
| Relative compaction | $RC = \rho_d(\text{field}) / \rho_d(\text{max}) \times 100$ |

**Worked Sample:**

| Observation | Value |
|-------------|-------|
| Internal diameter of cutter, $D$ | 10 cm |
| Internal height of cutter, $H$ | 13 cm |
| Mass of cutter + wet soil, $M_1$ | 2800 g |
| Mass of empty cutter, $M_2$ | 1200 g |
| Mass of wet soil (moisture), $M_w$ | 100 g |
| Mass of dry soil (moisture), $M_d$ | 90 g |

**Calculations:**

$$V = \frac{\pi \cdot (10)^2 \cdot 13}{4} = \frac{1300\pi}{4} = 325\pi = 1021.02 \text{ cc}$$

$$M = 2800 - 1200 = 1600 \text{ g}$$

$$\rho = \frac{1600}{1021.02} = 1.567 \text{ g/cc}$$

$$w = \frac{100 - 90}{90} \times 100 = 11.11\% = 0.1111$$

$$\rho_d = \frac{1.567}{1 + 0.1111} = \frac{1.567}{1.1111} = 1.410 \text{ g/cc}$$

**Result:** Field bulk density = **1.567 g/cc**, Dry density = **1.410 g/cc**, Moisture content = **11.11%**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]`

**Explain the significance of field density determination in civil engineering projects. Describe the Core Cutter Method in detail and derive the relationship between bulk density and dry density. (14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a) — Significance and Theory (7 Marks)**

**Significance of Field Density in Civil Engineering:**

- **Compaction Quality Control:** Ensures earthwork (embankments, subgrades, dam cores) meets specified density requirements.
- **Settlement Prediction:** In-situ density directly affects consolidation settlement of foundations.
- **Bearing Capacity:** Higher field density generally indicates higher shear strength and bearing capacity.
- **Permeability Control:** Denser soils have lower permeability, critical for water-retaining structures.
- **Compliance Verification:** Required by **MoRTH, IRC, IS, and CWC** specifications for acceptance of compacted layers.

**Theory of Core Cutter Method:**

The method, standardised as per **IS 2720 (Part XXIX)**, determines the in-situ density of cohesive soils. A steel cylinder of known volume is driven into the soil, the contained sample is weighed, and moisture content is determined separately.

**Suitability:** Fine-grained, cohesive soils (clays and silts). Not suitable for gravelly or hard soils.

---

#### **Part (b) — Derivation of Dry Density from Bulk Density (7 Marks)**

Consider a soil mass with total volume $V$, total mass $M$, mass of solids $M_s$, mass of water $M_w$, and moisture content $w$.

**Step 1:** By definition of moisture content:

$$w = \frac{M_w}{M_s} \implies M_w = w \cdot M_s$$

**Step 2:** Total mass of soil:

$$M = M_s + M_w = M_s + w \cdot M_s = M_s(1 + w)$$

**Step 3:** Solving for mass of solids:

$$M_s = \frac{M}{1 + w}$$

**Step 4:** Bulk density (total mass per total volume):

$$\rho = \frac{M}{V}$$

**Step 5:** Dry density (mass of solids per total volume):

$$\rho_d = \frac{M_s}{V}$$

**Step 6:** Substituting $M_s$ from Step 3:

$$\rho_d = \frac{M}{V(1 + w)} = \frac{\rho}{1 + w}$$

$$\boxed{\rho_d = \frac{\rho}{1 + w}}$$

This is the **master relationship** used universally in compaction control.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 MARK-LOSS ZONES — READ CAREFULLY BEFORE EXAM**

| # | Common Mistake | Mark Loss | Correct Practice |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Forgetting to convert $w$ from % to decimal in $\rho_d = \rho/(1+w)$ | **1 mark** | Always write $w = X\% = X/100$ before substituting |
| 2 | Using outside diameter instead of inside diameter for volume | **1–2 marks** | Use the **internal** diameter $D$ only |
| 3 | Not subtracting empty cutter mass | **1 mark** | Always compute $M = M_1 - M_2$ |
| 4 | Reporting density without units (g/cc or kN/m³) | **0.5 mark** | Always mention units explicitly |
| 5 | Confusing bulk density with dry density in the relative compaction formula | **2 marks** | Use **dry density** in numerator and denominator |
| 6 | Ovens not at correct temperature (105–110°C) | **Lab only** | Maintain thermostatically controlled oven |
| 7 | Not trimming cutter ends flush, leaving protruded soil | **Lab only** | Use sharp palette knife for clean trimming |
| 8 | Driving cutter with tilt, causing non-vertical penetration | **Lab only** | Keep cutter perfectly vertical during driving |

---
