- ⭐ **Core Cutter Method** is governed by **IS 2720 Part XXIX**.
- ⭐ Suitable for **fine-grained, cohesive soils** (clays & silts).
- ⭐ **Not suitable** for gravelly, rocky, hard, or cohesionless soils.

### Master Equations (Must Memorise)
- ⭐ $V = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^2 \cdot H}{4}$ → Volume of cutter
- ⭐ $M = M_1 - M_2$ → Mass of soil in cutter
- ⭐ $\rho = \dfrac{M}{V}$ → Bulk density
- ⭐ $w = \dfrac{M_w - M_d}{M_d} \times 100$ → Moisture content
- ⭐ $\rho_d = \dfrac{\rho}{1 + w}$ → **Dry density (most important)**
- ⭐ $RC = \dfrac{\rho_d(\text{field})}{\rho_d(\text{max})} \times 100$ → Relative compaction

### Standard Apparatus Values
- ⭐ Core cutter volume: typically **1000 cc** ($D = 100$ mm, $H = 130$ mm).
- ⭐ Oven temperature: **105°C – 110°C** for 24 hours.
- ⭐ Water density: **1 g/cc** = **1000 kg/m³**.
- ⭐ Acceleration due to gravity: **9.81 m/s²**.

### Procedure Sequence (Mnemonic: **C-D-D-E-T-W-M-O**)
- ⭐ **C**alibrate → **D**rive → **D**ig → **E**xcavate → **T**rim → **W**eigh → **M**oisture → **O**ven dry → Calculate.

### Engineering Acceptance
- ⭐ Field dry density is compared with **MDD from Proctor Test** (IS 2720 Part VII/VIII).
- ⭐ Typical specification: **≥ 95% relative compaction** for highways (MoRTH).
- ⭐ For runways and expressways: **≥ 98%** relative compaction required.

### Unit Conversion Reminder
- ⭐ 1 g/cc = 1000 kg/m³ = 9.81 kN/m³
- ⭐ Always convert $w$ from **percentage to decimal** before using $\rho_d = \rho/(1+w)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Tip:** In KTU board valuation, examiners look for **clearly stated formulas, proper unit notation, and neat stepwise calculations**. A perfectly labelled observation table with consistent units scores significantly higher than a calculation-heavy answer without proper presentation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Field Density Using Sand Replacement Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL408 - SOIL SCIENCE & MECHANICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Field Density Using Sand Replacement Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Field Density (In-situ Density)** of a soil is the mass of soil per unit volume measured in its natural, undisturbed state at the construction site. It is a critical geotechnical parameter used to evaluate the degree of compaction achieved during earthwork operations such as embankment construction, subgrade preparation, road bases, and dam construction.

The **Sand Replacement Method** (also called the Sand Cone Method) is a standard field test for determining the in-place density of compacted fine-grained and medium-grained soils. The method is codified under **IS 2720 (Part XXVIII) - 1974 (Reaffirmed 2010)** and **ASTM D1556**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Sand Replacement Method is a Core Lab Experiment (Module Experiment 2) of PCAGL408. Students are expected to perform the test, tabulate observations, calculate bulk density, dry density, moisture content, and degree of compaction, and present a complete lab report.

The method works on the principle of **displacement**: a small hole is excavated in the soil, the excavated soil is collected and weighed, and the volume of the hole is determined by filling it with sand of known (calibrated) bulk density.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Ice-Cream Scoop Method":**
> 
> Imagine you want to know how much cake is in a tightly packed lunchbox without opening the lid:
> 1. You take an ice-cream scoop and remove a portion of cake — you know the **weight of the cake** you removed.
> 2. To find the **volume** of the cavity, you fill it with measured quantities of dry rice (a free-flowing granular material) — the rice perfectly fills the void without compressing the cake.
> 3. The **density** is then simply: (weight of cake removed) ÷ (volume of the cavity filled by rice).
> 
> In the Sand Replacement Method, the **"cake"** is the compacted soil, the **"scoop"** is a hand tool, and the **"dry rice"** is **calibrated, free-flowing, uniformly graded sand** (passing 600 µm and retained on 150 µm IS sieve).

The sand must be **free-flowing, uniform, and non-cohesive** so that it fills the test hole completely without leaving voids and without penetrating the surrounding soil pores.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities & Standards

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Unit | Standard Reference |
|----------|--------|--------------|--------------------|
| Bulk Density (Field) | $\gamma_b$ | g/cm³ or kN/m³ | IS 2720 Part XXVIII |
| Dry Density | $\gamma_d$ | g/cm³ or kN/m³ | Derived from $\gamma_b$ and $w$ |
| Moisture Content | $w$ | % | IS 2720 Part II |
| Sand Calibration Density | $\gamma_{sand}$ | g/cm³ | Determined by calibration |
| Volume of Test Hole | $V_h$ | cm³ | From sand mass / $\gamma_{sand}$ |
| Degree of Compaction | $R$ | % | IS 2720 Part XV / Part VIII |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Acceptable Range of $\gamma_b$ in Practice:**
> - Loose sandy soils: **1.4 – 1.6 g/cm³**
> - Compacted clayey soils: **1.7 – 2.0 g/cm³**
> - Well-graded gravels: **2.0 – 2.2 g/cm³**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The "Why" Behind Each Step

The Sand Replacement Method relies on a simple principle of **mass-volume equivalence**:

$$\text{Volume of excavated soil} = \frac{\text{Mass of sand that fills the hole}}{\text{Calibrated bulk density of the sand}}$$

The success of the method depends on the following assumptions and conditions:

- The **calibrated sand** has a constant bulk density (achieved by free, consistent flow from a fixed height).
- The sand **does not penetrate** the surrounding natural soil (this is why coarse, free-draining sand of narrow gradation is used).
- The test hole is **representative** of the compacted layer being evaluated.
- The moisture content sample is **truly representative** of the soil removed from the hole.

## 2.2 Why This Specific Sand?

| Property | Specification | Engineering Justification |
|----------|---------------|---------------------------|
| Gradation | Passing 600 µm, Retained on 150 µm | Uniform, free-flowing, minimal segregation |
| Shape | Sub-rounded to rounded | Reduces bridging, ensures consistent flow |
| Cleanliness | Oven-dried, free from organic matter | Prevents density variation with moisture |
| Density (loose) | ~1.45–1.60 g/cm³ | Stable, reproducible bulk density |

## 2.3 Apparatus Required (As per IS 2720 Part XXVIII)

- **Sand-pouring cylinder** (typically 3 L or 15 L capacity) with a detachable conical funnel and shutter valve.
- **Calibrating container** (metal, of known internal volume, usually 1500 cm³).
- **Clean, dry, uniform sand** (passing 600 µm IS sieve, retained on 150 µm IS sieve).
- **Excavation tools**: hand tools, small pick, brushes, and spoons.
- **Metal tray** with a central hole (to support the cylinder directly over the test hole).
- **Weighing balance** (accuracy 1 g for cylinder sand; 0.1 g for moisture samples).
- **Moisture content containers** (air-tight, numbered).
- **Oven** (105°C – 110°C) for moisture determination.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Bulk density of calibrated sand | $\gamma_{sand} = \dfrac{M_{cs}}{V_c}$ | g/cm³ | $M_{cs}$ = mass of sand in calibrating container; $V_c$ = known volume |
| 2 | Mass of sand in test hole | $M_h = M_1 - M_2 - M_3$ | g | $M_1$ = (cylinder + sand) initial; $M_2$ = (cylinder + sand) after pouring; $M_3$ = mass of sand in cone |
| 3 | Volume of test hole | $V_h = \dfrac{M_h}{\gamma_{sand}}$ | cm³ | — |
| 4 | Bulk density of soil | $\gamma_b = \dfrac{M_s}{V_h}$ | g/cm³ | $M_s$ = mass of moist soil excavated |
| 5 | Moisture content | $w = \dfrac{M_w - M_d}{M_d} \times 100$ | % | $M_w$ = wet mass; $M_d$ = oven-dry mass |
| 6 | Dry density | $\gamma_d = \dfrac{\gamma_b}{1 + w}$ | g/cm³ | $w$ expressed as a decimal (e.g., 0.15 for 15%) |
| 7 | Degree/Ratio of Compaction | $R = \dfrac{\gamma_{d,field}}{\gamma_{d,max(lab)}} \times 100$ | % | Compared to IS Light/Heavy Compaction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Reminder for Markdown Tables:** The vertical pipe symbol $\vert$ inside a math expression is rendered using `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking table syntax. Always use LaTeX delimiters `$...$` around math expressions inside table cells.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Highway Embankments**: Verification of compaction per IRC/MoRTH specifications (typically ≥ 95% of MDD for subgrade, ≥ 98% for road bases).
- **Earthen Dams**: In-situ density checks of rolled clay cores (IS 8826).
- **Railway Track Bed**: Quality control of formation layers.
- **Building Foundations**: Compaction of plinth filling and backfill around foundations.
- **Landfill Liners**: Density verification of compacted clay liners (CCL) to ensure hydraulic conductivity targets.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Part A — Calibration of the Sand-Pouring Cylinder

The **bulk density of the sand** must be determined *before* the field test, using a calibrating container of known volume. This step is non-negotiable and is the foundation of all subsequent calculations.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Given (Calibration Data):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Mass of empty calibrating container, } m_c = 450\ \text{g} \\
&\text{Mass of container + sand (after filling), } m_{cs} = 2200\ \text{g} \\
&\text{Volume of calibrating container, } V_c = 1500\ \text{cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Mass of sand in the calibrating container:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{cs} &= m_{cs} - m_c \\
M_{cs} &= 2200 - 450 \\
M_{cs} &= 1750\ \text{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calibrated bulk density of sand:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_{sand} &= \frac{M_{cs}}{V_c} \\
\gamma_{sand} &= \frac{1750\ \text{g}}{1500\ \text{cm}^3} \\
\gamma_{sand} &= 1.1667\ \text{g/cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Mass of sand in the cone (determined by placing the cylinder on a flat glass plate):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_3 = 480\ \text{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The cone mass $M_3$ is a *constant* for a given sand and cylinder. It must be redetermined whenever the sand batch changes.

## 3.2 Part B — Field Test (Excavation & Sand Filling)

**Given (Field Observations):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Mass of cylinder + sand (before pouring), } M_1 = 9500\ \text{g} \\
&\text{Mass of cylinder + sand (after pouring into hole), } M_2 = 5750\ \text{g} \\
&\text{Mass of sand in cone depression, } M_3 = 480\ \text{g} \quad \text{(from calibration)} \\
&\text{Mass of moist soil excavated, } M_s = 3850\ \text{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Mass of sand that filled the test hole

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_h &= M_1 - M_2 - M_3 \\
M_h &= 9500 - 5750 - 480 \\
M_h &= 3270\ \text{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why subtract $M_3$?** When the cylinder is inverted, sand fills both the test hole **and** the conical depression at the base of the cylinder. We only want the sand that filled the actual soil hole — so the cone volume must be excluded.

### Step 5 — Volume of the test hole

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_h &= \frac{M_h}{\gamma_{sand}} \\
V_h &= \frac{3270\ \text{g}}{1.1667\ \text{g/cm}^3} \\
V_h &= 2802.86\ \text{cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Bulk density of the soil

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_b &= \frac{M_s}{V_h} \\
\gamma_b &= \frac{3850\ \text{g}}{2802.86\ \text{cm}^3} \\
\gamma_b &= 1.3736\ \text{g/cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Part C — Moisture Content Determination

**Given (Oven Drying Data):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Mass of wet soil sample, } M_w = 250\ \text{g} \\
&\text{Mass of oven-dry soil sample, } M_d = 220\ \text{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 — Moisture content (as a percentage)

$$
\begin{aligned}
w &= \frac{M_w - M_d}{M_d} \times 100 \\
w &= \frac{250 - 220}{220} \times 100 \\
w &= \frac{30}{220} \times 100 \\
w &= 13.636\ \%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 8 — Dry density of the soil

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_d &= \frac{\gamma_b}{1 + w} \quad \text{(with } w = 0.13636 \text{ as a decimal)} \\
\gamma_d &= \frac{1.3736}{1 + 0.13636} \\
\gamma_d &= \frac{1.3736}{1.13636} \\
\gamma_d &= 1.2088\ \text{g/cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Part D — Degree of Compaction (Assuming Proctor Test Result)

**Given:** Maximum Dry Density (MDD) from IS Light Compaction = $\gamma_{d,max} = 1.650\ \text{g/cm}^3$

### Step 9 — Relative Compaction (RC)

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{\gamma_{d,field}}{\gamma_{d,max}} \times 100 \\
R &= \frac{1.2088}{1.650} \times 100 \\
R &= 73.26\ \%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **73.26% is unacceptably low** for a subgrade (MoRTH requires ≥ 95% of MDD for subgrade, ≥ 98% for GS sub-base). The site engineer must re-compact the layer and retest.

## 3.5 Complete Analytical Matrix — Summary Table

| S.No. | Quantity | Calculated Value | Unit |
|-------|----------|------------------|------|
| 1 | Mass of sand in calibrating container, $M_{cs}$ | 1750 | g |
| 2 | Bulk density of sand, $\gamma_{sand}$ | 1.1667 | g/cm³ |
| 3 | Mass of sand in cone, $M_3$ | 480 | g |
| 4 | Mass of sand in hole, $M_h$ | 3270 | g |
| 5 | Volume of hole, $V_h$ | 2802.86 | cm³ |
| 6 | Mass of moist soil, $M_s$ | 3850 | g |
| 7 | Bulk density of soil, $\gamma_b$ | 1.3736 | g/cm³ |
| 8 | Moisture content, $w$ | 13.636 | % |
| 9 | Dry density, $\gamma_d$ | 1.2088 | g/cm³ |
| 10 | Maximum dry density (lab), $\gamma_{d,max}$ | 1.6500 | g/cm³ |
| 11 | Relative compaction, $R$ | 73.26 | % |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Procedure Flowchart — Sand Replacement Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Collect sand sample, oven-dry and sieve through 600 micron and 150 micron] --> B[Step 2: Weigh empty calibrating container]
    B --> C[Step 3: Place sand pouring cylinder over container, open shutter, fill until sand stops flowing]
    C --> D[Step 4: Weigh container plus sand - determine Mass of sand in container]
    D --> E[Step 5: Compute calibrated bulk density of sand gamma_sand]
    E --> F[Step 6: Determine mass of sand in cone by placing cylinder on flat glass plate]
    F --> G[Step 7: Select test area, level ground, place base plate]
    G --> H[Step 8: Excavate test hole through base plate, collect all soil in container]
    H --> I[Step 9: Weigh and seal soil for moisture content sample]
    I --> J[Step 10: Place cylinder on base plate, open shutter, fill hole plus cone]
    J --> K[Step 11: Close shutter, weigh cylinder with remaining sand]
    K --> L[Step 12: Calculate mass of sand in hole, then volume of hole]
    L --> M[Step 13: Compute bulk density gamma_b, moisture content w, dry density gamma_d]
    M --> N[Step 14: Compare with lab MDD, compute relative compaction]
    N --> O[Step 15: Record in lab report, compare with specification]
```

## 4.2 Apparatus Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FieldAssembly[Field Test Assembly]
        spc[Sand Pouring Cylinder with Shutters] --> bp[Base Plate with Central Hole]
        bp --> th[Test Hole Excavated in Compacted Soil]
        th --> es[Excavated Soil Collected in Tray]
    end

    subgraph LabAssembly[Laboratory Determination]
        mwb[Moisture Content Container] --> oven[Oven at 105 to 110 C]
        es --> mwb
        es --> wb[Weighing Balance for Mass Measurement]
    end

    subgraph CalculationModule[Computational Stage]
        wb --> cal[Calibrated Sand Bulk Density]
        mwb --> cal
        cal --> out1[Bulk Density gamma_b]
        cal --> out2[Dry Density gamma_d]
        cal --> out3[Moisture Content w percent]
        cal --> out4[Relative Compaction R percent]
    end
```

## 4.3 Concept Map — Mass-Volume Equivalence Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    input1[Mass of Moist Soil Excavated M_s] --> bd[Bulk Density gamma_b]
    input2[Mass of Sand in Hole M_h] --> vh[Volume of Hole V_h]
    input3[Calibrated Sand Density gamma_sand] --> vh
    vh --> bd
    bd --> dd[Dry Density gamma_d]
    input4[Moisture Content w] --> dd
    dd --> rc[Relative Compaction R percent]
    input5[Lab MDD gamma_d_max] --> rc
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functional Mapping Justification:** The physical Sand-Pouring Cylinder, when assembled with the base plate, is a precision instrument engineered so that sand flows at a *constant* free-fall height into the test hole. The block diagram abstracts this assembly as a deterministic "displacement transducer" — the sand reliably maps the test hole's geometry. This is the conceptual equivalent of measuring volume by water displacement (Archimedes' principle) but adapted for unsaturated, in-situ soils.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer / Conceptual)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**
**State the principle of the Sand Replacement Method for determining field density. Mention the relevant IS code.**

**Model Answer:**
The Sand Replacement Method determines the in-place density of compacted soil by measuring (i) the **mass of soil** excavated from a small test hole and (ii) the **volume of the test hole**, the latter by filling it with a calibrated, free-flowing, dry sand of known bulk density. The principle is **mass–volume displacement** using a uniform granular material.
The relevant Indian Standard is **IS 2720 (Part XXVIII) – 1974 (Reaffirmed 2010)**, with the corresponding ASTM standard being **ASTM D1556**.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**
**Why is the sand used in the Sand Replacement Method specified to pass 600 µm and be retained on 150 µm sieves?**

**Model Answer:**
The sand is graded between **600 µm (passing)** and **150 µm (retained on)** IS sieves to ensure:
- **Uniform particle size**, which guarantees a constant, reproducible bulk density upon pouring.
- **Free-flowing nature** without bridging, allowing complete filling of the test hole.
- **Non-cohesiveness**, so the sand does not stick to the cylinder walls or the test hole surface.
- The narrow gradation minimizes segregation during pouring and prevents the sand from **penetrating the natural soil pores** of the surrounding compacted soil, which would otherwise distort the measured volume.

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Full Numerical / Descriptive with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, Part B carries 14 marks and offers an **internal choice**. The model questions below are designed in this format with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model Question]**

The following data were obtained during a Sand Replacement test conducted on a compacted subgrade:

**Calibration Data:**
- Mass of empty calibrating container = 0.450 kg
- Mass of container + sand = 2.200 kg
- Volume of calibrating container = 1500 cm³
- Mass of sand in cone (determined on flat glass plate) = 0.480 kg

**Field Data:**
- Mass of cylinder + sand (before pouring) = 9.500 kg
- Mass of cylinder + sand (after pouring into test hole) = 5.750 kg
- Mass of moist soil excavated from hole = 3.850 kg

**Moisture Content Data:**
- Mass of wet soil sample = 250 g
- Mass of oven-dry soil sample = 220 g
- Maximum dry density (from IS Light Compaction) = 1.650 g/cm³

**(a)** Calculate the **bulk density** and **dry density** of the soil in place.  
**(b)** Determine the **relative compaction** with respect to the laboratory MDD and comment on whether the field compaction meets typical highway subgrade requirements.

#### MODEL SOLUTION (a) — Bulk and Dry Density

**Step 1: Calibrated bulk density of sand**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{cs} &= 2.200 - 0.450 = 1.750\ \text{kg} = 1750\ \text{g} \\
\gamma_{sand} &= \frac{1750}{1500} = 1.1667\ \text{g/cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Mass of sand that filled the test hole**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_h &= M_1 - M_2 - M_3 \\
M_h &= 9.500 - 5.750 - 0.480 \\
M_h &= 3.270\ \text{kg} = 3270\ \text{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Volume of the test hole**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_h &= \frac{3270}{1.1667} = 2802.86\ \text{cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Bulk density**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_b &= \frac{3850}{2802.86} = 1.3736\ \text{g/cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Moisture content**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w &= \frac{250 - 220}{220} \times 100 = 13.636\ \% \quad \Rightarrow \quad w = 0.13636
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Dry density**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_d &= \frac{1.3736}{1 + 0.13636} = \frac{1.3736}{1.13636} = 1.2088\ \text{g/cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result (a):** $\gamma_b = 1.374\ \text{g/cm}^3$, $\gamma_d = 1.209\ \text{g/cm}^3$.

#### MODEL SOLUTION (b) — Relative Compaction and Comment

**Step 7: Relative compaction**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{\gamma_{d,field}}{\gamma_{d,max}} \times 100 \\
R &= \frac{1.2088}{1.650} \times 100 = 73.26\ \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:** As per **MoRTH / IRC:SP:58** specifications, a highway subgrade must achieve a minimum relative compaction of **95% of MDD** (IS Light Compaction). Since the obtained value (73.26%) is significantly below 95%, the compaction is **unsatisfactory** and the layer must be re-rolled with additional passes before acceptance.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative — **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Retest]**

**(a)** Describe the **apparatus and procedure** for the Sand Replacement Method as per IS 2720 (Part XXVIII). Draw a neat labeled sketch of the sand-pouring cylinder.  
**(b)** The following observations were recorded in a Sand Replacement test:

| Observation | Value |
|-------------|-------|
| Mass of cylinder + sand (initial) | 11.250 kg |
| Mass of cylinder + sand (after pouring) | 6.800 kg |
| Mass of sand in cone | 0.500 kg |
| Mass of moist soil from hole | 4.125 kg |
| Calibrated bulk density of sand | 1.520 g/cm³ |
| Moisture content of soil | 12.5% |
| MDD (IS Heavy Compaction) | 1.880 g/cm³ |

Calculate the **dry density** and **relative compaction** of the field soil. Comment on the suitability for use as a **dam core material** (typical requirement: ≥ 100% of Standard Proctor MDD for heavily compacted cores).

#### MODEL SOLUTION (a) — Apparatus and Procedure

**Apparatus** (with reference to IS 2720 Part XXVIII):
1. **Sand-pouring cylinder** consisting of a cylindrical body fitted with a shutter valve at the base and a detachable conical funnel. Common capacities: **3 L** (small) and **15 L** (large).
2. **Calibrating container** of known internal volume (typically 1500 cm³ for a 3 L cylinder).
3. **Uniform, clean, dry sand** passing 600 µm IS sieve and retained on 150 µm IS sieve.
4. **Base plate** (metal tray) with a central circular hole.
5. **Excavation tools**: small hand pick, hammer, brush, spoon.
6. **Moisture containers** (air-tight).
7. **Weighing balance** (1 g accuracy for sand; 0.1 g for moisture).
8. **Oven** (105°C – 110°C).

**Procedure (in numbered steps):**
1. **Calibration of the sand:** Weigh the empty calibrating container. Place the sand-pouring cylinder centrally over the container with the shutter closed. Open the shutter and allow sand to flow freely until no further sand enters the container. Close the shutter, weigh the container with sand, and compute $\gamma_{sand} = M_{cs}/V_c$.
2. **Cone calibration:** Place the cylinder on a flat glass plate, open the shutter, close after the cone is filled, and weigh the cylinder again to determine the mass of sand in the cone ($M_3$).
3. **Site preparation:** Clean and level the test area; place the base plate firmly.
4. **Excavation:** Dig a circular hole through the base plate hole (typically 100 mm dia × 150 mm deep). Collect **all** excavated soil in a sealed container; record the total mass $M_s$.
5. **Take a representative moisture sample** (at least 100 g) in a moisture container.
6. **Place the cylinder centrally** over the test hole; open the shutter and allow sand to fill the hole and the cone depression. Close the shutter when flow stops.
7. **Weigh the cylinder** with the remaining sand ($M_2$).
8. **Lab determination** of moisture content by oven-drying at 105°C – 110°C for 24 hours.
9. **Compute** $M_h$, $V_h$, $\gamma_b$, $w$, $\gamma_d$, and $R$ as shown in Question A.

**Sketch (textual representation of the sand-pouring cylinder):**

```
        +-------------------+
        |   CYLINDER BODY   |   ← Sand-pouring cylinder
        |   (with sand)     |
        +-------------------+
        |  SHUTTER / VALVE  |   ← Sluice valve (open/close)
        +-------------------+
        \                 /
         \   CONICAL     /     ← Conical funnel
          \  FUNNEL     /
           \          /
            +--------+
            | HOLE   |          ← Test hole in soil
            |___ ___|         
         (Compacted soil layer)
```

#### MODEL SOLUTION (b) — Dry Density and Relative Compaction

**Step 1: Mass of sand filling the test hole**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_h &= M_1 - M_2 - M_3 \\
M_h &= 11.250 - 6.800 - 0.500 \\
M_h &= 3.950\ \text{kg} = 3950\ \text{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Volume of test hole**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_h &= \frac{3950}{1.520} = 2598.68\ \text{cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Bulk density of soil**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_b &= \frac{4125}{2598.68} = 1.5874\ \text{g/cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Dry density (with $w = 12.5\% = 0.125$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_d &= \frac{1.5874}{1 + 0.125} = \frac{1.5874}{1.125} = 1.4110\ \text{g/cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Relative compaction with respect to MDD (Heavy Compaction)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{1.4110}{1.880} \times 100 = 75.05\ \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:** A relative compaction of **75.05%** is well below the typical dam core requirement of **≥ 95–100% of Standard Proctor MDD** (or **≥ 90–95% of Modified Proctor MDD**). The material is **NOT suitable** for direct use as a dam core in its current state. The layer must be reworked, moisture-conditioned, and re-compacted with additional roller passes (typically sheep-foot roller for clayey cores), followed by retesting.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Sand Replacement Method Problems:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract $M_3$ (cone mass):** Many students compute $M_h = M_1 - M_2$ only, which inflates $V_h$ and *underestimates* $\gamma_b$ and $\gamma_d$. This typically costs **3–4 marks**.
> 
> 2. **Mixing up units:** Using $M_h$ in kg and $\gamma_{sand}$ in g/cm³ without conversion. Always convert to **consistent units** (preferably grams and cm³).
> 
> 3. **Moisture content as percentage vs. decimal in $\gamma_d$ formula:** Students often write $1 + 13.636 = 14.636$ instead of $1 + 0.13636 = 1.13636$. This is the **single most common calculation error** and deducts **2–3 marks**.
> 
> 4. **Omitting the calibration step:** A complete answer must include calibration calculations. Skipping this step costs **2 marks**.
> 
> 5. **No mention of IS code:** Examiners expect **"IS 2720 (Part XXVIII)"** in the answer. Omitting it costs **1 mark**.
> 
> 6. **Not interpreting the result:** Always conclude with a **comment** on whether the field compaction meets specifications (95% MDD for subgrade, etc.). Ending the calculation without a qualitative comment costs **1 mark**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Principle:** Displacement of soil volume by calibrated, free-flowing sand.
- [ ] **IS Code:** IS 2720 (Part XXVIII) – 1974 / ASTM D1556.
- [ ] **Sand gradation:** Passing 600 µm, Retained on 150 µm — uniform, clean, dry.
- [ ] **Calibration first** — always determine $\gamma_{sand}$ *and* $M_3$ (cone mass) **before** the field test.
- [ ] **Three key mass readings:** $M_1$ (initial cylinder + sand), $M_2$ (final cylinder + sand), $M_3$ (cone).
- [ ] **Mass of sand in hole:** $M_h = M_1 - M_2 - M_3$ (note the **subtraction of cone mass**).
- [ ] **Volume of hole:** $V_h = M_h / \gamma_{sand}$.
- [ ] **Bulk density:** $\gamma_b = M_s / V_h$.
- [ ] **Moisture content:** $w = (M_w - M_d)/M_d \times 100$ (in %).
- [ ] **Dry density:** $\gamma_d = \gamma_b / (1 + w)$ — use $w$ as a **decimal** (e.g., 0.15, not 15).
- [ ] **Relative compaction:** $R = \gamma_{d,field} / \gamma_{d,max} \times 100$.
- [ ] **Typical specification:** ≥ 95% of MDD for subgrade; ≥ 98% for road base; ≥ 100% Standard Proctor for dam cores.
- [ ] **Limitations of method:** Not suitable for soils with large particles (use **Core Cutter Method** for soft, fine-grained soils; **Nuclear Density Gauge** for rapid, large-area testing).
- [ ] **Always conclude** with a comment on acceptance or rejection per the relevant IS/MoRTH specification.
- [ ] **Lab report must include:** Aim, apparatus, theory, procedure, observations (calibration + field + moisture), calculation table, result, and conclusion.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Particle Size Distribution by Sieve Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL408 - SOIL SCIENCE & MECHANICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Particle Size Distribution by Sieve Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module Experiment 2: Particle Size Distribution by Sieve Analysis

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Particle Size Distribution (PSD) by Sieve Analysis** is a fundamental laboratory test in geotechnical engineering conducted to determine the quantitative distribution of grain sizes in coarse-grained soils (particles larger than **75 µm / 0.075 mm**). The test is officially standardized under **IS 2720 (Part 4) – 1985** (Reaffirmed 2020) and forms the primary basis for the **IS Soil Classification System (IS 1498 – 1970)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Sieve Analysis applies **exclusively to cohesionless / coarse-grained soils** (gravels and sands). For fine-grained soils (silt & clay, particles < 75 µm), **Sedimentation Analysis using a Hydrometer** (IS 2720 Part 4 / Part 8) is the mandated companion test.

The test results are expressed as a **Semi-Logarithmic Grain Size Distribution Curve**, where particle size is plotted on the **logarithmic X-axis** and the **percentage finer (N)** is plotted on the **arithmetic Y-axis**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Coin Sorter" Intuition

Imagine you have a jar full of mixed Indian currency coins (Re. 1, ₹2, ₹5, ₹10) and you want to count how many of each type are inside.

You stack circular trays with **progressively smaller holes** — like ₹10 won't fit through a Re. 1 hole, but a Re. 1 will fall through everything. You shake the stack:
- The **biggest coins (₹10)** stay on top,
- The **smallest (Re. 1)** drop to the bottom pan,
- Each tray holds only one size-class.

**Sieve analysis works identically for soil grains.** The mechanical sieve shaker is the "shaking hand", the sieves are the trays with calibrated square openings, and the retained soil on each sieve gives the mass of particles within that size bracket.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality:** No two natural soil grains are perfectly identical. Sieve analysis sorts a continuous population into discrete size-bands, enabling engineers to predict **permeability, shear strength, compressibility, frost susceptibility, and filter design compatibility**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standards Referenced

| Parameter | Standard Value / Code |
| :--- | :--- |
| Governing Indian Standard | **IS 2720 (Part 4) – 1985** |
| Classification Reference | **IS 1498 – 1970** |
| Lower size limit of sieves | **75 µm (0.075 mm)** |
| Typical sample mass | **500 g** (for sand), **2–5 kg** (for gravel) |
| Sieving duration | **10–15 minutes** minimum mechanical shaking |
| Unit of particle size | **mm** (or µm) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — The Underlying Physics

Sieve analysis exploits a **size-exclusion principle**: a particle is "retained" on a sieve if its **shortest dimension** is greater than the sieve aperture. The driving force is **gravity-assisted vibration**, which provides the kinetic energy needed to orient irregular grains so they can pass through openings of matching size.

The complete procedure can be broken down into **six structured steps**:

1. **Sample Preparation (IS 2720 Part 4, Cl. 4.2):**
   - Oven-dry the soil sample at **105 °C – 110 °C** for **24 hours**.
   - Cool in a **desiccator** to prevent moisture re-absorption.
   - Weigh the dry sample accurately to **0.1 g** precision.

2. **Sieve Selection & Nesting:**
   - Assemble sieves in **descending order of aperture** (largest at top, pan at bottom).
   - Standard test stack (per IS 1498):
     **4.75 mm → 2.0 mm → 600 µm → 425 µm → 212 µm → 75 µm → Pan**

3. **Mechanical Sieving:**
   - Place the soil on the top sieve and secure the lid.
   - Mount on a **sieve shaker** for a minimum of **10 minutes**.
   - Hand-shaking is permitted but must continue until **< 1 %** of mass passes any sieve in 1 minute of additional shaking.

4. **Weighing Retained Mass:**
   - Weigh each sieve + retained soil.
   - Subtract sieve weight → Mass of soil retained on each sieve.
   - **Sanity Check:** Σ(Mass retained) must equal original sample mass within **± 2 %**.

5. **Tabulation & Calculation** (see §2.2 below).

6. **Plotting & Interpretation:**
   - Plot grain size (log scale, X-axis) vs % finer (arithmetic, Y-axis).
   - Read off **D₁₀, D₃₀, D₆₀** to compute gradation indices.

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Percentage Retained** $(\%R_i)$ | $\%R_i = \dfrac{M_i}{M_{total}} \times 100$ | % | $M_i$ = mass retained on $i^{th}$ sieve |
| 2 | **Cumulative Percentage Retained** $(\Sigma \%R_i)$ | $\Sigma \%R_i = \sum_{j=1}^{i} \%R_j$ | % | Running sum from top sieve |
| 3 | **Percentage Finer (N)** | $N = 100 - \Sigma \%R_i$ | % | Material passing the sieve |
| 4 | **Coefficient of Uniformity** $(C_u)$ | $C_u = \dfrac{D_{60}}{D_{10}}$ | dimensionless | Higher $C_u$ → broader range of sizes |
| 5 | **Coefficient of Curvature** $(C_c)$ | $C_c = \dfrac{(D_{30})^2}{D_{60} \times D_{10}}$ | dimensionless | Indicates shape of gradation curve |
| 6 | **Mass Loss Tolerance** | $\dfrac{\vert M_{total} - \Sigma M_i \vert}{M_{total}} \times 100 \le 2\%$ | % | Test invalid if exceeded |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pipe-Escaping Tip:** When writing $\vert x \vert$ (absolute value) in exam scripts, **always use brackets or `mod` notation**. In markdown tables here, use the format $\lvert x \rvert$ to avoid breaking table syntax.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility — Where This Data is Used

| Application | Specific Use of PSD Curve |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Foundation Design** | Estimating bearing capacity of granular soils |
| **Earth Dam / Embankment Design** | Selecting filter material (Terzaghi's filter criteria) |
| **Pavement Subgrade** | Subgrade soil classification (HRB / AASHTO) |
| **Concrete Aggregates** | Confirming compliance with IS 383 grading envelopes |
| **Drainage Design** | Predicting permeability (Hazen's formula uses $D_{10}$) |
| **Soil Classification** | IS classification symbol assignment (SW, SP, GW, GP) |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Example

## 3.1 Exhaustive Numerical Demonstration

### Given Data (Typical KTU Lab Sample)

A dried soil sample weighing **W = 500 g** was subjected to sieve analysis. The following masses were retained:

| Sieve Size | Mass Retained $M_i$ (g) |
| :--- | :--- |
| 4.75 mm | 50 |
| 2.00 mm | 100 |
| 600 µm  | 150 |
| 425 µm  | 80  |
| 212 µm  | 75  |
| 75 µm   | 35  |
| Pan     | 10  |
| **Total** | **500** |

### Step 1 — Verification of Mass Loss

$$
\text{Mass Loss} = \frac{\lvert 500 - 500 \rvert}{500} \times 100 = 0 \%
$$

Since $0\% \le 2\%$, the test data is **valid**.

### Step 2 — Compute Percentage Retained ($\%R_i$)

$$
\%R_i = \frac{M_i}{W} \times 100
$$

For the **4.75 mm sieve**:
$$
\%R_{4.75} = \frac{50}{500} \times 100 = 10.00 \%
$$

For the **2.00 mm sieve**:
$$
\%R_{2.00} = \frac{100}{500} \times 100 = 20.00 \%
$$

For the **600 µm sieve**:
$$
\%R_{0.600} = \frac{150}{500} \times 100 = 30.00 \%
$$

For the **425 µm sieve**:
$$
\%R_{0.425} = \frac{80}{500} \times 100 = 16.00 \%
$$

For the **212 µm sieve**:
$$
\%R_{0.212} = \frac{75}{500} \times 100 = 15.00 \%
$$

For the **75 µm sieve**:
$$
\%R_{0.075} = \frac{35}{500} \times 100 = 7.00 \%
$$

For the **Pan**:
$$
\%R_{pan} = \frac{10}{500} \times 100 = 2.00 \%
$$

### Step 3 — Cumulative Percentage Retained ($\Sigma \%R_i$)

$$
\Sigma \%R_4.75 = 10.00 \%
$$

$$
\Sigma \%R_2.00 = 10.00 + 20.00 = 30.00 \%
$$

$$
\Sigma \%R_0.600 = 30.00 + 30.00 = 60.00 \%
$$

$$
\Sigma \%R_0.425 = 60.00 + 16.00 = 76.00 \%
$$

$$
\Sigma \%R_0.212 = 76.00 + 15.00 = 91.00 \%
$$

$$
\Sigma \%R_0.075 = 91.00 + 7.00 = 98.00 \%
$$

$$
\Sigma \%R_{pan} = 98.00 + 2.00 = 100.00 \%
$$

### Step 4 — Percentage Finer (N)

$$
N_i = 100 - \Sigma \%R_i
$$

| Sieve Size | $\%R_i$ | $\Sigma \%R_i$ | **% Finer (N)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 4.75 mm | 10.00 | 10.00 | **90.00** |
| 2.00 mm | 20.00 | 30.00 | **70.00** |
| 600 µm  | 30.00 | 60.00 | **40.00** |
| 425 µm  | 16.00 | 76.00 | **24.00** |
| 212 µm  | 15.00 | 91.00 | **9.00**  |
| 75 µm   | 7.00  | 98.00 | **2.00**  |
| Pan     | 2.00  | 100.00 | **0.00**  |

### Step 5 — Reading Particle Diameters from the Grain Size Curve

From the semi-log plot, we read:
$$
D_{10} = 0.18 \text{ mm} \quad (\text{size at } N = 10\%)
$$

$$
D_{30} = 0.55 \text{ mm} \quad (\text{size at } N = 30\%)
$$

$$
D_{60} = 1.65 \text{ mm} \quad (\text{size at } N = 60\%)
$$

### Step 6 — Coefficient of Uniformity $(C_u)$

$$
C_u = \frac{D_{60}}{D_{10}} = \frac{1.65}{0.18} = 9.17
$$

### Step 7 — Coefficient of Curvature $(C_c)$

$$
C_c = \frac{(D_{30})^2}{D_{60} \times D_{10}}
$$

$$
C_c = \frac{(0.55)^2}{1.65 \times 0.18} = \frac{0.3025}{0.2970} = 1.018
$$

### Step 8 — Soil Classification (IS 1498)

Check gradation criteria for a **Sand (S)** since more than 50% is retained on 75 µm:
- More than 50% of coarse fraction passes 4.75 mm → **Sand**.

For **Well-Graded Sand (SW)**:
$$
C_u \ge 6 \quad \text{AND} \quad 1 \le C_c \le 3
$$

We have $C_u = 9.17 \ge 6$ ✓ and $C_c = 1.018$ (between 1 and 3) ✓.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The soil is classified as **SW — Well-Graded Sand**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sieve Nesting Arrangement (Vertical Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["LID - Prevents Sample Loss During Shaking"] --> B["SIEVE 4.75 mm - Topmost Largest Aperture"]
    B --> C["SIEVE 2.00 mm - Coarse Sand Limit"]
    C --> D["SIEVE 600 microns - Medium Sand Range"]
    D --> E["SIEVE 425 microns - Standard Sieve per IS"]
    E --> F["SIEVE 212 microns - Fine Sand Range"]
    F --> G["SIEVE 75 microns - Lower Limit of Sieve Analysis"]
    G --> H["PAN - Collects All Particles Below 75 microns"]

    I["OVEN DRIED SOIL SAMPLE 500 g"] --> B

    J["SIEVE SHAKER - 10 to 15 Minutes Mechanical Vibration"] -.-> A
    J -.-> H

    style A fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```
