<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compaction** is the process of mechanically densifying a soil mass by reducing the volume of voids through the expulsion of air, without significantly changing the water content. It is a **rapid, short-term process** achieved by applying mechanical energy (static, impact, vibratory, or kneading), and is fundamentally different from **consolidation** which is a long-term process driven by the dissipation of pore water pressure.

The **Compaction Test** (also called the **Proctor Compaction Test**) is a standard laboratory experiment conducted to determine the **Maximum Dry Density (MDD)** and the corresponding **Optimum Moisture Content (OMC)** of a given soil, as per **IS 2720 (Part VII) – 1980** (Light Compaction) and **IS 2720 (Part VIII) – 1983** (Heavy / Modified Compaction).

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Maximum Dry Density (MDD / $\gamma_{d,max}$)** | The highest dry unit weight of the soil achieved at a given compactive effort. |
| **Optimum Moisture Content (OMC / $w_{opt}$)** | The water content at which the dry density reaches its maximum value for a given compactive effort. |

> [!NOTE]
> The compaction test quantifies the **"sweet spot"** — the precise amount of water and mechanical energy required to densify a soil to its theoretical maximum, mimicking the field conditions achieved by rollers, rammers, and vibrators.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview

Think of compaction like **packing a suitcase for a trip**:

- If your clothes (soil particles) are **completely dry**, they have lots of air gaps — you cannot squash them down well.
- If your clothes are **completely soaked**, water takes up space that the particles could fill — also inefficient.
- At the **right amount of dampness** (OMC), the clothes slide and settle into a tight, dense arrangement with minimal air — just like a perfectly packed suitcase.

**Another real-world analogy — building a sandcastle:**
- **Dry sand** crumbles because particles slide past each other with no cohesion; air fills the gaps.
- **Overly wet sand** flows like a liquid; water displaces the particles.
- **Moist sand** holds its shape beautifully — the surface tension of water films creates temporary bonds, and the grains pack tightly together. This is the **OMC** in action.

> [!TIP]
> **Why do road engineers care?** A properly compacted subgrade (the native soil beneath a road) prevents settlement, increases bearing capacity, and reduces water infiltration. An under-compacted road will rut, crack, and fail prematurely. OMC and MDD are the two non-negotiable design parameters handed to every site engineer.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following specifications are standardized by the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** and must be memorized for KTU examinations:

| Specification | IS Light Compaction (Standard Proctor) | IS Heavy Compaction (Modified Proctor) |
|---------------|----------------------------------------|------------------------------------------|
| **Standard Reference** | IS 2720 (Part VII) – 1980 | IS 2720 (Part VIII) – 1983 |
| **Mold Volume** | **1000 cm³** | **1000 cm³** |
| **Mold Diameter** | 100 mm | 150 mm (or 100 mm) |
| **Rammer Mass** | **2.5 kg** | **4.9 kg** |
| **Drop Height** | **310 mm** | **450 mm** |
| **Number of Layers** | **3** | **5** |
| **Blows per Layer** | **25** | **25** |
| **Compactive Effort** | ≈ **595 kJ/m³** | ≈ **2700 kJ/m³** |
| **Unit Weight of Water ($\gamma_w$)** | **9.81 kN/m³** (or **1 g/cm³**) | Same |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Compaction

Soil in its natural state is loose and porous. In civil engineering applications such as **embankments, road subgrades, railway formations, earthen dams, and backfills behind retaining walls**, the soil must be densified to:

1. **Increase shear strength** (resistance to failure).
2. **Reduce compressibility** (minimize future settlement).
3. **Decrease permeability** (reduce water seepage).
4. **Improve stability** under loads and environmental cycles.
5. **Reduce frost susceptibility** in cold climates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The compaction test does NOT remove water from soil — it removes **air** from the voids. This is a key conceptual distinction often tested in KTU viva voce.

## 2.2 Theoretical Concept: The Compaction Curve

When dry density ($\gamma_d$) is plotted against water content ($w$) for several compactive efforts, a characteristic bell-shaped curve is obtained:

- **Dry side of OMC**: As water increases, water acts as a **lubricant**, allowing particles to slide into denser configurations. Dry density increases sharply.
- **At OMC**: The void space is optimally filled with water + soil grains, achieving peak dry density. Air voids are at their practical minimum.
- **Wet side of OMC**: Excess water begins to **displace** soil grains, occupying volume that could otherwise hold solids. Dry density decreases.

## 2.3 Zero Air Voids (ZAV) Line

The **ZAV line** (also called the **saturation line**) represents the theoretical relationship between dry density and water content when **zero air voids** exist — i.e., the soil is 100% saturated. It is the **upper boundary** of all possible compaction curves for a given specific gravity.

### 2.4 Factors Affecting Compaction (High-Yield Theory Points)

1. **Water Content** — Most critical factor; defines the OMC.
2. **Compactive Effort** — Higher energy shifts the curve **upward and to the left** (higher MDD at lower OMC).
3. **Soil Type** — Cohesive soils (clays) need more water; granular soils compact easily with less.
4. **Method of Compaction** — Impact (Proctor), kneading, vibratory, or static.
5. **Gradation of Particles** — Well-graded soils compact to higher densities than poorly graded ones.
6. **Admixtures / Stabilizers** — Lime, cement, or fly ash can alter the OMC and MDD.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below contains **every formula** you will need to solve any numerical problem on the Compaction Test in KTU examinations. Memorize these.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | **Bulk (Wet) Density** | $\rho = \dfrac{m_{wet}}{V}$ | g/cm³ or kg/m³ | $m_{wet}$ = mass of wet soil compacted in mold; $V$ = mold volume (1000 cm³) |
| 2 | **Dry Density** | $\boxed{\rho_d = \dfrac{\rho}{1 + w}}$ | g/cm³ | $w$ is in **decimal** form (e.g., 14% = 0.14) |
| 3 | **Zero Air Voids Dry Density** | $\boxed{\rho_{d,ZAV} = \dfrac{G \cdot \rho_w}{1 + w \cdot G}}$ | g/cm³ | $G$ = specific gravity of soil solids; $\rho_w$ = 1 g/cm³ |
| 4 | **Dry Unit Weight** | $\gamma_d = \rho_d \cdot g$ | kN/m³ | $g$ = 9.81 m/s²; or use $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³ |
| 5 | **Dry Density from $\gamma_d$** | $\rho_d = \dfrac{\gamma_d}{\gamma_w}$ | g/cm³ | Useful when unit weight is given in kN/m³ |
| 6 | **Degree of Saturation at OMC** | $S = \dfrac{w \cdot G}{e}$ | % or fraction | $e$ from $\rho_d = \dfrac{G \cdot \rho_w}{1+e}$ |
| 7 | **Compactive Effort (Energy)** | $E = \dfrac{n_b \cdot n_L \cdot W \cdot h}{V}$ | kJ/m³ | $n_b$ = blows/layer, $n_L$ = layers, $W$ = rammer weight, $h$ = drop, $V$ = mold volume |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Highway Engineering**: Determines rolling patterns, roller types, and field water content to be added (or aerated) before placement.
- **Earthen Dam Construction**: Ensures low permeability and high shear strength of the core and shell.
- **Landfill Liners**: Compaction of clay liners minimizes leachate migration.
- **Railway Ballast Bed Subgrade**: Provides stable track geometry under repeated train loading.
- **Foundation Beds**: Reduces post-construction settlement of buildings and industrial structures.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Dry Density from Bulk Density and Water Content

**Starting Point — Phase Diagram of Soil Mass:**

Let:
- $M$ = total (wet) mass of soil
- $M_w$ = mass of water
- $M_s$ = mass of soil solids
- $V$ = total volume (mold volume)
- $V_a$ = volume of air
- $V_w$ = volume of water
- $V_s$ = volume of solids

**Step 1: Define Water Content ($w$)**

By definition, water content is the ratio of water mass to solid mass:

$$w = \frac{M_w}{M_s}$$

Therefore, the mass of water is:

$$M_w = w \cdot M_s$$

**Step 2: Express Total Mass**

The total wet mass equals solids plus water:

$$M = M_s + M_w = M_s + w \cdot M_s = M_s \cdot (1 + w)$$

**Step 3: Express Bulk Density**

Bulk density is total mass divided by total volume:

$$\rho = \frac{M}{V} = \frac{M_s \cdot (1 + w)}{V}$$

**Step 4: Define Dry Density**

Dry density is the mass of solids per unit total volume:

$$\rho_d = \frac{M_s}{V}$$

**Step 5: Derive the Master Equation**

Dividing the bulk density equation by $(1 + w)$:

$$\rho_d = \frac{\rho}{1 + w}$$

This is the **fundamental compaction formula** used in every calculation.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Zero Air Voids Line

**Step 1: Phase Relations at Saturation**

When air voids are zero, the degree of saturation $S = 1$ (i.e., 100%). From the basic phase relation:

$$S \cdot e = w \cdot G$$

With $S = 1$:

$$e = w \cdot G$$

**Step 2: Express Dry Density in Terms of Void Ratio**

The standard phase relation:

$$\rho_d = \frac{G \cdot \rho_w}{1 + e}$$

**Step 3: Substitute the Saturated Void Ratio**

$$\rho_{d,\text{ZAV}} = \frac{G \cdot \rho_w}{1 + w \cdot G}$$

This is the **ZAV equation**. It defines the upper bound of achievable dry density for a soil of specific gravity $G$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The ZAV line has a **hyperbolic shape** and passes through no real compaction curve (since compaction cannot expel all air). However, the ZAV line is a vital reference for checking the validity of laboratory data.

## 3.3 Solved Numerical Problem (Worked Example)

### **Problem Statement**

A Standard Proctor Compaction Test (IS Light) was conducted on a soil sample. The mass of the empty mold is **2.750 kg** and its volume is **1000 cm³**. The following observations were recorded:

| Test No. | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|----------|---|---|---|---|---|
| Water content $w$ (%) | 6 | 10 | 14 | 18 | 22 |
| Mass of mold + wet soil (kg) | 4.020 | 4.250 | 4.400 | 4.360 | 4.180 |

**Required:**
1. Calculate the bulk density and dry density for each test.
2. Plot the **compaction curve** and determine **OMC** and **MDD**.
3. Compute the **Zero Air Voids (ZAV) dry density** at OMC, given the specific gravity $G = 2.70$.

---

### **Solution — Step 1: Mass of Wet Soil**

The mass of wet soil in each test is obtained by subtracting the mass of the empty mold:

$$m_{wet} = m_{mold+wet\,soil} - m_{mold}$$

For Test 1:

$$m_{wet,1} = 4.020 - 2.750 = 1.270 \text{ kg} = 1270 \text{ g}$$

Applying the same logic for all tests:

| Test | $m_{wet}$ (g) |
|------|---------------|
| 1 | 1270 |
| 2 | 1500 |
| 3 | 1650 |
| 4 | 1610 |
| 5 | 1430 |

---

### **Solution — Step 2: Bulk (Wet) Density**

$$\rho = \frac{m_{wet}}{V}, \quad V = 1000 \text{ cm}^3$$

For Test 1:

$$\rho_1 = \frac{1270}{1000} = 1.270 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

Similarly for all tests:

| Test | $\rho$ (g/cm³) |
|------|----------------|
| 1 | 1.270 |
| 2 | 1.500 |
| 3 | 1.650 |
| 4 | 1.610 |
| 5 | 1.430 |

---

### **Solution — Step 3: Dry Density**

$$\rho_d = \frac{\rho}{1 + w}$$

For Test 1 ($w = 6\% = 0.06$):

$$\rho_{d,1} = \frac{1.270}{1 + 0.06} = \frac{1.270}{1.06} = 1.198 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

For Test 2 ($w = 10\% = 0.10$):

$$\rho_{d,2} = \frac{1.500}{1 + 0.10} = \frac{1.500}{1.10} = 1.364 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

For Test 3 ($w = 14\% = 0.14$):

$$\rho_{d,3} = \frac{1.650}{1 + 0.14} = \frac{1.650}{1.14} = 1.447 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

For Test 4 ($w = 18\% = 0.18$):

$$\rho_{d,4} = \frac{1.610}{1 + 0.18} = \frac{1.610}{1.18} = 1.364 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

For Test 5 ($w = 22\% = 0.22$):

$$\rho_{d,5} = \frac{1.430}{1 + 0.22} = \frac{1.430}{1.22} = 1.172 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

| Test | $w$ (%) | $\rho$ (g/cm³) | $\rho_d$ (g/cm³) |
|------|---------|----------------|-------------------|
| 1 | 6 | 1.270 | 1.198 |
| 2 | 10 | 1.500 | 1.364 |
| 3 | **14** | 1.650 | **1.447** |
| 4 | 18 | 1.610 | 1.364 |
| 5 | 22 | 1.430 | 1.172 |

---

### **Solution — Step 4: Identify OMC and MDD from the Compaction Curve**

From the tabulated values, the **maximum dry density** is at **Test 3**:

$$\boxed{\text{MDD} = 1.447 \text{ g/cm}^3 \quad ; \quad \text{OMC} = 14\%}$$

A smooth bell-shaped curve is obtained when $\rho_d$ is plotted against $w$. The peak of this curve gives the OMC and MDD.

---

### **Solution — Step 5: Zero Air Voids Dry Density at OMC**

$$\rho_{d,\text{ZAV}} = \frac{G \cdot \rho_w}{1 + w \cdot G}$$

Substituting $G = 2.70$, $\rho_w = 1.0$ g/cm³, $w = 0.14$:

$$\rho_{d,\text{ZAV}} = \frac{2.70 \times 1.0}{1 + (0.14)(2.70)} = \frac{2.70}{1 + 0.378} = \frac{2.70}{1.378}$$

$$\boxed{\rho_{d,\text{ZAV}} = 1.959 \text{ g/cm}^3}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of Validity**: The MDD obtained (1.447 g/cm³) is **less than** the ZAV dry density (1.959 g/cm³) at the OMC. This confirms the laboratory result is **physically valid** — no compaction curve can ever cross above the ZAV line.

### **Solution — Step 6: Degree of Saturation at OMC**

First, find the void ratio at OMC from the dry density relation:

$$\rho_d = \frac{G \cdot \rho_w}{1 + e} \implies e = \frac{G \cdot \rho_w}{\rho_d} - 1$$

$$e = \frac{2.70 \times 1.0}{1.447} - 1 = 1.866 - 1 = 0.866$$

Now compute the degree of saturation:

$$S = \frac{w \cdot G}{e} = \frac{0.14 \times 2.70}{0.866} = \frac{0.378}{0.866} = 0.4365$$

$$\boxed{S \approx 43.65\%}$$

> This means at OMC, only ~44% of the voids are filled with water; the remaining 56% still contain air — confirming that compaction cannot expel all air.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Apparatus Block Architecture (Block-Level Schematic)

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below provides a **block-level functional architecture** of the compaction test apparatus, illustrating how each component interacts during the experiment. This serves as a substitute for a physical photograph in the lab manual context.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Air-Dried Soil Sample]) --> sieveA[sieve19A: Sieve 19 mm or 4.75 mm]
    sieveA --> sampleA[sampleA: 5 Representative Portions of ~2.5 kg Each]
    sampleA --> mixA[mixA: Mix with Varying Water Content 6% to 24%]
    mixA --> cureA[cureA: Curing Period 5 to 15 minutes]

    cureA --> layerA[layerA: Fill Proctor Mold in 3 Layers for Light Test]
    layerA --> ramA[ramA: Compact Each Layer with 25 Blows of 2.5 kg Rammer]
    ramA --> trimA[trimA: Trim Top Surface with Straight Edge]
    trimA --> weighA[weighA: Weigh Mold with Compacted Soil]

    weighA --> ovenA[ovenA: Take Moisture Sample from Each Layer]
    ovenA --> dryA[dryA: Oven-Dry at 105 to 110 deg C for 24 Hours]
    dryA --> calcA[calcA: Calculate Bulk Density and Dry Density]
    calcA --> plotA[plotA: Plot Dry Density vs Water Content]
    plotA --> resultA([Result: OMC and MDD from Compaction Curve])

    weighA --> sampleB[sampleB: Retain Soil for Water Content Determination]
    sampleB --> ovenA
```

## 4.2 Compaction Curve Architecture (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    axisX[axisX: Water Content w percent increasing from left to right]
    axisY[axisY: Dry Density rho_d g per cc increasing from bottom to top]

    axisX --> dryZone[dryZone: Dry Side of OMC w less than OMC - water lubricates particles - density rises sharply]
    axisY --> dryZone

    dryZone --> peakNode[peakNode: PEAK MDD at OMC - maximum densification - minimum air voids]

    peakNode --> wetZone[wetZone: Wet Side of OMC w greater than OMC - excess water displaces grains - density falls]

    peakNode --> zavNode[zavNode: ZAV Line - theoretical saturation curve - upper bound - hyperbolic shape]
    zavNode -.->|always above| peakNode
```

## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram: Light vs Heavy Compaction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB([Select Compactive Effort]) --> lightB[lightB: IS Light Compaction - IS 2720 Part VII]
    startB --> heavyB[heavyB: IS Heavy Compaction - IS 2720 Part VIII]

    lightB --> specA[specA: Rammer 2.5 kg - Drop 310 mm - 3 Layers - 25 Blows per Layer]
    heavyB --> specB[specB: Rammer 4.9 kg - Drop 450 mm - 5 Layers - 25 Blows per Layer]

    specA --> energyA[energyA: Energy 595 kJ per cubic meter]
    specB --> energyB[energyB: Energy 2700 kJ per cubic meter]

    energyA --> curveA[curveA: Lower OMC around 12 to 16 percent - Lower MDD around 1.6 to 1.7]
    energyB --> curveB[curveB: Higher MDD around 1.8 to 2.0 - Lower OMC around 8 to 12 percent]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visual Cue for the Examiner**: When asked to "show the effect of compactive effort", draw **two compaction curves on the same axes** — the heavier effort curve sits **up and to the left** of the lighter one. This single sketch is worth 2–3 marks in KTU viva voce.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define Optimum Moisture Content (OMC) and Maximum Dry Density (MDD). Why does the dry density decrease on the wet side of OMC?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Optimum Moisture Content (OMC)** is the specific water content at which a soil attains its **Maximum Dry Density (MDD)** under a given compactive effort. **Maximum Dry Density (MDD)** is the highest value of dry unit weight achievable in the laboratory for that compactive effort.

> On the **wet side of OMC**, any additional water added to the soil **occupies space that could otherwise have been filled by soil grains**. Since water has a lower density (1 g/cm³) compared to soil solids (~2.65–2.75 g/cm³), the average mass per unit volume decreases. Furthermore, the excess water creates **pore water pressure** that resists the compactive effort, preventing closer packing of particles. Consequently, the dry density **decreases** beyond OMC.

*(Examiner's Note: Award full 3 marks only if both definitions are stated AND the wet-side mechanism is explained. A one-line answer scores at most 1 mark.)*

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: List the apparatus required for the Standard Proctor Compaction Test and state any three specifications.**

**Model Answer:**

**Apparatus Required:**
1. Proctor mold with collar and base plate (Volume = **1000 cm³**)
2. Mechanical rammer (Mass = **2.5 kg** for light compaction)
3. Straight edge (for trimming)
4. Moisture content cans
5. Thermostatically controlled oven (105°C – 110°C)
6. Weighing balance (accuracy 0.01 g)
7. Sieve (19 mm or 4.75 mm as per soil type)
8. Mixing tray, trowel, and graduated cylinder

**Three Specifications (IS 2720 Part VII – Light Compaction):**

| Specification | Value |
|---------------|-------|
| Rammer mass | **2.5 kg** |
| Drop height | **310 mm** |
| Number of layers | **3** |
| Blows per layer | **25** |

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Before Attempting**
> - **Do NOT confuse** Light (Standard) and Heavy (Modified) Proctor specifications. Mixing up the rammer mass (2.5 vs 4.9 kg) or layer count (3 vs 5) is a **favourite trick** to deduct 2–3 marks.
> - In dry density calculations, **forget the (1 + w) factor** and you lose 3–4 marks instantly. Always show the substitution step.
> - **Plot the ZAV line** wherever the question involves specific gravity — examiners award a separate 2-mark credit for it.
> - When asked for a "sketch", even a hand-drawn bell curve with axes labelled will fetch full marks — do not skip it.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat sketch the laboratory procedure for determining the OMC and MDD of a soil by the Standard Proctor Compaction Test as per IS 2720 (Part VII). State the specifications of the test.

**(b) [7 Marks]** The following data were obtained from a Standard Proctor Compaction Test on a soil sample (mold volume = 1000 cm³, mass of empty mold = 2.50 kg):

| Test No. | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|----------|---|---|---|---|---|
| Water content (%) | 8 | 12 | 16 | 20 | 24 |
| Mass of mold + wet soil (kg) | 3.95 | 4.21 | 4.36 | 4.28 | 4.06 |

**Plot the compaction curve and determine OMC and MDD.** Take $G = 2.68$ and find the **zero air voids dry density at OMC**.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Procedure & Specifications [7 Marks]**

**Specifications (IS 2720 Part VII – Light Compaction) [1 mark]:**

- Mold volume: **1000 cm³**
- Rammer mass: **2.5 kg**
- Drop height: **310 mm**
- Number of layers: **3**
- Blows per layer: **25**
- Compactive effort: ≈ 595 kJ/m³

**Procedure [5 marks for steps + 1 mark for sketch]:**

1. **Sample Preparation**: Take about **20 kg of air-dried soil**. Sieve through **19 mm sieve** (or 4.75 mm for fine-grained soils). Divide into **5 equal portions** of ~2.5–3 kg each.

2. **Initial Water Addition**: Add water to the first portion to bring it to about **4–6%** water content. Mix thoroughly. Keep subsequent portions at increasing water contents (typically 2% increments), spanning both dry and wet sides of the expected OMC.

3. **Curing**: Allow each mixed sample to **cure for 5–15 minutes** in a covered container so water distributes uniformly.

4. **Compaction in Mold**: Attach the collar to the mold. Compact the soil in **3 equal layers**, each receiving **25 uniformly distributed blows** from the 2.5 kg rammer dropped from 310 mm.

5. **Trimming**: Remove the collar. Trim the soil flush with the top of the mold using a **straight edge**. Clean the outside of the mold.

6. **Weighing**: Weigh the mold with the compacted wet soil to the nearest 1 g.

7. **Moisture Content**: Take a representative moisture sample from the **middle of the compacted layer**, weigh it, oven-dry at **105°C – 110°C for 24 hours**, and determine the water content.

8. **Repetition**: Repeat steps 4–7 for the remaining 4 portions at progressively higher water contents.

9. **Plotting**: Plot **dry density ($\rho_d$) on the y-axis** vs **water content ($w$) on the x-axis**. Draw a smooth curve through the points. The **peak of the curve** gives the MDD and OMC.

> **Neat Sketch [1 mark]**: Bell-shaped compaction curve with axes clearly labelled, peak point identified, and the OMC and MDD annotated.

---

#### **Part (b) — Calculations [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Wet mass of soil in each test [1 mark]**

$$m_{wet} = m_{mold+wet\,soil} - 2.50 \text{ kg}$$

| Test | $m_{mold+wet}$ (kg) | $m_{wet}$ (kg) | $m_{wet}$ (g) |
|------|---------------------|----------------|----------------|
| 1 | 3.95 | 1.45 | 1450 |
| 2 | 4.21 | 1.71 | 1710 |
| 3 | 4.36 | 1.86 | 1860 |
| 4 | 4.28 | 1.78 | 1780 |
| 5 | 4.06 | 1.56 | 1560 |

**Step 2: Bulk density [1 mark]**

$$\rho = \frac{m_{wet}}{V} = \frac{m_{wet}}{1000} \text{ g/cm}^3$$

| Test | $\rho$ (g/cm³) |
|------|----------------|
| 1 | 1.450 |
| 2 | 1.710 |
| 3 | 1.860 |
| 4 | 1.780 |
| 5 | 1.560 |

**Step 3: Dry density [2 marks]**

$$\rho_d = \frac{\rho}{1 + w}$$

For Test 1 ($w = 0.08$):

$$\rho_{d,1} = \frac{1.450}{1.08} = 1.343 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

For Test 2 ($w = 0.12$):

$$\rho_{d,2} = \frac{1.710}{1.12} = 1.527 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

For Test 3 ($w = 0.16$):

$$\rho_{d,3} = \frac{1.860}{1.16} = 1.603 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

For Test 4 ($w = 0.20$):

$$\rho_{d,4} = \frac{1.780}{1.20} = 1.483 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

For Test 5 ($w = 0.24$):

$$\rho_{d,5} = \frac{1.560}{1.24} = 1.258 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

| Test | $w$ (%) | $\rho$ | $\rho_d$ (g/cm³) |
|------|---------|--------|-------------------|
| 1 | 8 | 1.450 | 1.343 |
| 2 | 12 | 1.710 | 1.527 |
| 3 | **16** | 1.860 | **1.603** ← Peak |
| 4 | 20 | 1.780 | 1.483 |
| 5 | 24 | 1.560 | 1.258 |

**Step 4: OMC and MDD [1 mark]**

From the compaction curve (plot of $\rho_d$ vs $w$):

$$\boxed{\text{OMC} = 16\% \quad ; \quad \text{MDD} = 1.603 \text{ g/cm}^3}$$

**Step 5: ZAV dry density at OMC [2 marks]**

$$\rho_{d,\text{ZAV}} = \frac{G \cdot \rho_w}{1 + w \cdot G} = \frac{2.68 \times 1.0}{1 + 0.16 \times 2.68}$$

$$\rho_{d,\text{ZAV}} = \frac{2.68}{1 + 0.4288} = \frac{2.68}{1.4288} = 1.876 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

**Verification**: MDD (1.603) < ZAV density (1.876) ✓ — Result is physically valid.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between **Standard Proctor (Light Compaction) Test** and **Modified Proctor (Heavy Compaction) Test**. Explain how the compaction curve shifts with increasing compactive effort. State the engineering significance of OMC and MDD.

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is the **Zero Air Voids (ZAV) line**? Derive its equation. A soil has a specific gravity of 2.72. Compute the ZAV dry densities at water contents of 8%, 12%, 16%, and 20%. Plot the ZAV curve and comment on its significance as an upper bound to the compaction curve.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### **Part (a) — Comparison Table & Engineering Significance [7 Marks]**

**Differences Table [4 marks]:**

| Parameter | Standard Proctor (Light) — IS 2720 (Part VII) | Modified Proctor (Heavy) — IS 2720 (Part VIII) |
|-----------|-----------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------|
| Rammer mass | 2.5 kg | 4.9 kg |
| Drop height | 310 mm | 450 mm |
| Number of layers | 3 | 5 |
| Blows per layer | 25 | 25 |
| Compactive effort | ≈ 595 kJ/m³ | ≈ 2700 kJ/m³ |
| Typical OMC | Higher (12–18%) | Lower (8–12%) |
| Typical MDD | Lower | Higher |

**Effect of Increasing Compactive Effort on the Compaction Curve [2 marks]:**

- The compaction curve shifts **upward and to the left** — the **MDD increases** while the **OMC decreases**.
- Physically: more energy per unit volume expels more air, allowing denser packing at lower water content.
- The ZAV line remains the **same upper bound** (since ZAV depends only on $G$, not on effort).

**Engineering Significance of OMC and MDD [1 mark]:**
- OMC guides **field water addition** before rolling.
- MDD is the **field density target** (typically 95–98% of MDD is required as per IRC/MORTH specifications for highway subgrades).
- Together they define the **compaction specification** in any earthwork contract.

---

#### **Part (b) — ZAV Line Derivation & Plot [7 Marks]**

**Derivation [3 marks]:**

At zero air voids, $S = 1$ (100% saturated). From the basic phase relation:

$$S \cdot e = w \cdot G$$

With $S = 1$:

$$e = w \cdot G$$

The general dry density expression is:

$$\rho_d = \frac{G \cdot \rho_w}{1 + e}$$

Substituting $e = w \cdot G$:

$$\boxed{\rho_{d,\text{ZAV}} = \frac{G \cdot \rho_w}{1 + w \cdot G}}$$

**Calculation [3 marks]:** With $G = 2.72$ and $\rho_w = 1.0$ g/cm³:

For $w = 0.08$:

$$\rho_{d} = \frac{2.72}{1 + 0.08 \times 2.72} = \frac{2.72}{1.2176} = 2.234 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

For $w = 0.12$:

$$\rho_{d} = \frac{2.72}{1 + 0.12 \times 2.72} = \frac{2.72}{1.3264} = 2.051 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

For $w = 0.16$:

$$\rho_{d} = \frac{2.72}{1 + 0.16 \times 2.72} = \frac{2.72}{1.4352} = 1.895 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

For $w = 0.20$:

$$\rho_{d} = \frac{2.72}{1 + 0.20 \times 2.72} = \frac{2.72}{1.5440} = 1.762 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

| $w$ (%) | $\rho_{d,\text{ZAV}}$ (g/cm³) |
|---------|-------------------------------|
| 8 | 2.234 |
| 12 | 2.051 |
| 16 | 1.895 |
| 20 | 1.762 |

**Significance / Comment [1 mark]:**

The ZAV line is a **hyperbolic upper bound** to all compaction curves for the same soil. Since compaction cannot expel all air, no experimental point can lie on or above the ZAV line. If a laboratory result plots **above** the ZAV, the data is erroneous (likely due to incorrect moisture content, specific gravity assumption, or mold volume). In practice, field compaction curves approach but never touch the ZAV line.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets — Read Before Every KTU Exam**

- [x] **Compaction** removes **air**, not water. **Consolidation** removes water from saturated clays.
- [x] **Standard Proctor (Light)**: 2.5 kg rammer, 310 mm drop, **3 layers**, 25 blows. Effort ≈ 595 kJ/m³.
- [x] **Modified Proctor (Heavy)**: 4.9 kg rammer, 450 mm drop, **5 layers**, 25 blows. Effort ≈ 2700 kJ/m³.
- [x] **Mold volume** is always **1000 cm³** for both standards.
- [x] **Master formula**: $\rho_d = \dfrac{\rho}{1+w}$ — water content $w$ must be in **decimal** form.
- [x] **ZAV formula**: $\rho_{d,\text{ZAV}} = \dfrac{G \cdot \rho_w}{1 + w \cdot G}$.
- [x] Compaction curve is **bell-shaped**: dry density rises on dry side, peaks at OMC, falls on wet side.
- [x] Increased compactive effort → **higher MDD, lower OMC** (curve shifts up and left).
- [x] **ZAV line** is the **upper bound** of all compaction curves for a given $G$.
- [x] Degree of saturation at OMC is typically **80–90%** (not 100%) — compaction cannot achieve full saturation.
- [x] **Curing time** of 5–15 minutes is essential for uniform water distribution.
- [x] **Oven temperature**: 105°C – 110°C for **24 hours**.
- [x] **Field specification**: Achieve 95–98% of MDD at OMC ± 2% (as per IS/IRC standards for highways).
- [x] The ZAV curve is a **hyperbola** — never linear.
- [x] When a question provides 5 data points, the **peak** of the smooth curve (not necessarily a data point) defines OMC and MDD.
- [x] In KTU exams, **always plot the ZAV line** if $G$ is given — it carries **dedicated marks**.
- [x] **Common units**: $w$ in % for plotting, in decimal for calculation; $\rho_d$ in g/cm³; $\gamma_d$ in kN/m³.
- [x] Conversion: $\gamma_d = \rho_d \times 9.81$ kN/m³; $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³ or 1000 kgf/m³ or 1 g/cm³.
- [x] **Significance of OMC**: Determines the quantity of water to be added (or dried) in the field before rolling.
- [x] **Significance of MDD**: Sets the target density for field quality control (sand cone, core cutter, or nuclear density gauge tests).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shrinkage Limit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL408 - SOIL SCIENCE & MECHANICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Shrinkage Limit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Technical Definition (IS 2720 – Part 6)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shrinkage Limit (Ws)** is the maximum water content (expressed as a percentage of the mass of oven-dried soil) at which any further reduction in moisture content will **not** cause a decrease in the volume of the soil mass. Below this water content, the soil is in the **solid state**, and voids are partially filled with air; the soil-water-air system behaves as an unsaturated medium with a fixed soil skeleton.

Mathematically, it represents the water content at which the soil is just **saturated** at its minimum volume. It is the lower-boundary water content in the Atterberg limits framework.

$$W_s \;=\; \frac{\text{Mass of water just sufficient to fill all voids of the dry soil pat}}{\text{Mass of oven-dried soil}} \times 100$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine soaking a **kitchen sponge** fully in water and then leaving it on a sunny windowsill.
>  - As the sponge drips, its **volume shrinks** (water leaves, sponge contracts).
>  - At some point, the sponge stops shrinking even though it still feels slightly damp. From this moment on, drying only makes it stiffer, not smaller — the air is simply replacing water inside the pores.
>  - The water content at the moment the sponge **stops shrinking** is exactly analogous to the **Shrinkage Limit** of a clay soil.

For soils, the same phenomenon occurs: a saturated clay pat dries, contracts uniformly while it remains saturated, and at the **shrinkage limit** the voids are no longer completely full of water — air starts to enter, and further drying no longer changes the volume.

### 1.3 Why it Matters in Civil Engineering

> [!TIP]
> - Predicts **heave and shrinkage** of expansive clays under buildings, roads, and canal linings.
> - Used to compute **Shrinkage Ratio (SR)** and **Volumetric Shrinkage (VS)**, both used in pavement and earthwork design.
> - Indicates the **stability** of soil as a construction material.
> - Helps identify problematic black-cotton soils (very high shrinkage potential).

**Typical values of Ws (for context):**
- **Sands & silts:** Often non-shrinkable; Ws approaches the water content at placement.
- **Clays of low plasticity (CL):** Ws ≈ **8 % – 15 %**
- **Clays of high plasticity (CH):** Ws ≈ **10 % – 20 %**
- **Expansive / black-cotton soils:** Ws may exceed **25 %**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Position of Shrinkage Limit in the Atterberg Limits Framework

The behaviour of a fine-grained soil as water is progressively removed passes through **four distinct states**, separated by three limits:

| Soil State          | Boundary Limit        | Behaviour on Drying                          |
|---------------------|-----------------------|----------------------------------------------|
| Liquid              | —                     | Soil flows like a viscous fluid.             |
| Plastic             | **Liquid Limit (WL)** | Soil can be moulded; deforms plastically.    |
| Semi-Solid          | **Plastic Limit (Wp)**| Soil crumbles; loses plasticity.             |
| Solid               | **Shrinkage Limit (Ws)** | Soil stops changing volume; only mass decreases. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The order of the limits is always: **Ws < Wp < WL**. The numerical difference Wp − Ws is a useful index of soil shrinkage behaviour.

### 2.2 Volume–Water Content Relationship (Theoretical Basis)

If we plot **volume of soil pat (V)** against **water content (w)** during drying:

1. **Above Ws (saturated regime):** The relationship is linear. Volume decreases **1:1** with water loss. The plot is a straight line of slope ≈ −1 (when w is in % and V is in cc).
2. **At Ws:** The line bends. The soil reaches its **minimum volume** (Vd) at the shrinkage limit.
3. **Below Ws (unsaturated regime):** The plot is horizontal — volume is constant, equal to Vd. Further loss of water only replaces water with air in the voids.

The shrinkage limit is therefore the **knee point** of this volume-change curve.

### 2.3 Methods of Determination

1. **Mercury Displacement Method** (IS 2720 Part 6, 1972) – *most common* and recommended.
2. **Wax Coating Method** – used for very soft or crumbly dry pats.
3. **Water Displacement Method** – direct immersion of dry pat in a graduated cylinder (least accurate).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every equation the examiner expects you to remember. Memorise the symbol definitions and the density of mercury (**13.6 g/cc**).

| #  | Quantity | Formula | Definition of Terms |
|----|----------|---------|---------------------|
| 1  | **Shrinkage Limit, Ws** | $W_s=\dfrac{\left(M_w\right)-\left(V_1-V_d\right)\rho_w}{M_s}\times 100$ | $M_w$ = mass of water in wet pat; $V_1$ = volume of wet pat (= volume of dish); $V_d$ = volume of dry pat; $\rho_w$ = 1 g/cc; $M_s$ = mass of oven-dry soil |
| 2  | Simplified Ws (when $V_1=V_d$) | $W_s=\dfrac{M_w}{M_s}\times 100$ | Used only when no shrinkage occurred |
| 3  | **Volume of Mercury in Dish** | $V_1=\dfrac{m_{Hg,\;dish}}{\rho_{Hg}}=\dfrac{m_2-m_1}{13.6}$ | $m_1$ = empty dish; $m_2$ = dish + mercury (full) |
| 4  | **Volume of Dry Soil Pat** | $V_d=\dfrac{(m_2-m_1)-(m_4-m_3)}{13.6}$ | $m_3$ = dish + dry pat; $m_4$ = dish + dry pat + mercury (rest filled) |
| 5  | **Shrinkage Ratio, SR** | $SR=\dfrac{M_s}{V_d}$ | Dimensionless (or g/cc); indicates the bulk density of the dry pat |
| 6  | **Volumetric Shrinkage, VS** | $VS=\left(W-W_s\right)\cdot SR$ | $W$ = natural water content; VS expressed in % of dry volume |
| 7  | **Linear Shrinkage, LS** | $LS=\left[1-\sqrt[3]{1-\dfrac{VS}{100}}\right]\times 100$ | Used in pavement design; LS in % |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pavement Engineering:** VS is used by IRC / IRC-SP guidelines to estimate cracking of expansive sub-grades.
- **Building Foundations:** Differential heave due to seasonal moisture change can crack light structures; Ws helps classify the hazard.
- **Earth Dams and Canals:** Shrinkage of clay cores/lining must be predicted; low Ws indicates good resistance to cracking on drying.
- **Soil Identification & Classification (IS 1498, IS 1726):** Ws is a parameter in the **shrinkage index (Ip − Is)** which separates clay minerals.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Shrinkage-Limit Formula

Let a saturated soil pat occupy a volume $V_1$ (equal to the volume of the shrinkage dish) at the start of the test. Let the mass of oven-dry soil in the pat be $M_s$ and the mass of water present be $M_w$. As drying proceeds:

**Step 1 — Identify the two regimes**

(a) **Above Ws (saturated):** Every gram of water lost produces a corresponding 1 cc reduction in volume (water is being replaced by nothing — the void closes up). This is the *1-for-1* rule.

$$\Delta V \;=\; \Delta M_w \quad (\text{for } W > W_s)$$

(b) **Below Ws (unsaturated):** Any further loss of water simply replaces it with air; volume stays equal to $V_d$.

**Step 2 — Define the volume change**

The total volume lost during drying from the initial wet state to the dry pat is:

$$\Delta V \;=\; V_1 - V_d$$

**Step 3 — Relate the water lost to the volume lost**

By the 1-for-1 rule, the mass of water that left the soil *purely as shrinkage* is:

$$M_{w,\text{shrink}} \;=\; (V_1 - V_d)\cdot \rho_w \;=\; (V_1 - V_d) \cdot 1 \;\text{g}$$

**Step 4 — Determine water that remains in the dry pat**

The water remaining in the dry soil at the shrinkage limit is:

$$M_{w,\text{remaining}} \;=\; M_w - (V_1 - V_d)\rho_w$$

**Step 5 — Apply the definition of Ws**

By definition, Ws is the water content of the dry pat when it has just reached its minimum volume (i.e., when its voids are still saturated with water but no more shrinkage is possible):

$$W_s \;=\; \frac{M_{w,\text{remaining}}}{M_s}\times 100$$

Substituting Step 4:

$$\boxed{\,W_s \;=\; \frac{\left[M_w - (V_1 - V_d)\rho_w\right]}{M_s}\times 100\,}$$

This is the **fundamental IS 2720 expression** for shrinkage limit.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Mercury Displacement Method)

> [!NOTE]
> This is a classic KTU/University exam-style problem. Master the steps and the unit conversions.

**Given Data (Mercury Displacement Method):**

| Symbol | Observation                                              | Mass (g) |
|:------:|----------------------------------------------------------|---------:|
| $W_3$  | Mass of empty shrinkage dish                             | 32.50    |
| $W_1$  | Mass of dish + wet soil                                  | 73.00    |
| $W_2$  | Mass of dish + oven-dry soil                             | 62.50    |
| $W_4$  | Mass of dish completely filled with mercury              | 329.25   |
| $W_5$  | Mass of dish + dry soil pat, with rest filled by mercury | 103.30   |

**Required:** Shrinkage Limit, Shrinkage Ratio, and Volumetric Shrinkage (assuming natural water content $W = 30\,\%$).

**Solution – Step by Step**

**Step A — Mass of dry soil and water in the original wet pat**

$$M_s \;=\; W_2 - W_3 \;=\; 62.50 - 32.50 \;=\; 30.00 \text{ g}$$

$$M_w \;=\; W_1 - W_2 \;=\; 73.00 - 62.50 \;=\; 10.50 \text{ g}$$

**Step B — Volume of the shrinkage dish, $V_1$**

Mass of mercury that completely fills the dish:

$$m_{Hg,\;dish} \;=\; W_4 - W_3 \;=\; 329.25 - 32.50 \;=\; 296.75 \text{ g}$$

$$V_1 \;=\; \frac{m_{Hg,\;dish}}{\rho_{Hg}} \;=\; \frac{296.75}{13.6} \;=\; 21.819 \text{ cc}$$

**Step C — Volume of the dry soil pat, $V_d$**

Mass of mercury that fills the void space around the soil pat:

$$m_{Hg,\;around} \;=\; W_5 - W_2 \;=\; 103.30 - 62.50 \;=\; 40.80 \text{ g}$$

$$V_{Hg,\;around} \;=\; \frac{40.80}{13.6} \;=\; 3.000 \text{ cc}$$

Therefore the dry soil pat occupies:

$$V_d \;=\; V_1 - V_{Hg,\;around} \;=\; 21.819 - 3.000 \;=\; 18.819 \text{ cc}$$

**Step D — Compute Shrinkage Limit, $W_s$**

Applying the derived formula:

$$W_s \;=\; \frac{M_w - (V_1 - V_d)\rho_w}{M_s}\times 100$$

Substitute $M_w = 10.50$, $V_1 - V_d = 21.819 - 18.819 = 3.000$ cc, $\rho_w = 1$, $M_s = 30.00$:

$$W_s \;=\; \frac{10.50 - (3.000)(1.0)}{30.00}\times 100 \;=\; \frac{7.50}{30.00}\times 100$$

$$\boxed{\,W_s \;=\; 25.00\,\%\,}$$

**Step E — Compute Shrinkage Ratio, SR**

$$SR \;=\; \frac{M_s}{V_d} \;=\; \frac{30.00}{18.819} \;=\; 1.594$$

**Step F — Compute Volumetric Shrinkage, VS**

$$VS \;=\; (W - W_s)\times SR \;=\; (30 - 25)\times 1.594 \;=\; 7.97\,\%$$

**Step G — Optional: Linear Shrinkage, LS**

$$LS \;=\; \left[1 - \sqrt[3]{1 - \dfrac{VS}{100}}\right]\times 100$$

$$LS \;=\; \left[1 - \sqrt[3]{1 - 0.0797}\right]\times 100$$

$$LS \;=\; \left[1 - 0.9724\right]\times 100 \;=\; 2.76\,\%$$

**Summary of Results:**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|------:|
| Shrinkage Limit, $W_s$ | **25.00 %** |
| Shrinkage Ratio, $SR$ | **1.594** |
| Volumetric Shrinkage, $VS$ | **7.97 %** |
| Linear Shrinkage, $LS$ | **2.76 %** |

---

### 3.3 Phase-Diagram Check (Verification of Result)

> [!TIP]
> Always cross-check using the fundamental relation at the shrinkage limit:
> $$V_d = \frac{M_s}{G_s\cdot\rho_w} + \frac{W_s\cdot M_s}{100}\cdot\frac{1}{\rho_w}$$
> With $G_s = 2.65$ and $W_s = 25\,\%$:
> $$V_d = \frac{30}{2.65} + 0.25\times 30 = 11.32 + 7.50 = 18.82 \text{ cc}\;\;(\text{matches!})$$

This phase-relation check is a powerful tool for KTU 14-mark problems where multiple sub-questions are linked.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Flow Architecture: Mercury-Displacement Test Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Clean Dry Shrinkage Dish] --> A1[Weigh Empty Dish as W3]
    A1 --> A2[Fill Dish with Mercury Level Off with Glass Plate]
    A2 --> A3[Weigh as W4: Compute V1 Equals W4 minus W3 divided by 13.6]
    A3 --> A4[Empty Dish and Re Clean]
    A4 --> A5[Place Wet Soil in Dish 1 Third Volume Tap to Remove Air]
    A5 --> A6[Weigh Dish plus Wet Soil as W1]
    A6 --> A7[Oven Dry at 105 to 110 Degree Celsius for 24 Hours]
    A7 --> A8[Cool in Desiccator Weigh Dish plus Dry Pat as W2]
    A8 --> A9[Transfer Dry Pat Back to Empty Dish]
    A9 --> A10[Fill Rest of Dish with Mercury Level Off with Glass Plate]
    A10 --> A11[Weigh as W5: Compute Vd]
    A11 --> A12[Apply Formula: Ws equals Mw minus V1 minus Vd divided by Ms times 100]
    A12 --> A13[Compute Shrinkage Ratio and Volumetric Shrinkage]
    A13 --> A14[Report Result with Two Replicate Values within 2 Percent]
```

### 4.2 Block Architecture: Soil State Diagram with Atterberg Limits

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ST1[Liquid State] --> ST2[Liquid Limit WL]
    ST2 --> ST3[Plastic State]
    ST3 --> ST4[Plastic Limit WP]
    ST4 --> ST5[Semi Solid State]
    ST5 --> ST6[Shrinkage Limit WS]
    ST6 --> ST7[Solid State]
    ST7 -.->|Air Replaces Water Volume Constant| ST7
```

### 4.3 Block Architecture: Volume versus Water Content Behaviour

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BB1[Wet Saturated Soil Pat] --> BB2[Water Content Greater than WS]
    BB2 --> BB3[Volume Decreases 1 is to 1 with Water Loss]
    BB3 --> BB4[At WS: Minimum Volume Vd Reached]
    BB4 --> BB5[Below WS: Air Enters Voids]
    BB5 --> BB6[Volume Stays Constant Further Drying Only Reduces Mass]
    BB6 --> BB7[Final State: Dry Soil Pat Volume Vd Mass Ms]
```

### 4.4 Comparison Matrix of Test Methods (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CM1[Shrinkage Limit Test] --> CM2[Mercury Displacement Method]
    CM1 --> CM3[Wax Coating Method]
    CM1 --> CM4[Water Displacement Method]
    CM2 --> CM5[Mercury Density 13.6 g per cc Highly Accurate]
    CM3 --> CM6[Archimedes Principle for Irregular Pats Avoids Mercury Hazard]
    CM4 --> CM7[Simplest Least Accurate Slow Pat Disintegrates]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Rendering Safeguard:** All node identifiers in the diagrams above are prefixed with letters (A0, ST1, BB1, CM1) and contain no reserved mermaid keywords. Labels are kept as plain uppercase alphanumeric text — **no bold, italic, or HTML formatting inside the quotes** — to ensure clean rendering in the GitHub/KTU LMS environment.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`
> **Define Shrinkage Limit. Explain its physical significance in the context of fine-grained soils.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Shrinkage limit ($W_s$) is defined as the maximum water content at which a further reduction in moisture will not cause any decrease in the volume of the soil mass. Below $W_s$, the soil attains its minimum volume $V_d$ and any additional water loss simply replaces pore-water by air without changing the volume. Physically, it marks the boundary between the **semi-solid and solid states** of a cohesive soil. Significance: it is used to evaluate the **shrinkage potential** of expansive clays, to compute the **shrinkage ratio** and **volumetric shrinkage** for pavement and foundation design, and to identify problematic soils (e.g., black cotton soil) prone to cracking on drying.

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`
> **Differentiate between Shrinkage Limit and Plastic Limit.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | Plastic Limit ($W_p$) | Shrinkage Limit ($W_s$) |
|-------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| Boundary | Between **plastic** and **semi-solid** states | Between **semi-solid** and **solid** states |
| Test | Rolling thread to 3 mm diameter until it crumbles | Mercury displacement on a dried pat |
| Property Indicated | Loss of **plasticity** (workability) | Loss of **volume change** on drying |
| Numerical Order | $W_s < W_p < W_L$ | — |
| Typical Range (Clays) | 15 – 30 % | 8 – 20 % |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU ESE - Module 2, 2024 Scheme Pattern]`
> **(a)** Explain the procedure for the determination of the shrinkage limit of a soil by the **Mercury Displacement Method** as per IS 2720 (Part 6). List the apparatus required. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** The following observations were recorded during a shrinkage limit test on a clayey soil using the mercury displacement method: **(7 Marks)**
>
> - Mass of empty shrinkage dish = **45.20 g**
> - Mass of dish + wet soil = **118.40 g**
> - Mass of dish + dry soil (after oven drying) = **101.80 g**
> - Mass of dish + mercury (completely filled) = **362.50 g**
> - Mass of dish + dry soil pat + mercury (rest filled) = **125.60 g**
>
> Determine:
> 1. Shrinkage Limit of the soil
> 2. Shrinkage Ratio
> 3. Volumetric Shrinkage, if the natural water content of the soil is **32 %**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — Procedure & Apparatus (7 marks)**

**Apparatus required:**
1. Shrinkage dish (porcelain, ~40 mm dia × 12.5 mm height, IS standard).
2. Glass plate with three prongs to level the mercury.
3. Mercury (≥ 250 g, technical grade), handled with a **spatula** — never touch with fingers.
4. Oven (105 °C – 110 °C, thermostatic).
5. Balance accurate to **0.01 g**.
6. Desiccator with silica gel.
7. Spatula, glass rod, dropper.

**Procedure (write in steps for full marks):**

1. Clean the shrinkage dish, dry and weigh it. Record as $W_3$.
2. Fill the dish with mercury, remove excess with a **pronged glass plate** ensuring no air is entrapped. Weigh as $W_4$. Compute dish volume $V_1 = (W_4 - W_3)/13.6$.
3. Empty the dish. Place a wet soil pat (about 1/3 the dish volume) and tap gently to remove entrapped air. Weigh as $W_1$.
4. Oven-dry the pat at 105 – 110 °C for 24 h. Cool in desiccator and weigh as $W_2$.
5. Place the **dry pat** back in the empty dish. Fill the remaining void with mercury, levelling with the pronged glass plate. Weigh as $W_5$.
6. Compute $V_d$ from the mercury displaced around the soil pat.
7. Apply the shrinkage-limit formula.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Dry soil mass**

$$M_s \;=\; W_2 - W_3 \;=\; 101.80 - 45.20 \;=\; 56.60 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2: Water mass in original wet pat**

$$M_w \;=\; W_1 - W_2 \;=\; 118.40 - 101.80 \;=\; 16.60 \text{ g}$$

**Step 3: Volume of dish, $V_1$**

$$m_{Hg,\;dish} \;=\; W_4 - W_3 \;=\; 362.50 - 45.20 \;=\; 317.30 \text{ g}$$

$$V_1 \;=\; \frac{317.30}{13.6} \;=\; 23.331 \text{ cc}$$

**Step 4: Volume of dry pat, $V_d$**

$$m_{Hg,\;around} \;=\; W_5 - W_2 \;=\; 125.60 - 101.80 \;=\; 23.80 \text{ g}$$

$$V_{Hg,\;around} \;=\; \frac{23.80}{13.6} \;=\; 1.750 \text{ cc}$$

$$V_d \;=\; V_1 - V_{Hg,\;around} \;=\; 23.331 - 1.750 \;=\; 21.581 \text{ cc}$$

**Step 5: Shrinkage Limit, $W_s$**

$$W_s \;=\; \frac{M_w - (V_1 - V_d)\rho_w}{M_s}\times 100$$

$$W_s \;=\; \frac{16.60 - (23.331 - 21.581)(1.0)}{56.60}\times 100$$

$$W_s \;=\; \frac{16.60 - 1.750}{56.60}\times 100 \;=\; \frac{14.85}{56.60}\times 100$$

$$\boxed{\,W_s \;=\; 26.24\,\%\,}$$

**Step 6: Shrinkage Ratio, SR**

$$SR \;=\; \frac{M_s}{V_d} \;=\; \frac{56.60}{21.581} \;=\; 2.622$$

**Step 7: Volumetric Shrinkage, VS (for $W = 32\,\%$)**

$$VS \;=\; (W - W_s)\cdot SR \;=\; (32 - 26.24)\times 2.622$$

$$VS \;=\; 5.76 \times 2.622 \;=\; 15.10\,\%$$

**Final Answer:** $W_s = 26.24\,\%$, $SR = 2.622$, $VS = 15.10\,\%$

---

> **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):** `[KTU ESE - Module 2, Internal Choice Pattern]`
> **(a)** What are **Atterberg Limits**? Sketch the soil state diagram showing liquid, plastic and shrinkage limits. Discuss the factors affecting the shrinkage limit of a soil. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A clay sample has a **shrinkage limit of 22 %**, a **plastic limit of 28 %**, and a **liquid limit of 62 %**. If the **natural water content is 35 %**, compute the **shrinkage ratio, volumetric shrinkage and linear shrinkage** of the soil. Given: $G_s = 2.70$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — Atterberg Limits (7 marks)**

**Definition:** Atterberg limits are the water contents at which a fine-grained soil changes its state from liquid to plastic, plastic to semi-solid, and semi-solid to solid.

**Soil state diagram (refer Section 4.2).**

**Three primary limits:**
- **Liquid Limit (WL)** — minimum water content at which soil flows under its own weight (Casagrande cup / fall-cone test).
- **Plastic Limit (Wp)** — minimum water content at which the soil can be rolled into a 3 mm thread without crumbling.
- **Shrinkage Limit (Ws)** — water content below which no further volume reduction occurs on drying.

**Derived indices:**
- Plasticity Index: $I_p = W_L - W_p$
- Shrinkage Index: $I_s = W_p - W_s$
- Consistency Index: $I_c = (W_L - W)/I_p$

**Factors affecting Shrinkage Limit:**

1. **Clay mineralogy:** Montmorillonite (smectite) has very high $W_s$ (high surface area, high water-holding capacity); kaolinite has low $W_s$.
2. **Specific surface area:** Higher surface area → higher $W_s$.
3. **Specific gravity ($G_s$):** Heavier minerals have lower $W_s$.
4. **Organic content:** Increases $W_s$ (organic matter holds water).
5. **Cation exchange capacity (CEC):** Soils with high CEC retain more water → higher $W_s$.
6. **Pore-fluid chemistry:** Saline water reduces double-layer thickness → reduces $W_s$.
7. **Compaction effort / initial density:** Minor effect, but highly compacted soils may show slightly higher $W_s$ due to better particle packing.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**Given:** $W_s = 22\,\%$, $W_p = 28\,\%$, $W_L = 62\,\%$, natural water content $W = 35\,\%$, $G_s = 2.70$.

**Step 1 — Mass of dry soil, $M_s$ (take a reference of 100 g)**
Assume $M_s = 100 \text{ g}$ for convenience.

**Step 2 — Volume of dry soil pat, $V_d$ (using phase-relation)**

At shrinkage limit, the soil is saturated and at minimum volume:

$$V_d \;=\; \frac{M_s}{G_s\cdot\rho_w} + \frac{W_s\cdot M_s}{100}\cdot\frac{1}{\rho_w}$$

$$V_d \;=\; \frac{100}{2.70 \times 1.0} + \frac{22 \times 100}{100 \times 1.0} \;=\; 37.037 + 22.000$$

$$V_d \;=\; 59.037 \text{ cc}$$

**Step 3 — Shrinkage Ratio, SR**

$$SR \;=\; \frac{M_s}{V_d} \;=\; \frac{100}{59.037} \;=\; 1.694$$

**Step 4 — Volumetric Shrinkage, VS**

$$VS \;=\; (W - W_s)\times SR \;=\; (35 - 22)\times 1.694$$

$$VS \;=\; 13 \times 1.694 \;=\; 22.02\,\%$$

**Step 5 — Linear Shrinkage, LS**

$$LS \;=\; \left[1 - \sqrt[3]{1 - \dfrac{VS}{100}}\right]\times 100$$

$$LS \;=\; \left[1 - \sqrt[3]{1 - 0.2202}\right]\times 100$$

$$LS \;=\; \left[1 - 0.9235\right]\times 100 \;=\; 7.95\,\%$$

**Final Answer:** $SR = 1.694$, $VS = 22.02\,\%$, $LS \approx 7.95\,\%$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Shrinkage-Limit Problems:**
> 1. **Density of mercury unit error:** Examiners frequently report partial credit deduction if students write **13.6 kg/L** or forget to convert to g/cc when mass is in grams. Always use **$\rho_{Hg} = 13.6 \text{ g/cc}$** when masses are in grams and volumes in cc.
> 2. **Sign convention in Ws formula:** Some students mistakenly write $W_s = (M_w + (V_1 - V_d)\rho_w)/M_s$. The correct expression **subtracts** the shrinkage-equivalent water because $V_1 > V_d$ implies the volume change is positive, but the remaining water is reduced.
> 3. **Confusing $W_4$ and $W_5$:** $W_4$ = dish + mercury (full, no soil). $W_5$ = dish + dry soil + mercury (rest filled). Mixing these will produce a **negative Ws** (an obvious mistake).
> 4. **Forgetting to multiply by 100:** Ws is reported as a *percentage*. Many students write the value as 0.25 instead of 25 %. **Always end with the % sign.**
> 5. **No two-replicate rule:** IS code requires the two values of Ws to agree within **2 %**; if not, the test must be repeated. Examiners give credit for mentioning this repeatability criterion.
> 6. **Neglecting safety in mercury method:** Mercury is highly toxic. Examiners expect mention of: "use gloves, spatula, do not spill, and dispose of mercury waste through authorised chemical waste channels."

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (pin this before every lab viva and exam):**

- ✅ **Shrinkage Limit ($W_s$)** = max water content below which no further volume change occurs.
- ✅ **Boundary:** $W_s < W_p < W_L$ — always.
- ✅ **Standard test method:** IS 2720 (Part 6) — Mercury Displacement Method.
- ✅ **Density of mercury:** $\rho_{Hg} = 13.6 \text{ g/cc}$ (memorise verbatim).
- ✅ **Core formula:** $W_s = \dfrac{M_w - (V_1 - V_d)\rho_w}{M_s}\times 100$ — derive on demand.
- ✅ **Shrinkage Ratio:** $SR = M_s / V_d$ — a measure of the dry pat's bulk density; dimensionless.
- ✅ **Volumetric Shrinkage:** $VS = (W - W_s)\times SR$ — predicts the percentage volume loss on drying.
- ✅ **Linear Shrinkage:** $LS = \left[1 - \sqrt[3]{1 - VS/100}\right]\times 100$ — used in pavement design.
- ✅ **Phase-relation check at $W_s$:** $V_d = \dfrac{M_s}{G_s\rho_w} + \dfrac{W_s M_s}{100\rho_w}$ — always cross-check.
- ✅ **High Ws** indicates **expansive** clay (montmorillonite-rich, black-cotton soil) — engineering hazard.
- ✅ **Two values of Ws must agree within 2 %** — otherwise repeat the test (IS code requirement).
- ✅ **Apparatus & safety:** shrinkage dish (40 mm × 12.5 mm), pronged glass plate, mercury, oven (105–110 °C), desiccator — mercury handled with spatula, never bare hands.
- ✅ **Practical tip for lab:** Ensure the dish is **completely dry and at room temperature** before weighing; warm dish + warm mercury → erroneous readings.
- ✅ **Atterberg framework:** Ws is one of three limits, all expressed as **water content (%)** but measured with **different test methods** (Casagrande/Fall-cone for WL; rolling thread for Wp; mercury displacement for Ws).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Constant Head Test to Determine Coefficient of Permeability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL408 - SOIL SCIENCE & MECHANICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Constant Head Test to Determine Coefficient of Permeability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Coefficient of Permeability (k)** is defined as the rate of flow of water through a unit cross-sectional area of soil under a unit hydraulic gradient, at a specified temperature. It has units of velocity (**cm/s** or **m/s**).

The **Constant Head Permeability Test** is a laboratory method used to determine the coefficient of permeability (**k**) of **coarse-grained soils** (clean sands and gravels with little or no fines). In this test, water is allowed to flow through a saturated soil specimen under a **steady (constant) hydraulic head difference**, and the volume of water flowing out in a measured time interval is recorded. The test is governed by **Darcy's Law (1856)**, which states that for laminar flow conditions in saturated porous media, the discharge velocity is directly proportional to the hydraulic gradient.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCAGL408 – Module 2):** The constant head test is restricted to **coarse-grained soils** because the high flow rates in such soils allow accurate volumetric measurements. For fine-grained soils, the **variable head permeameter** is preferred due to very low flow rates.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine pouring water through a **vertical glass cylinder filled with marbles** versus the same cylinder filled with **wet clay powder**. Water rushes through the marble-filled cylinder almost instantly because the void spaces between marbles are large and interconnected. Through the clay powder, water barely seeps out because the pores are microscopically tiny.

Now imagine fixing a **constant water level** at the top using a small overflow tank (like a swimming pool with an automatic filler). The water will flow steadily downward through the soil at a steady rate. If you collect the outflow water in a measuring jug for 1 minute and find, say, **500 mL**, you can mathematically work backward to determine how easily water passes through the soil. That "ease of passage" is exactly the **coefficient of permeability**.

> [!TIP]
> **Think of `k` as the "hydraulic conductivity passport"** of a soil — it tells you how freely water can travel through the soil skeleton under standard conditions.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range (Coarse Soils) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Coefficient of Permeability | $k$ | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-5}$ | cm/s |
| Standard Test Temperature | $T_{std}$ | 20 | °C |
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | 9.81 | m/s² |
| Dynamic viscosity of water at 20°C | $\mu_{20}$ | $1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ | Pa·s |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Underlying Principle — Darcy's Law

The entire test is built upon **Henry Darcy's Law** for one-dimensional laminar flow through saturated porous media:

$$v = k \cdot i$$

Where:
- $v$ = **discharge velocity** (also called Darcy velocity or superficial velocity) in **cm/s**
- $k$ = **coefficient of permeability** (or hydraulic conductivity) in **cm/s**
- $i$ = **hydraulic gradient** (dimensionless), defined as $i = \dfrac{h}{L}$

## 2.2 Why "Constant Head"?

In coarse soils, water flows **fast** (high $k$). If we used a falling-head setup, the head difference would drop noticeably during a single reading, making accurate measurement difficult. By **maintaining a constant overflow tank**, the head $h$ stays fixed during the test, and the outflow $Q$ remains steady. This is the essence of the **constant head test**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validity Conditions of Darcy's Law:** The test results are valid **only under laminar flow conditions**. The Reynolds number for flow through soil pores should be less than **1 to 10** (typically taken as 1 for conservative design). For coarse gravels, Darcy's law may be violated due to turbulent flow.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The experiment unfolds in a strict logical sequence:

1. **Specimen Preparation:** A representative coarse-grained soil sample is oven-dried and carefully placed in the permeameter mould in uniform layers, compacted to a known relative density.
2. **Saturation:** The specimen is saturated from below (to expel air pockets) using de-aired water. Air in voids would block flow and yield erroneously low $k$ values.
3. **Establishing Constant Head:** The inlet reservoir is adjusted until a steady overflow occurs, ensuring $h$ = constant throughout the test.
4. **Steady-State Flow:** Water is allowed to flow until a constant discharge rate is achieved (this confirms steady-state conditions).
5. **Volumetric Measurement:** A measuring cylinder collects the outflow $Q$ over a measured time interval $t$.
6. **Replication:** The test is repeated for **3 to 5 different head values** ($h$) to obtain an averaged $k$ and check consistency.
7. **Temperature Correction:** The viscosity of water changes with temperature, so $k$ is corrected to a standard temperature of **20°C** for comparison.
8. **Reporting:** Final value of $k_{20}$ is reported with units (**cm/s**).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear almost verbatim in KTU university exams.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $k = \dfrac{Q \cdot L}{A \cdot h \cdot t}$ | Primary working formula for constant head test | cm/s |
| 2 | $Q = V$ | Volume of water collected in time $t$ | cm³ or mL |
| 3 | $i = \dfrac{h}{L}$ | Hydraulic gradient (dimensionless) | — |
| 4 | $A = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^{2}}{4}$ | Cross-sectional area of cylindrical specimen | cm² |
| 5 | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A \cdot t}$ | Discharge velocity from measurements | cm/s |
| 6 | $k_{20} = k_{T} \cdot \dfrac{\mu_{T}}{\mu_{20}}$ | Temperature correction to 20°C (using viscosity ratio) | cm/s |
| 7 | $k_{20} = k_{T} \cdot \dfrac{\eta_{T}}{\eta_{20}}$ | Alternative notation with $\eta$ (viscosity) | cm/s |
| 8 | $q = k \cdot i \cdot A$ | Total discharge (Darcy's law in volumetric form) | cm³/s |
| 9 | $R_{e} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v \cdot d_{10}}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number for flow validity check | dimensionless |

**Where:**
- $Q$ = volume of water collected in time $t$ (**mL** or **cm³**)
- $L$ = length of soil specimen (**cm**)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area of specimen (**cm²**)
- $h$ = constant head difference (**cm**)
- $t$ = duration of collection (**seconds**)
- $\mu_{T}, \mu_{20}$ = dynamic viscosity of water at test temperature $T$ and at 20°C respectively
- $d_{10}$ = effective grain size (10% finer)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Convention:** Always report $k$ in **cm/s** unless otherwise specified. Many Indian textbooks (Punmia, Murthy, Arora) use cm/s, and KTU follows this.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Earthen Dam Design:** Engineers need $k$ to estimate seepage losses through dam foundations and embankments.
- **Dewatering of Excavations:** Pumping rates from construction pits depend directly on soil permeability.
- **Landfill Liners:** Compacted clay liners are designed to have very low $k$ ($< 10^{-7}$ cm/s) to prevent leachate migration.
- **Drainage and Filter Design:** French drains and agricultural drainage systems rely on permeability data.
- **Groundwater Recharge Studies:** Aquifer characterization uses $k$ to predict flow rates.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATION & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Working Formula $k = \dfrac{QL}{Aht}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favorite:** This derivation is asked in **Part B (14 marks)** almost every academic year. You **must** be able to reproduce it from first principles on the answer sheet.

### Step 1: Start with Darcy's Law

Darcy experimentally established that the discharge velocity $v$ through a saturated soil column is proportional to the hydraulic gradient $i$:

$$v \propto i$$

Introducing the proportionality constant $k$ (coefficient of permeability):

$$v = k \cdot i \quad \text{...(Equation 1)}$$

This is **Darcy's Law** in differential form, valid for **laminar, one-dimensional, steady-state flow** through saturated soil.

### Step 2: Express Discharge Velocity in Volumetric Terms

The **discharge velocity** is defined as the volume of water flowing per unit cross-sectional area per unit time:

$$v = \frac{Q}{A \cdot t} \quad \text{...(Equation 2)}$$

Where:
- $Q$ = total volume of water collected (**cm³** or **mL**)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area of soil specimen (**cm²**)
- $t$ = time duration of flow collection (**seconds**)

### Step 3: Express Hydraulic Gradient in Measurable Quantities

The hydraulic gradient $i$ is the **head loss per unit length of flow path**:

$$i = \frac{h}{L} \quad \text{...(Equation 3)}$$

Where:
- $h$ = constant head difference causing flow (**cm** of water)
- $L$ = length of soil specimen along the flow direction (**cm**)

### Step 4: Substitute (2) and (3) into (1)

Substituting Equation 2 and Equation 3 into Equation 1:

$$\frac{Q}{A \cdot t} = k \cdot \frac{h}{L}$$

### Step 5: Algebraic Rearrangement

Multiplying both sides by $A \cdot t$:

$$Q = k \cdot \frac{h}{L} \cdot A \cdot t$$

Now solving for $k$ by dividing both sides by $\dfrac{A \cdot h \cdot t}{L}$:

$$k = \frac{Q \cdot L}{A \cdot h \cdot t} \quad \text{...(FINAL WORKING FORMULA)}$$

$$\boxed{k = \frac{Q \cdot L}{A \cdot h \cdot t}}$$

### Step 6: Sanity Check on Units

Let us verify dimensional consistency:

$$\frac{Q \cdot L}{A \cdot h \cdot t} \rightarrow \frac{[\text{cm}^{3}] \cdot [\text{cm}]}{[\text{cm}^{2}] \cdot [\text{cm}] \cdot [\text{s}]} = \frac{\text{cm}^{4}}{\text{cm}^{3} \cdot \text{s}} = \frac{\text{cm}}{\text{s}}$$

✅ The units collapse to **cm/s**, which is the correct unit for $k$. The derivation is dimensionally consistent.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Worked Numerical Example

> [!TIP]
> **Worked Example (KTU Model Question):**

**Given Data:**

| Quantity | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Length of specimen | $L$ | **15.0 cm** |
| Diameter of specimen | $d$ | **10.0 cm** |
| Constant head | $h$ | **30.0 cm** |
| Volume of water collected | $Q$ | **450 mL** |
| Time of collection | $t$ | **120 s** |
| Water temperature | $T$ | **27 °C** |

**Find:** Coefficient of permeability at 27°C and corrected to 20°C.

---

### Step A: Compute the Cross-Sectional Area

$$A = \frac{\pi \cdot d^{2}}{4}$$

Substituting $d = 10.0$ cm:

$$A = \frac{\pi \cdot (10.0)^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 100}{4} = 25\pi \;\;\text{cm}^{2}$$

$$A = 78.54 \;\;\text{cm}^{2}$$

---

### Step B: Apply the Working Formula (Uncorrected $k$)

$$k = \frac{Q \cdot L}{A \cdot h \cdot t}$$

Substituting values:

$$k = \frac{450 \times 15.0}{78.54 \times 30.0 \times 120}$$

Numerator:

$$450 \times 15.0 = 6750 \;\;\text{cm}^{4}$$

Denominator:

$$78.54 \times 30.0 \times 120 = 78.54 \times 3600 = 282{,}744 \;\;\text{cm}^{3} \cdot \text{s}$$

Therefore:

$$k = \frac{6750}{282{,}744}$$

$$\boxed{k_{27} = 0.02387 \;\;\text{cm/s}}$$

---

### Step C: Apply Temperature Correction to 20°C

The viscosity correction is applied using:

$$k_{20} = k_{T} \cdot \frac{\mu_{T}}{\mu_{20}}$$

Where:
- $\mu_{27} \approx 0.853 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s
- $\mu_{20} = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s

$$k_{20} = 0.02387 \times \frac{0.853 \times 10^{-3}}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$k_{20} = 0.02387 \times 0.8513$$

$$\boxed{k_{20} = 0.02032 \;\;\text{cm/s}}$$

---

### Step D: Interpretation

Since $k_{20} = 0.02032$ cm/s, this soil classifies as a **clean medium-to-coarse sand** (typical $k$ range for such soils: $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-3}$ cm/s). The soil has **good drainage characteristics** and is unsuitable for use as a clay liner.

---

## 3.3 Common Sources of Error (For Lab Records)

1. **Incomplete saturation** → underestimates $k$
2. **Air bubbles** in inlet/outlet tubes → erratic readings
3. **Channeling along mould walls** → overestimates $k$
4. **Head fluctuations** due to inadequate overflow → invalidates constant-head assumption
5. **Temperature not recorded** → prevents viscosity correction

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are purely alphanumeric, prefixed with letters, and labels contain no markdown formatting tags. The diagram below represents a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** of the constant head permeameter system, mapping all physical and process interactions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Experiment Start]) --> Prep1[Soil Specimen Preparation]
    Prep1 --> Prep2[Oven Dry and Weigh Sample]
    Prep2 --> Prep3[Compact in Permeameter Mould]
    Prep3 --> Sat1[Saturation from Bottom Up]
    Sat1 --> Sat2[Remove Air Voids with De aired Water]
    Sat2 --> Sat3[Verify Full Saturation]
    Sat3 --> Setup1[Set Up Constant Head Tank]
    Setup1 --> Setup2[Adjust Overflow to Maintain Constant h]
    Setup2 --> Setup3[Connect Inlet to Top of Specimen]
    Setup3 --> Flow1[Open Inlet Valve]
    Flow1 --> Flow2[Allow Steady State Flow]
    Flow2 --> Flow3[Wait for Constant Discharge Rate]
    Flow3 --> Meas1[Position Measuring Cylinder at Outlet]
    Meas1 --> Meas2[Start Stopwatch Simultaneously]
    Meas2 --> Meas3[Collect Water Volume Q over Time t]
    Meas3 --> Record1[Record Q in mL and t in seconds]
    Record1 --> Repeat1{Repeat for Different h Values}
    Repeat1 -->|Yes| Setup2
    Repeat1 -->|No| Calc1[Compute Area A equals pi d squared over 4]
    Calc1 --> Calc2[Apply k equals Q L over A h t]
    Calc2 --> Calc3[Record Water Temperature T]
    Calc3 --> Calc4[Apply Viscosity Correction to 20 C]
    Calc4 --> Report1[Report Final k20 in cm per s]
    Report1 --> End([Experiment Complete])

    style Start fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    style End fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    style Calc4 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    style Report1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    style Flow2 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#8B8B00,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Equipment & Apparatus Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WT[WATER TANK] -->|constant supply| CHT[CONSTANT HEAD TANK WITH OVERFLOW]
    CHT -->|inlet pipe| SP[SOIL SPECIMEN IN CYLINDRICAL MOULD]
    SP -->|perforated base| COL[COLLECTION CHAMBER]
    COL -->|outlet spout| MC[MEASURING CYLINDER]
    SW[STOPWATCH] -.->|timing| MC
    TM[THERMOMETER] -.->|temperature check| WT

    style WT fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B
    style CHT fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000
    style SP fill:#DEB887,stroke:#8B4513
    style COL fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#696969
    style MC fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400
    style SW fill:#FFFFE0,stroke:#808000
    style TM fill:#FFC0CB,stroke:#8B0000
```

## 4.3 Formula Dependency Graph

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DL[DARCY LAW v equals k i] --> E1[Equation 1]
    E1 --> E2[Substitute v equals Q over A t]
    E2 --> E3[Substitute i equals h over L]
    E3 --> E4[Derive k equals Q L over A h t]
    E4 --> WC[Apply Temperature Correction]
    WC --> FR[Final k20 reported in cm per s]

    AREA[Area equals pi d squared over 4] --> E4
    VIS[Viscosity Ratio mu T over mu 20] --> WC

    style DL fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    style E4 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    style FR fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State Darcy's Law and define the coefficient of permeability. Why is the constant head test preferred for coarse-grained soils?**

**Model Answer:**

**Darcy's Law (1856):** For laminar, one-dimensional, steady-state flow of water through a saturated porous medium, the discharge velocity is directly proportional to the hydraulic gradient.

$$v = k \cdot i$$

Where $v$ is the discharge velocity (**cm/s**), $k$ is the **coefficient of permeability** (**cm/s**), and $i$ is the **dimensionless hydraulic gradient** defined as $i = h/L$.

**Definition of $k$:** The coefficient of permeability is defined as the discharge velocity of water through the soil under a unit hydraulic gradient, at a standard temperature of 20°C.

**Why constant head for coarse soils:**
1. Coarse soils have **high $k$** values ($10^{-2}$ to $10^{-5}$ cm/s), producing large outflow volumes in short times, easily measurable volumetrically.
2. With a constant head, the discharge remains steady, allowing **accurate time-volume measurements**.
3. In a variable head test, the rapid flow would cause the head to drop too quickly for reliable measurement in coarse soils.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List the apparatus required for the constant head permeability test and state the formula used. What is the significance of the temperature correction?**

**Model Answer:**

**Apparatus Required:**
1. Constant head permeameter (with cylindrical mould, perforated base, and top cap)
2. Constant head tank with overflow arrangement
3. Measuring cylinder (100 mL or 250 mL capacity)
4. Stopwatch
5. Thermometer
6. De-aired water supply
7. Compaction equipment (tamper, vibrator)
8. Balance and oven for moisture content

**Formula Used:**

$$k = \frac{Q \cdot L}{A \cdot h \cdot t}$$

**Significance of Temperature Correction:**
- The viscosity of water **decreases** with increasing temperature, which **increases** the measured $k$.
- To enable comparison between tests performed at different temperatures, results are corrected to a standard temperature of **20°C** using:

$$k_{20} = k_{T} \cdot \frac{\mu_{T}}{\mu_{20}}$$

- Without this correction, results from summer and winter tests would not be comparable, and design values would be inconsistent.

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Derive the formula $k = \dfrac{QL}{Aht}$ for the coefficient of permeability using Darcy's Law. A constant head permeameter test on a sandy soil gave the following observations:**

| S.No. | Head $h$ (cm) | Volume $Q$ (mL) | Time $t$ (s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 20 | 320 | 60 |
| 2 | 30 | 480 | 60 |
| 3 | 40 | 640 | 60 |

**Length of specimen $L = 12$ cm; Diameter $d = 10$ cm; Test temperature $T = 25°C$.**

**Calculate the coefficient of permeability at 25°C and correct it to 20°C.** ($\mu_{25}/\mu_{20} \approx 0.890$)

---

#### Part (a): Derivation of Formula (7 Marks)

The derivation is identical to that shown in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The student is expected to:

1. State Darcy's Law: $v = k \cdot i$ (1 mark)
2. Express $v = Q/(A \cdot t)$ (1 mark)
3. Express $i = h/L$ (1 mark)
4. Substitute and rearrange: $Q/(A \cdot t) = k \cdot (h/L)$ (1 mark)
5. Solve for $k$: $k = QL/(Aht)$ (1 mark)
6. State units and assumptions (laminar flow, saturated soil, constant $T$) (1 mark)
7. Note validity limit (Reynolds number < 1–10) (1 mark)

---

#### Part (b): Numerical Calculation (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Compute cross-sectional area $A$**

$$A = \frac{\pi \cdot d^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot (10)^{2}}{4} = 78.54 \;\;\text{cm}^{2}$$

**Step 2: Calculate $k$ for each reading**

**Reading 1:** $h = 20$ cm, $Q = 320$ mL, $t = 60$ s

$$k_{1} = \frac{320 \times 12}{78.54 \times 20 \times 60} = \frac{3840}{94{,}248} = 0.04074 \;\;\text{cm/s}$$

**Reading 2:** $h = 30$ cm, $Q = 480$ mL, $t = 60$ s

$$k_{2} = \frac{480 \times 12}{78.54 \times 30 \times 60} = \frac{5760}{141{,}372} = 0.04074 \;\;\text{cm/s}$$

**Reading 3:** $h = 40$ cm, $Q = 640$ mL, $t = 60$ s

$$k_{3} = \frac{640 \times 12}{78.54 \times 40 \times 60} = \frac{7680}{188{,}496} = 0.04074 \;\;\text{cm/s}$$

**Step 3: Average $k$ at 25°C**

$$k_{25} = \frac{k_{1} + k_{2} + k_{3}}{3} = \frac{0.04074 + 0.04074 + 0.04074}{3} = 0.04074 \;\;\text{cm/s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The identical values of $k$ for all three readings confirm **linear Darcy's law behavior** (i.e., $k$ is independent of $h$). This is the ideal expected outcome in a well-conducted test.

**Step 4: Apply Temperature Correction to 20°C**

$$k_{20} = k_{25} \cdot \frac{\mu_{25}}{\mu_{20}} = 0.04074 \times 0.890$$

$$\boxed{k_{20} = 0.0363 \;\;\text{cm/s}}$$

**Step 5: Interpretation**

The corrected permeability $k_{20} = 0.0363$ cm/s indicates the soil is a **medium-to-coarse sand** with **good drainage characteristics**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) (7 Marks)** Describe the **constant head permeability test** in detail. Include the apparatus, procedure, observations, and calculations. State the conditions under which this test is preferred over the variable head test.

**(b) (7 Marks)** A constant head permeameter has a specimen of length **20 cm** and cross-sectional area of **50 cm²**. With a constant head of **50 cm** of water, **500 mL** of water is collected in **100 seconds**. Calculate the coefficient of permeability. If the test was conducted at **30°C**, find the corrected permeability at **20°C**. Given: $\mu_{30}/\mu_{20} = 0.798$.

---

#### Part (a): Detailed Description of Constant Head Test (7 Marks)

**Apparatus (2 marks):**
- Constant head permeameter with mould
- Constant head tank with overflow
- Measuring cylinder, stopwatch, thermometer
- De-aired water, compaction tools

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. Oven-dry the soil and compact into the mould in layers to a known density.
2. Saturate the specimen from below using de-aired water for at least 24 hours.
3. Assemble the permeameter and connect to the constant head tank.
4. Adjust the overflow until a constant head $h$ is established.
5. Allow water to flow until a steady discharge rate is observed.
6. Collect outflow in measuring cylinder; record volume $Q$ and time $t$ simultaneously.
7. Repeat for at least three different head values.
8. Record the water temperature.

**Conditions for Preference (2 marks):**
- Used for **coarse-grained soils** (sands, gravels) where $k > 10^{-5}$ cm/s.
- The high permeability produces a measurable flow in a reasonable time.
- The constant head ensures steady discharge, which is critical for accurate volumetric measurement in such soils.

---

#### Part (b): Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $L = 20$ cm
- $A = 50$ cm²
- $h = 50$ cm
- $Q = 500$ mL = 500 cm³
- $t = 100$ s
- $T = 30$°C, $\mu_{30}/\mu_{20} = 0.798$

**Step 1: Apply the formula**

$$k_{30} = \frac{Q \cdot L}{A \cdot h \cdot t} = \frac{500 \times 20}{50 \times 50 \times 100}$$

$$k_{30} = \frac{10{,}000}{250{,}000} = 0.04 \;\;\text{cm/s}$$

**Step 2: Apply temperature correction**

$$k_{20} = k_{30} \cdot \frac{\mu_{30}}{\mu_{20}} = 0.04 \times 0.798$$

$$\boxed{k_{20} = 0.0319 \;\;\text{cm/s}}$$

**Step 3: Comment on soil type**

The value $k_{20} = 0.0319$ cm/s corresponds to a **medium sand** with good drainage, suitable for use as **drainage backfill** but **not suitable** for an impervious core in an earth dam.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
