> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks (KTU Board Examiner's Observations):**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (–2 marks):** Mixing mL and cm³ (they are equal numerically for water, but students often write Q in L without converting). KTU expects $Q$ in **cm³** when using $A$ in cm² and $L$, $h$ in cm.
> 2. **Missing Temperature Correction (–2 marks):** Reporting $k$ at test temperature without correcting to 20°C. This is the **most common omission** flagged in KTU valuation keys.
> 3. **Skipping the Derivation (–3 to –5 marks):** In 14-mark questions, simply writing the formula $k = QL/Aht$ without derivation is treated as an incomplete answer. The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests **CO2: Application of scientific principles**.
> 4. **Not Computing Area Separately (–1 mark):** Forgetting to show $A = \pi d^{2}/4$ calculation.
> 5. **Using the Wrong Test (–2 marks):** Recommending constant head for fine soils in viva/questions.
> 6. **Not Stating Reynolds Number Limit (–1 mark):** Failing to mention the laminar flow assumption in the derivation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ The **constant head test** is the standard method for determining permeability of **coarse-grained soils** (sands and gravels).
- ✅ The governing principle is **Darcy's Law**: $v = k \cdot i$, valid for **laminar, one-dimensional, steady-state, saturated flow**.
- ✅ The **primary working formula** is $k = \dfrac{Q \cdot L}{A \cdot h \cdot t}$ with units of **cm/s**.
- ✅ The **cross-sectional area** of a cylindrical specimen is $A = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^{2}}{4}$.
- ✅ The **hydraulic gradient** in this test is $i = \dfrac{h}{L}$, where $h$ is the constant head maintained.
- ✅ A **steady overflow** at the top of the inlet tank is essential to maintain $h$ = constant.
- ✅ **Specimen saturation from below** is critical to expel air voids — trapped air drastically reduces measured $k$.
- ✅ **Temperature correction to 20°C** is mandatory: $k_{20} = k_{T} \cdot \dfrac{\mu_{T}}{\mu_{20}}$.
- ✅ Darcy's law is **valid for $R_{e} < 1$ to $10$** — for highly coarse gravels, results may be non-Darcian.
- ✅ **Typical $k$ ranges:** Gravels $\sim 10^{-1}$ cm/s; Sands $\sim 10^{-2}$ to $10^{-5}$ cm/s; Silts $\sim 10^{-5}$ to $10^{-7}$ cm/s; Clays $\sim 10^{-7}$ to $10^{-9}$ cm/s.
- ✅ For **fine-grained soils**, the **variable head test** is preferred.
- ✅ The test should be repeated for **at least 3 different heads** to verify linear behavior and obtain an averaged $k$.
- ✅ **Don't forget** to convert $Q$ to consistent units (cm³) when using $A$ in cm² and lengths in cm.
- ✅ Always report $k$ with **temperature correction applied** in the final answer block of your KTU answer sheet.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Falling Head Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL408 - SOIL SCIENCE & MECHANICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Falling Head Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Falling Head Permeability Test** is a standard laboratory method used in geotechnical engineering to determine the **coefficient of permeability (k)** of **fine-grained soils** (silts, clays, and fine sands) under conditions of a variable hydraulic head. The test is conducted by allowing water to flow through a saturated soil specimen under a head that decreases with elapsed time, and the discharge is measured indirectly by observing the rate of fall of water in a graduated standpipe connected to the top of the specimen.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** This test is specifically prescribed for soils with low permeability (typically $k < 10^{-3} \text{ cm/sec}$). The constant head test is unsuitable for such soils because the discharge volume is too small to be measured accurately within a practical time interval.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a hollow, transparent straw (the **standpipe**) filled with water and pressed into a wet, compacted soil column. Now poke a tiny pinhole at the bottom of the straw, inside the soil. As water leaks out of the straw by seeping through the soil, the water level inside the straw slowly drops. 

- If the soil is loose and very permeable (like **gravel**), the water level falls almost instantly — this is why we use a *constant* head test for coarse soils.
- If the soil is dense and tightly packed (like **clay**), the water level falls very slowly, almost imperceptibly. By carefully timing how fast the water level drops, we can "reverse-engineer" how resistant the soil is to water flow.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** The Falling Head Test is essentially a stopwatch experiment. We measure how *fast* a thin column of water drains through a soil sample. The slower it drains, the less permeable the soil is.

### 1.3 Governing Standard

The test is conducted as per **IS 2720 (Part 17):1986** (Reaffirmed 2002) — *"Determination of Permeability of Soils by Falling Head Method."* The standard temperature for reporting permeability is **27 °C (standard room temperature)**, and results are often corrected to **20 °C** using viscosity corrections.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- Standard reference temperature for water viscosity: **20 °C** (or **27 °C** per IS 2720)
- Dynamic viscosity of water at 20 °C: $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$
- Permeability is conventionally expressed in **cm/sec** in Indian geotechnical practice.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Hook for Board Exams:** 
> - "**F**alling = **F**ine" — FHT for **Fine** soils. 
> - "**C**onstant = **C**oarse" — CHT for **C**oarse soils.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — The "Why" Behind the Test

The Falling Head Test is fundamentally a **time-rate experiment** that applies **Darcy's Law** to a transient (non-steady) flow condition. Unlike the Constant Head Test, where the hydraulic head is maintained steady, here the head is allowed to decay, and we measure *time* rather than *volume* of discharge.

**The core logic flow:**

- Water flows through a saturated soil column of length $L$ and cross-section $A$.
- A thin graduated tube (standpipe) of cross-section $a$ is fixed on top.
- At any instant $t$, the head causing flow is $h(t)$, which is decreasing.
- A small change in head $dh$ corresponds to a small change in time $dt$.
- Using Darcy's Law in differential form and integrating over the head drop, we obtain the coefficient of permeability $k$.

### 2.2 Assumptions of the Test

For the theoretical derivation to hold, the following conditions must be satisfied:

1. The soil specimen is **fully saturated** before the test begins.
2. **Darcy's Law is valid** for the given flow regime (laminar flow).
3. Flow is **one-dimensional** along the length of the specimen.
4. The soil structure does not change during the test (no migration of fines, no consolidation).
5. The temperature is **constant** during the test, or a correction factor is applied.
6. The cross-sectional areas of the standpipe ($a$) and the soil sample ($A$) remain constant.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Examiner Trap:** Students often forget that the test must be run for a long enough duration to ensure the soil reaches a **steady-state flow condition** (saturation) before timing starts. Reading the timing from the very first moment of head drop is a classic mistake.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Units (SI)** | **Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Coefficient of permeability (natural log form) | $k$ | $k = \dfrac{a \cdot L}{A \cdot t} \cdot \ln \left( \dfrac{h_1}{h_2} \right)$ | m/s or cm/s | Continuous flow, head drop from $h_1$ to $h_2$ |
| Coefficient of permeability (log₁₀ form) | $k$ | $k = \dfrac{2.303 \cdot a \cdot L}{A \cdot t} \cdot \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{h_1}{h_2} \right)$ | m/s or cm/s | Most commonly used in lab calculations |
| Velocity of flow (Darcy's Law) | $v$ | $v = k \cdot i$ | m/s | $i$ is the hydraulic gradient |
| Hydraulic gradient | $i$ | $i = \dfrac{h}{L}$ | dimensionless | $h$ is the head at any instant |
| Permeability at 27 °C | $k_{27}$ | $k_{27} = k_T \cdot \dfrac{\mu_T}{\mu_{27}}$ | m/s | Temperature correction per IS 2720 |
| Standard cross-section area of standpipe | $a$ | $a = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^2}{4}$ | $\text{m}^2$ | $d$ is the internal diameter of the standpipe |
| Cross-section of soil specimen | $A$ | $A = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4}$ | $\text{m}^2$ | $D$ is the internal diameter of the permeameter mould |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Note on Units:** Always convert all linear dimensions to the **same unit system** (preferably cm) before substitution, since the final answer is conventionally reported in **cm/sec** in Indian KTU examinations.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Falling Head Test is indispensable in:

- **Design of earthen dams and levees** — estimating seepage losses through clay cores.
- **Slope stability analysis** — computing pore pressure dissipation in fine-grained slopes.
- **Settlement predictions of clay layers** — permeability governs the time-rate of consolidation.
- **Landfill liner design** — assessing the effectiveness of compacted clay liners (CCLs).
- **Groundwater hydrology** — determining vertical hydraulic conductivity of aquitards.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Insight:** The value of $k$ for clays determined by the falling head test is often used in **Terzaghi's 1-D Consolidation Theory** to estimate the coefficient of consolidation $c_v$ via the relationship $c_v = \dfrac{k \cdot M}{\gamma_w}$, where $M$ is the constrained modulus.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Falling Head Permeability Equation

**Step 1: Definition of the physical setup**

Consider a cylindrical soil specimen of:
- Length: $L$ (along the direction of flow)
- Cross-sectional area: $A$

A vertical **standpipe** (graduated tube) of:
- Internal cross-sectional area: $a$ (where $a \ll A$)
- Initial water level: $h_1$ above the outlet

Water flows **downward** through the soil, and the level in the standpipe falls over time.

**Step 2: Establish the head at any instant**

At any arbitrary time $t$, the hydraulic head driving the flow is the difference in water level between the standpipe and the outlet. Let this be $h$ (which is a function of time $t$).

The hydraulic gradient at this instant is:

$$i = \frac{h}{L}$$

**Step 3: Apply Darcy's Law for instantaneous discharge**

The instantaneous rate of discharge $Q$ (volume per unit time) through the soil specimen is given by Darcy's Law:

$$Q = k \cdot i \cdot A = k \cdot \frac{h}{L} \cdot A$$

Therefore, the volume of water flowing through the soil in an infinitesimally small time interval $dt$ is:

$$dV = Q \cdot dt = k \cdot \frac{h}{L} \cdot A \cdot dt$$

**Step 4: Express $dV$ from the standpipe geometry**

In the same time interval $dt$, the water level in the standpipe drops by an amount $dh$ (which is negative since $h$ decreases). The volume of water leaving the standpipe is:

$$dV = -a \cdot dh$$

The negative sign is essential: as $h$ decreases ($dh < 0$), $-dh > 0$, giving a positive volume of outflow.

**Step 5: Equate the two expressions for $dV$**

Since the water leaving the standpipe must equal the water flowing through the soil (continuity principle):

$$-a \cdot dh = k \cdot \frac{h}{L} \cdot A \cdot dt$$

**Step 6: Separate the variables**

Rearranging the equation so that all terms involving $h$ are on one side and $t$ on the other:

$$-a \cdot \frac{dh}{h} = \frac{k \cdot A}{L} \cdot dt$$

**Step 7: Set up the limits of integration**

- At time $t = 0$, the head is $h = h_1$ (initial head).
- At time $t = t$, the head has dropped to $h = h_2$ (final head).
- Therefore, $h$ goes from $h_1$ to $h_2$, and $t$ goes from $0$ to $t$.

Integrating both sides:

$$\int_{h_1}^{h_2} -a \cdot \frac{dh}{h} = \int_{0}^{t} \frac{k \cdot A}{L} \cdot dt$$

**Step 8: Perform the integration**

The left side gives:

$$-a \cdot \left[ \ln h \right]_{h_1}^{h_2} = -a \cdot \left( \ln h_2 - \ln h_1 \right) = -a \cdot \ln \left( \frac{h_2}{h_1} \right) = a \cdot \ln \left( \frac{h_1}{h_2} \right)$$

The right side gives:

$$\frac{k \cdot A}{L} \cdot \left[ t \right]_{0}^{t} = \frac{k \cdot A \cdot t}{L}$$

**Step 9: Equate and solve for $k$**

$$a \cdot \ln \left( \frac{h_1}{h_2} \right) = \frac{k \cdot A \cdot t}{L}$$

Solving for $k$:

$$k = \frac{a \cdot L}{A \cdot t} \cdot \ln \left( \frac{h_1}{h_2} \right)$$

**Step 10: Convert to common logarithm (base 10)**

Since laboratory calculators typically use $\log_{10}$ instead of $\ln$, we apply the conversion $\ln x = 2.303 \cdot \log_{10} x$:

$$k = \frac{2.303 \cdot a \cdot L}{A \cdot t} \cdot \log_{10} \left( \frac{h_1}{h_2} \right)$$

This is the **standard working equation** for the Falling Head Permeability Test.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem Statement:**

A falling head permeability test was conducted on a fine-grained soil specimen. The following data are given:
- Length of soil specimen: $L = 12 \text{ cm}$
- Diameter of soil specimen: $D = 10 \text{ cm}$
- Diameter of standpipe: $d = 1.0 \text{ cm}$
- Initial head: $h_1 = 100 \text{ cm}$
- Final head: $h_2 = 40 \text{ cm}$
- Time for head drop: $t = 240 \text{ seconds}$

**Determine the coefficient of permeability $k$ in cm/sec.**

**Solution — Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1: Calculate cross-sectional areas**

$$A = \frac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot (10)^2}{4} = 78.54 \text{ cm}^2$$

$$a = \frac{\pi \cdot d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot (1.0)^2}{4} = 0.7854 \text{ cm}^2$$

**Step 2: Compute the head ratio**

$$\frac{h_1}{h_2} = \frac{100}{40} = 2.5$$

**Step 3: Compute $\log_{10}$ of the head ratio**

$$\log_{10}(2.5) = 0.3979$$

**Step 4: Substitute into the falling head equation**

$$k = \frac{2.303 \cdot a \cdot L}{A \cdot t} \cdot \log_{10} \left( \frac{h_1}{h_2} \right)$$

$$k = \frac{2.303 \times 0.7854 \times 12}{78.54 \times 240} \times 0.3979$$

**Step 5: Evaluate the numerator and denominator**

$$\text{Numerator} = 2.303 \times 0.7854 \times 12 \times 0.3979 = 8.635$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 78.54 \times 240 = 18849.6$$

**Step 6: Final calculation**

$$k = \frac{8.635}{18849.6} = 4.58 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm/sec}$$

**Step 7: Comment on soil type**

> [!NOTE]
> Since $k = 4.58 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm/sec}$ falls in the range of **$10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ cm/sec**, the soil is classified as a **fine sand to silty sand** as per Terzaghi's classification. This value is consistent with the use of the falling head test.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Falling Head vs. Constant Head Test

| **Parameter** | **Falling Head Test (FHT)** | **Constant Head Test (CHT)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Suitable soil type** | Fine-grained (silt, clay, fine sand) | Coarse-grained (gravel, sand) |
| **Typical $k$ range** | $k < 10^{-3}$ cm/sec | $k > 10^{-3}$ cm/sec |
| **Quantity measured** | Time for head drop | Volume of discharge |
| **Hydraulic condition** | Variable head | Steady head |
| **Standpipe** | Required (graduated) | Not required |
| **Accuracy for clays** | High | Low (discharge too small) |
| **IS Code reference** | IS 2720 (Part 17) | IS 2720 (Part 36) |
| **Mathematical complexity** | Logarithmic computation | Direct linear computation |
| **Test duration** | Long (hours) | Short (minutes) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of the Falling Head Test Apparatus

The following Mermaid diagram maps the functional architecture and flow interactions of the Falling Head Test setup, illustrating how each component contributes to the permeability measurement.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Constant Head Water Tank] --> B1[Inflow Control Valve]
    B1 --> C1[Graduated Standpipe]
    C1 --> D1[Top Porous Stone]
    D1 --> E1[Compacted Soil Specimen]
    E1 --> F1[Bottom Porous Stone]
    F1 --> G1[Outlet to Collecting Jar]
    E1 --> H1[Overflow Outlet]
    H1 --> I1[Drainage Channel]
    C1 --> J1[Initial Head h1 Marker]
    C1 --> K1[Final Head h2 Marker]
    J1 --> L1[Stopwatch Start at t=0]
    K1 --> M1[Stopwatch Stop at t=T]
    L1 --> N1[Log Sheet Recording]
    M1 --> N1
    N1 --> O1[Apply Formula k equals 2.303 a L over A t times log h1 over h2]
    O1 --> P1[Permeability Coefficient k in cm per sec]
    A2[Permeameter Mould] -.houses.-> E1
    A3[Internal Diameter D] -.defines.-> A2
    A4[Length of Specimen L] -.defines.-> E1
    A5[Standpipe Diameter d] -.defines.-> C1
    P1 --> Q1[Soil Classification per Terzaghi]
    Q1 --> R1[Engineering Design Parameter]
```

### 4.2 Logical Sequence Diagram of Test Procedure

The following Mermaid sequence diagram illustrates the temporal workflow of conducting a falling head test, from setup to final computation.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Tech as Lab Technician
    participant Appr as Apparatus
    participant Soil as Soil Specimen
    participant Watch as Stopwatch
    participant Calc as Calculation Sheet
    Tech->>Appr: Step 1 Assemble permeameter with saturated soil
    Tech->>Appr: Step 2 Connect graduated standpipe on top
    Tech->>Appr: Step 3 Fill standpipe to initial head h1
    Tech->>Watch: Step 4 Start stopwatch at t equals 0
    Watch->>Tech: Time begins
    Appr->>Soil: Water flows through soil under falling head
    Soil->>Appr: Standpipe water level drops
    Tech->>Appr: Step 5 Observe and record head h2 at time t
    Appr->>Tech: Head drops to h2 after time t seconds
    Tech->>Watch: Step 6 Stop stopwatch
    Watch->>Tech: Time t recorded
    Tech->>Calc: Step 7 Measure lengths L and diameters D and d
    Tech->>Calc: Step 8 Substitute values into falling head equation
    Calc->>Tech: Permeability k computed in cm per sec
    Tech->>Appr: Step 9 Repeat test for reliability
    Appr->>Tech: Average of three trials reported
```

### 4.3 Physical Component Layout (Schematic Representation)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the physical layout of components in the falling head permeameter in cross-section view.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ST[STANDPIPE ASSEMBLY]
        direction TB
        SP1[Graduated Tube with cm markings]
        SP2[Water Column of height h above outlet]
        SP3[Air at top of standpipe]
    end
    subgraph PM[PERMEAMETER MOULD ASSEMBLY]
        direction TB
        PM1[Top porous stone saturated]
        PM2[Compacted fine grained soil of length L]
        PM3[Bottom porous stone saturated]
        PM4[Outlet pipe for collected water]
    end
    ST -->|Flow downward through soil| PM
    PM1 --> PM2
    PM2 --> PM3
    PM3 --> PM4
    PM4 --> O1[Water collected in graduated jar]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide:** The standpipe assembly provides the variable hydraulic head, while the permeameter mould contains the soil specimen through which flow occurs. The head difference between the standpipe water level and the outlet level drives the flow, and the rate of head drop is the measurable variable used to compute permeability.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
> 
> **State the principle of the falling head permeability test and name the type of soils for which it is most suitable.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The falling head permeability test is based on the principle of **Darcy's Law** applied under a **variable hydraulic head** condition. Water is allowed to flow through a saturated soil specimen under a head that decreases with time, and the rate of fall of water level in a graduated standpipe is measured. The coefficient of permeability is computed using the equation:

$$k = \frac{2.303 \cdot a \cdot L}{A \cdot t} \cdot \log_{10} \left( \frac{h_1}{h_2} \right)$$

The test is most suitable for **fine-grained soils** such as **silts and clays** having low coefficients of permeability (typically $k < 10^{-3}$ cm/sec), where the discharge volume is too small to be measured accurately in a constant head test.

---

> **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> 
> **List any six apparatus required for conducting the falling head permeability test.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The following apparatus are required:

1. **Permeameter mould** — to contain the soil specimen.
2. **Compaction equipment** — for preparing the soil sample.
3. **Two porous stones** (top and bottom) — to prevent soil particle washout.
4. **Graduated standpipe** — to measure the falling head.
5. **Constant head water reservoir** — to maintain saturation.
6. **Stopwatch** — to record the time for head drop.
7. **Measuring cylinder** — to verify outflow volume (optional).
8. **Thermometer** — for temperature corrections.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – July 2024]`
> 
> **(a)** Describe with a neat sketch the experimental procedure for determining the coefficient of permeability of a fine-grained soil using the falling head permeability test. List the apparatus required. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** Derive the expression for the coefficient of permeability of a soil using the falling head test. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Apparatus Required:**
- Permeameter (cylindrical mould)
- Compacted fine-grained soil specimen
- Top and bottom porous stones
- Graduated standpipe (internal diameter $d$, cross-section $a$)
- Constant head water tank
- Stopwatch
- Measuring scale
- Thermometer

**Test Procedure:**

1. **Specimen Preparation:** Take a representative fine-grained soil sample. Compact it to the required dry density inside the permeameter mould using the standard Proctor or modified Proctor method. Record the length $L$ and diameter $D$ of the specimen.

2. **Saturation:** Saturate the soil specimen from bottom to top to expel entrapped air. Allow saturation to continue until a steady flow condition is achieved.

3. **Assembly:** Place the saturated soil between two porous stones inside the permeameter mould. Connect the graduated standpipe vertically to the top of the mould. Ensure all connections are watertight.

4. **Initial Reading:** Fill the standpipe with water up to a convenient initial head $h_1$ (typically 100 cm). Note that the outlet at the bottom is open to the atmosphere.

5. **Timing Operation:** Start the stopwatch simultaneously when the water level begins to fall. Note the time $t$ taken for the water level to drop from $h_1$ to a chosen lower head $h_2$ (typically 40 cm).

6. **Repetition:** Repeat the procedure for at least three different initial heads or for the same head drop multiple times to obtain a reliable average value of $k$.

7. **Recording:** Note the temperature of the water during the test using a thermometer.

8. **Computation:** Calculate $k$ using the derived equation and apply temperature correction to obtain $k$ at the standard temperature of 27 °C.

9. **Reporting:** Report the average coefficient of permeability in **cm/sec** to the appropriate number of significant figures.

#### Model Solution for (b):

The complete derivation was presented in Section 3.1 above. The final expression is:

$$k = \frac{2.303 \cdot a \cdot L}{A \cdot t} \cdot \log_{10} \left( \frac{h_1}{h_2} \right)$$

Where:
- $k$ = coefficient of permeability
- $a$ = cross-sectional area of standpipe
- $A$ = cross-sectional area of soil specimen
- $L$ = length of soil specimen
- $h_1$ = initial head at $t = 0$
- $h_2$ = final head at time $t$
- $t$ = time interval for head drop from $h_1$ to $h_2$

> **End of Question A**

---

> **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – July 2024]`
> 
> **(a)** State Darcy's Law and explain its validity. Compare the constant head and falling head permeability tests. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** A falling head permeability test was conducted on a fine-grained soil specimen with the following data:
> - Length of specimen $L = 15$ cm
> - Diameter of specimen $D = 10$ cm
> - Diameter of standpipe $d = 1.5$ cm
> - Initial head $h_1 = 120$ cm
> - Final head $h_2 = 60$ cm
> - Time for head drop $t = 300$ seconds
> 
> Calculate the coefficient of permeability and classify the soil. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Darcy's Law Statement:**

Darcy's Law states that for laminar flow through a saturated porous medium, the **discharge velocity $v$** is directly proportional to the **hydraulic gradient $i$**.

Mathematically:

$$v = k \cdot i$$

where $k$ is the **coefficient of permeability** (a property of the porous medium and the fluid).

In terms of discharge $Q$:

$$Q = k \cdot i \cdot A$$

where $A$ is the cross-sectional area of flow.

**Validity of Darcy's Law:**

Darcy's Law is valid only for **laminar flow conditions**, which is typically the case for flow through fine-grained soils. The upper limit of validity is governed by the **Reynolds number**, with laminar flow generally existing for $Re < 1$ (some references extend to $Re < 10$). For coarse-grained soils at high gradients, flow may become turbulent, and Darcy's Law fails.

**Comparison Table:**

| **Aspect** | **Constant Head Test** | **Falling Head Test** |
|---|---|---|
| Suitable soil | Coarse-grained | Fine-grained |
| Quantity measured | Volume of water | Time of head drop |
| Head condition | Steady | Variable |
| Standpipe | Not needed | Required |
| Range of $k$ | $10^{-3}$ to $10^0$ cm/sec | $10^{-7}$ to $10^{-3}$ cm/sec |
| Accuracy | High for coarse soils | High for fine soils |

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1: Compute cross-sectional areas**

$$A = \frac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot (10)^2}{4} = 78.54 \text{ cm}^2$$

$$a = \frac{\pi \cdot d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot (1.5)^2}{4} = 1.767 \text{ cm}^2$$

**Step 2: Compute the head ratio**

$$\frac{h_1}{h_2} = \frac{120}{60} = 2.0$$

**Step 3: Compute the logarithm**

$$\log_{10}(2.0) = 0.3010$$

**Step 4: Substitute into the falling head equation**

$$k = \frac{2.303 \cdot a \cdot L}{A \cdot t} \cdot \log_{10} \left( \frac{h_1}{h_2} \right)$$

$$k = \frac{2.303 \times 1.767 \times 15}{78.54 \times 300} \times 0.3010$$

**Step 5: Compute numerator**

$$2.303 \times 1.767 \times 15 \times 0.3010 = 18.37$$

**Step 6: Compute denominator**

$$78.54 \times 300 = 23562$$

**Step 7: Final value**

$$k = \frac{18.37}{23562} = 7.79 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm/sec}$$

**Step 8: Soil Classification**

Since $k = 7.79 \times 10^{-4}$ cm/sec falls in the range $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ cm/sec, the soil is classified as **silty sand / fine sand** as per Terzaghi's permeability classification.

> **End of Question B**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** The most common mistake is mixing cm and mm in the same calculation. All linear dimensions must be in the **same units** (preferably cm) before substitution.
> 2. **Forgetting the 2.303 factor:** Students using $\ln$ instead of $\log_{10}$ in the equation forget the conversion factor, leading to answers off by a factor of **2.303** (significant mark loss).
> 3. **Confusing $a$ and $A$:** The standpipe area $a$ is **small**, and the specimen area $A$ is **large**. Reversing them is a recurring error.
> 4. **Omitting negative sign in derivation:** When equating $-a \, dh = kAh/L \, dt$, the negative sign is **mathematically essential** and must be shown explicitly for full marks.
> 5. **No temperature correction:** When the test temperature deviates from 27 °C, the result must be corrected using the viscosity ratio; students frequently skip this step.
> 6. **No mention of saturation:** Failing to mention pre-saturation of the specimen before timing starts is a common omission that costs method-marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Falling Head Test (FHT)** is used for **fine-grained soils** with $k < 10^{-3}$ cm/sec (silts and clays).
- The test is governed by **IS 2720 (Part 17):1986**.
- It is based on **Darcy's Law** applied under a **variable head** condition.
- The **standard working equation** is $k = \dfrac{2.303 \cdot a \cdot L}{A \cdot t} \cdot \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{h_1}{h_2} \right)$.
- The **standpipe** is a graduated tube of small cross-section $a$ mounted on top of the soil specimen.
- The **soil specimen** has cross-section $A$ and length $L$ (in the direction of flow).
- $h_1$ is the **initial head** at $t = 0$, and $h_2$ is the **head** after time $t$.
- A **negative sign** appears in the differential equation derivation since the head is *decreasing* with time.
- **Pre-saturation** of the specimen is mandatory before starting the stopwatch.
- **Temperature correction** is applied to standardize $k$ at **27 °C** as per IS code.
- Always report $k$ in **cm/sec** in Indian KTU examinations.
- A typical fine sand has $k \approx 10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ cm/sec; silt has $k \approx 10^{-6}$ to $10^{-4}$ cm/sec; clay has $k \approx 10^{-9}$ to $10^{-6}$ cm/sec.
- For **coarse-grained soils**, use the **Constant Head Test (CHT)** instead.
- Darcy's Law is valid only under **laminar flow** conditions (low Reynolds number).
- The derivation integrates Darcy's Law from $h_1$ to $h_2$ and from $0$ to $t$ to obtain the working equation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Shear Parameters by Direct Shear Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL408 - SOIL SCIENCE & MECHANICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Shear Parameters by Direct Shear Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Direct Shear Test (DST)** is a laboratory shear test standardized under **IS 2720 (Part 13) – 1986** used to determine the **shear strength parameters** of soils — specifically the **cohesion (c)** and the **angle of internal friction (φ)**. The test is performed by placing a soil specimen inside a split shear box, applying a controlled **normal load (P_v)** vertically, and then forcing one half of the box to slide relative to the other at a **constant rate of shearing** (typically **1.25 mm/min** for drained conditions) until failure occurs along a pre-defined horizontal plane.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The shear strength of soil is the maximum internal resistance per unit area that the soil mass can offer against failure. By Coulomb's Law, it is given by:
> $$\tau_f = c + \sigma \tan\phi$$
> where **c** = cohesion (kN/m² or kPa) and **φ** = angle of internal friction (degrees).

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a heavy book resting on a flat table. Now place your hand flat on top of the book and try to **push the book sideways** while simultaneously **pressing it down** with your palm. 

- The downward push from your palm is the **normal stress (σ)** — it "clamps" the surfaces together.
- The horizontal push is the **shear stress (τ)** — it tries to slide the book.
- The friction between the book and the table — plus any "stickiness" between them — is the **shear strength**.

If you push harder sideways, the book will eventually slip. The harder you press down, the more force you need to push sideways. This linear relationship between normal force and resisting shear force is exactly what the **Direct Shear Test** measures. Now imagine doing this for soil: the soil grains are the microscopic "bumpy surfaces," and the moisture acts as the "stickiness" (cohesion).

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Apparatus Specs

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Test Specifications (IS 2720 Part 13):**
> - **Shear box size:** 60 mm × 60 mm × 50 mm depth (square) — the most common configuration
> - **Rate of shearing:** **1.25 mm/min** (drained) for sandy soils; **0.5 mm/min** for clays
> - **Minimum 3 specimens** are tested at different normal loads (typically **50 kPa, 100 kPa, 150 kPa**)
> - **Proving ring capacity:** chosen to match the expected peak shear force
> - **Porous stones** placed at top and bottom to allow drainage

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Principle — Mohr-Coulomb Failure Criterion

The direct shear test directly implements the **Mohr-Coulomb failure law** along a single, predetermined plane. Unlike a triaxial test, where failure can occur on any plane, the DST forces failure along the **horizontal plane separating the upper and lower halves** of the shear box.

The mathematical backbone is the **Coulomb Equation**:

$$\tau_f = c + \sigma_n \tan\phi$$

Where:
- **τ_f** = Shear strength at failure (kN/m²)
- **c** = Apparent cohesion (kN/m²) — the y-intercept of the failure envelope
- **σ_n** = Normal stress on the failure plane (kN/m²)
- **φ** = Angle of internal friction (degrees) — measured from the slope of the failure envelope

### 2.2 The Step-by-Step Logic of the Test

1. **Specimen Preparation:** Soil is compacted or remolded into the lower half of the shear box, with the upper half placed on top and locked in position with a fixing pin.
2. **Application of Normal Load:** A vertical load is applied through a loading cap using a dead-weight hanger/lever arm. This produces a constant normal stress, σ = P_v / A.
3. **Removal of Fixing Pin:** The pin holding the two halves together is removed, so that only the friction along the central plane resists horizontal motion.
4. **Application of Shear Force:** A horizontal force P_h is applied at a constant rate using a motorized loading unit, pushing the lower half forward.
5. **Recording Data:** A proving ring measures the shear force; dial gauges record horizontal displacement and vertical (dilation/contraction) movement.
6. **Failure Identification:** The peak shear force on the load-displacement curve is identified as the failure point. (If no peak, use the value at 15% strain or 6 mm displacement.)
7. **Repetition:** Steps 1–6 are repeated for at least **three different normal stresses**.
8. **Plotting:** A graph of **shear stress (τ) vs. normal stress (σ)** is drawn. A best-fit straight line is drawn, whose slope and y-intercept give **tan φ** and **c** respectively.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table for the exam — these are the only formulas you need.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Normal Stress | $\sigma = \dfrac{P_v}{A}$ | kN/m² (kPa) | P_v = vertical load; A = cross-sectional area of box |
| 2 | Shear Stress | $\tau = \dfrac{P_h}{A}$ | kN/m² (kPa) | P_h = horizontal shear force at failure |
| 3 | Proving Ring Calibration | $P_h = C \times d$ | N or kN | C = proving ring constant (N/div); d = dial gauge reading |
| 4 | Coulomb's Equation | $\tau_f = c + \sigma \tan\phi$ | kN/m² | Used to plot the failure envelope |
| 5 | Slope of Failure Line | $\tan\phi = \dfrac{\Delta\tau}{\Delta\sigma}$ | dimensionless | Gives the angle φ |
| 6 | y-intercept | $c = \tau_f - \sigma \tan\phi$ | kN/m² | Gives the cohesion |
| 7 | Box Area (60×60 mm) | $A = 60 \times 60 = 3600$ mm² = $36 \times 10^{-4}$ m² | m² | Use **3600 mm²** in calculations to avoid decimals |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design of earth dams, embankments, and retaining walls** — slope stability analyses rely directly on c and φ.
- **Foundation design** for shallow footings on cohesionless soils uses φ to estimate bearing capacity via Terzaghi's equation.
- **Pavement subgrade design** uses the CBR (California Bearing Ratio) test, whose results correlate with the shear parameters.
- **Tunneling and excavation** in soft clays requires accurate c values for stability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical insight for KTU exams:** The DST is the **fastest, simplest, and most economical** way to obtain c and φ. However, it has limitations — the failure plane is **forced** (not natural), and pore water pressure cannot be measured directly. Hence, it is classified as a **drained test (CD)** and is best suited for **sandy soils**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Failure Envelope from Test Data

Suppose we conduct three DSTs on a soil with normal loads of 50 kPa, 100 kPa, and 150 kPa. The peak shear forces obtained from the proving ring readings are P₁, P₂, and P₃ respectively. We must convert each to shear stress.

**Step 1: Convert proving ring readings to shear force**

For each test, the shear force is the proving ring constant times the dial reading at failure:

$$P_{h,i} = C \times d_i$$

where the subscript **i** denotes the test number (1, 2, 3).

**Step 2: Compute the shear stress for each test**

For a 60 mm × 60 mm box, A = 3600 mm². Converting to m² for SI consistency:

$$A = 3600 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2 = 3.6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

So the shear stress is:

$$\tau_i = \frac{P_{h,i}}{A}$$

**Step 3: Compute normal stress for each test**

The normal stress is the applied vertical load divided by the box area:

$$\sigma_i = \frac{P_{v,i}}{A}$$

**Step 4: Plot τ vs σ and fit a straight line**

Using a graph with σ on the x-axis and τ on the y-axis, plot the three points. A linear regression gives:

$$\tau = c + \sigma \tan\phi$$

**Step 5: Extract c and φ**

From the linear equation $\tau = c + (\tan\phi) \cdot \sigma$:
- **y-intercept (when σ = 0)** = **c**
- **slope** = **tan φ**, so $\phi = \tan^{-1}(\text{slope})$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Lab Question Pattern)

**Given:**
- Shear box: 60 mm × 60 mm
- Proving ring constant: **C = 12 N/div** (i.e., 0.012 kN/div)
- Three normal loads applied: 0.5 kN, 1.0 kN, 1.5 kN
- Dial gauge readings at failure: 90 div, 140 div, 190 div respectively

**Step 1: Convert dial readings to shear forces**

$$P_{h,1} = 12 \times 90 = 1080 \text{ N} = 1.080 \text{ kN}$$

$$P_{h,2} = 12 \times 140 = 1680 \text{ N} = 1.680 \text{ kN}$$

$$P_{h,3} = 12 \times 190 = 2280 \text{ N} = 2.280 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Convert to stresses (Area = 3600 mm² = 3.6 × 10⁻³ m²)**

For test 1:

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{0.5}{3.6 \times 10^{-3}} = 138.89 \text{ kPa}$$

$$\tau_1 = \frac{1.080}{3.6 \times 10^{-3}} = 300.00 \text{ kPa}$$

For test 2:

$$\sigma_2 = \frac{1.0}{3.6 \times 10^{-3}} = 277.78 \text{ kPa}$$

$$\tau_2 = \frac{1.680}{3.6 \times 10^{-3}} = 466.67 \text{ kPa}$$

For test 3:

$$\sigma_3 = \frac{1.5}{3.6 \times 10^{-3}} = 416.67 \text{ kPa}$$

$$\tau_3 = \frac{2.280}{3.6 \times 10^{-3}} = 633.33 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3: Tabulate the data**

| Test No. | Normal Stress σ (kPa) | Shear Stress τ (kPa) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 138.89 | 300.00 |
| 2 | 277.78 | 466.67 |
| 3 | 416.67 | 633.33 |

**Step 4: Fit a straight line**

Using linear regression (or the two-point formula between test 1 and test 3):

$$\tan\phi = \frac{\tau_3 - \tau_1}{\sigma_3 - \sigma_1} = \frac{633.33 - 300.00}{416.67 - 138.89}$$

$$\tan\phi = \frac{333.33}{277.78} = 1.20$$

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}(1.20) = 50.19^{\circ}$$

**Step 5: Calculate c using any test data point**

Using test 1:

$$c = \tau_1 - \sigma_1 \tan\phi = 300.00 - (138.89 \times 1.20)$$

$$c = 300.00 - 166.67 = 133.33 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 6: Final answer**

$$\boxed{c = 133.33 \text{ kPa}, \quad \phi = 50.19^{\circ}}$$

This soil is highly **cohesive and highly frictional** — typical of a dense, well-graded sandy clay.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When Each Test Outcome is Used

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always check if the test is "strain-softening" or "strain-hardening":
> - If the load-displacement curve shows a **clear peak**, use the peak value as failure.
> - If the curve **rises and then plateaus** (typical of loose sand or normally consolidated clay), use the value at **15% strain** (i.e., 6 mm displacement for a 40 mm shearing length).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Direct Shear Apparatus

The following Mermaid block-diagram maps the **functional interconnections** of the apparatus components, since a physical 3D drawing is not feasible in Mermaid.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Vertical Loading Frame] -->|applies P_v via lever arm| B[Loading Cap with Ball Joint]
    B -->|transfers normal stress| C[Upper Half of Shear Box]
    C -->|contains soil specimen| D[Porous Stone - Top]
    D --> E[Soil Specimen 60mm x 60mm]
    E --> F[Porous Stone - Bottom]
    F --> G[Lower Half of Shear Box]
    G -->|rests on| H[Shear Box Base Plate]
    I[Horizontal Loading Jack] -->|applies P_h| J[Loading Plunger to Lower Box]
    J -->|induces shear| G
    K[Proving Ring] -->|measures P_h| J
    L[Dial Gauge 1 - Horizontal] -->|records shear displacement| J
    M[Dial Gauge 2 - Vertical] -->|records dilation or contraction| C
    N[Shear Box Container with Water] -->|provides drained conditions| E
    O[Data Acquisition - Manual Tabulation] -->|records all readings| K
    O --> L
    O --> M
```

### 4.2 Process Flow Diagram — Experimental Procedure

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Step 1: Prepare oven-dried or remolded soil specimen] --> S2[Step 2: Assemble shear box with porous stones and loading pad]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Apply first normal load P_v1 to specimen]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Remove fixing pin separating upper and lower box]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Apply horizontal shear load at 1.25 mm/min constant rate]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Record proving ring reading and horizontal displacement continuously]
    S6 --> S7{Peak or 15 percent strain reached}
    S7 -->|Yes| S8[Step 8: Note the failure shear force and displacement]
    S7 -->|No| S5
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Repeat test with increased normal load P_v2 and P_v3]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10: Calculate normal stress sigma and shear stress tau for all three tests]
    S10 --> S11[Step 11: Plot tau versus sigma graph]
    S11 --> S12[Step 12: Draw best fit line to obtain c and phi]
```

### 4.3 Typical Mohr-Coulomb Failure Envelope (Schematic)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Origin sigma equals 0] -->|X axis| B[Normal Stress sigma in kPa]
    A -->|Y axis| C[Shear Stress tau in kPa]
    D[Test 1: low sigma low tau] -->|line of best fit| E[Test 2: medium sigma medium tau]
    E -->|line of best fit| F[Test 3: high sigma high tau]
    G[Y intercept equals cohesion c] -->|located on tau axis| A
    H[Angle of slope equals phi] -->|measured from horizontal| F
```

### 4.4 Schematic Components Layout

| Component Label | Function in the Apparatus |
|:---|:---|
| **A** | Vertical loading plunger (sits above loading cap) |
| **B** | Proving ring (calibrated spring for shear force measurement) |
| **C** | Upper shear box half (remains stationary horizontally) |
| **D** | Lower shear box half (slides on roller bearings) |
| **E** | Soil specimen confined in the box |
| **F** | Porous stones (top and bottom) — allow drainage |
| **G** | Shear box base plate (translates horizontally) |
| **H** | Dial gauge for horizontal displacement |
| **I** | Dial gauge for vertical displacement (dilation) |
| **J** | Loading hanger with dead weights (normal load) |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State Coulomb's equation for shear strength of soil and explain the terms involved.**

**Model Answer:**
Coulomb's equation for shear strength of soil is:
$$\tau_f = c + \sigma \tan\phi$$
where **τ_f** is the shear strength at failure (kN/m²), **c** is the apparent cohesion of soil (kN/m²), **σ** is the normal stress on the failure plane (kN/m²), and **φ** is the angle of internal friction (degrees). The first term (c) represents the inherent stickiness of soil particles, while the second term (σ tan φ) represents the frictional resistance that increases linearly with normal stress. *(3 marks)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Why is the direct shear test called a "drained" test? When is it preferred over the triaxial test?**

**Model Answer:**
The direct shear test is called a "drained" test because the soil specimen is allowed to consolidate and drain freely through the porous stones during shearing, and the test is conducted slowly (rate = 1.25 mm/min) so that excess pore water pressure dissipates. It is preferred over the triaxial test when: (i) quick and approximate values of c and φ are required, (ii) the soil is sandy or cohesionless, and (iii) simplicity and economy of testing are desired, as the apparatus is cheaper and the test is faster to perform. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** confuse **shear force (kN)** with **shear stress (kPa)** — always divide by area.
> - **Do NOT** plot **normal load** vs. **shear load** — always plot **stresses** (i.e., divide by A first).
> - **Always state units** in the final answer (kPa for c, degrees for φ).
> - **Always check** whether the failure was identified from a peak or from 15% strain.
> - In box-area calculations, **60 mm × 60 mm = 3600 mm²** — using the wrong area is the #1 mark-loss reason!

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Concept + Numerical*

**(a)** Describe with a neat sketch the working principle and components of the Direct Shear Test apparatus. List the IS code referred to. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The Direct Shear Test apparatus consists of:
1. **Shear box:** A square box (60 mm × 60 mm × 50 mm) split horizontally into two halves. The lower half rests on rollers and can slide; the upper half is held stationary.
2. **Porous stones:** Placed at the top and bottom of the specimen to permit drainage.
3. **Loading pad:** A ball-jointed cap on top to apply uniform vertical load.
4. **Loading frame:** A screw-jack or lever system to apply normal load P_v through a hanger and dead weights.
5. **Horizontal loading unit:** A motorized gear system pushing the lower box at a constant rate of 1.25 mm/min.
6. **Proving ring:** A calibrated spring element that measures the horizontal shear force P_h.
7. **Dial gauges:** Two gauges measure (i) horizontal shear displacement and (ii) vertical dilation/contraction.
8. **Water reservoir (optional):** The box is sometimes submerged for fully saturated, drained tests.

**Working Principle:** A normal load P_v is applied vertically, producing normal stress σ = P_v/A. A horizontal shear force P_h is then applied at a constant rate. The shear force at failure, P_h(failure), divided by area gives the shear stress τ. The test is repeated at three or more different normal loads. A plot of τ vs. σ yields a straight line whose slope is tan φ and y-intercept is c, in accordance with Coulomb's equation τ_f = c + σ tan φ.

**IS Code:** **IS 2720 (Part 13) – 1986**. *(7 marks: 4 for description, 2 for sketch, 1 for IS code)*

**(b)** In a Direct Shear Test on a sandy soil using a 60 mm × 60 mm shear box, the following data were obtained at failure. Determine the shear parameters c and φ. **(7 marks)**

| Test | Normal Load (N) | Shear Force at Failure (N) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 200 | 180 |
| 2 | 400 | 360 |
| 3 | 600 | 540 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Area of the box A = 60 × 60 = 3600 mm² = 3.6 × 10⁻³ m²

**Step 2:** Compute normal and shear stresses for each test:

For test 1:
$$\sigma_1 = \frac{200}{3.6 \times 10^{-3}} = 55.56 \text{ kPa}$$
$$\tau_1 = \frac{180}{3.6 \times 10^{-3}} = 50.00 \text{ kPa}$$

For test 2:
$$\sigma_2 = \frac{400}{3.6 \times 10^{-3}} = 111.11 \text{ kPa}$$
$$\tau_2 = \frac{360}{3.6 \times 10^{-3}} = 100.00 \text{ kPa}$$

For test 3:
$$\sigma_3 = \frac{600}{3.6 \times 10^{-3}} = 166.67 \text{ kPa}$$
$$\tau_3 = \frac{540}{3.6 \times 10^{-3}} = 150.00 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3:** Notice that τ/σ = 0.90 in all three tests — so the failure line passes through the origin. Therefore **c = 0 kPa** (purely cohesionless sandy soil, as expected).

**Step 4:** Compute the slope between any two points (say, test 1 and test 3):
$$\tan\phi = \frac{\tau_3 - \tau_1}{\sigma_3 - \sigma_1} = \frac{150 - 50}{166.67 - 55.56} = \frac{100}{111.11} = 0.90$$

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}(0.90) = 41.99^{\circ} \approx 42^{\circ}$$

**Final Answer:** **c = 0 kPa** and **φ ≈ 42°** — characteristic of a **dense sand**. *(7 marks: 1 for area, 3 for stress calculations, 2 for c and φ derivation, 1 for final answer with units)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Theory + Graph Interpretation*

**(a)** Explain the Mohr-Coulomb failure criterion. How is it implemented in the Direct Shear Test? What are the limitations of the DST? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **Mohr-Coulomb failure criterion** states that failure in a soil mass occurs along a plane when the shear stress on that plane reaches a limiting value that depends on the normal stress acting on it. Mathematically:
$$\tau_f = c + \sigma \tan\phi$$

**Implementation in DST:** The Direct Shear Test is a direct physical implementation of this criterion. The horizontal plane separating the upper and lower halves of the shear box is the **predetermined failure plane**. By applying a known normal stress σ and measuring the shear stress τ at which failure occurs, one data point (σ, τ_f) of the Mohr-Coulomb envelope is obtained. By repeating the test at different normal stresses, multiple points are generated, and a straight line fitted through them gives the envelope — from which c and φ are extracted.

**Limitations of the Direct Shear Test:**
1. **Forced failure plane:** The test forces failure along a horizontal plane, whereas the actual failure plane in the field depends on the stress state. The plane of least resistance is not necessarily horizontal.
2. **Non-uniform stress distribution:** The stress distribution along the failure plane is not uniform; stress concentrations occur near the edges of the box.
3. **No pore pressure measurement:** The test cannot measure pore water pressure; hence effective stress parameters cannot be directly determined.
4. **Restricted drainage control:** Drainage conditions cannot be controlled precisely as in a triaxial test.
5. **Area correction:** As the lower half slides, the area of contact reduces, requiring an area correction in long-displacement tests.
6. **Limited applicability to clays:** Because drainage cannot be precisely controlled, the test is less reliable for cohesive soils than the triaxial test. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** In a Direct Shear Test conducted on a soil sample, the peak shear stresses at failure for three normal stresses are given below. Plot the failure envelope and determine c and φ. **(7 marks)**

| Normal Stress σ (kPa) | Peak Shear Stress τ (kPa) |
|:---:|:---:|
| 50 | 65 |
| 100 | 95 |
| 150 | 125 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the trend — τ increases linearly with σ. As σ increases by 50 kPa, τ increases by 30 kPa. Hence the slope is constant.

**Step 2:** Compute the slope:
$$\tan\phi = \frac{95 - 65}{100 - 50} = \frac{30}{50} = 0.60$$

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}(0.60) = 30.96^{\circ} \approx 31^{\circ}$$

**Step 3:** Compute c using any data point (e.g., σ = 50 kPa, τ = 65 kPa):
$$c = \tau - \sigma \tan\phi = 65 - (50 \times 0.60) = 65 - 30 = 35 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 4:** Verify using another point (σ = 150 kPa, τ = 125 kPa):
$$c = 125 - (150 \times 0.60) = 125 - 90 = 35 \text{ kPa} \checkmark$$

**Step 5:** Final Answer:
$$\boxed{c = 35 \text{ kPa}, \quad \phi \approx 31^{\circ}}$$

This soil has **moderate cohesion and moderate friction** — typical of a **silty clay or clayey sand**. *(7 marks: 1 for trend, 2 for slope/φ, 3 for c, 1 for final answer)*

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📋 Quick-Fire Revision Points — Memorize Before Exam:**

- **Aim of test:** To determine **shear strength parameters c (cohesion) and φ (angle of internal friction)**.
- **IS Code:** **IS 2720 (Part 13) – 1986**.
- **Specimen size:** **60 mm × 60 mm × 50 mm** depth (square box).
- **Area of standard box:** **A = 3600 mm² = 3.6 × 10⁻³ m²**.
- **Rate of shearing:** **1.25 mm/min** (drained test).
- **Minimum number of tests:** **3**, at different normal loads.
- **Key formulas:**
  - Normal stress: $\sigma = P_v / A$
  - Shear stress: $\tau = P_h / A$
  - Coulomb: $\tau_f = c + \sigma \tan\phi$
- **Shear force from proving ring:** $P_h = C \times d$ (where C is in N/div and d is the dial reading).
- **Graph:** Plot **τ (y-axis) vs σ (x-axis)** — the slope = tan φ, the y-intercept = c.
- **Failure criterion:** Peak load, OR load at **15% strain** (6 mm) if no clear peak.
- **DST is a drained test** — preferred for **sandy/cohesionless** soils.
- **Limitations:** Forced failure plane, non-uniform stress, no pore pressure measurement, area change during shearing.
- **Common unit error to avoid:** 1 N/mm² = 1 MPa = 1000 kPa — always use **kPa** in KTU answers.
- **Always** state c in **kPa** (or kN/m²) and φ in **degrees** in the final answer.
- **Pure sand** → c = 0, only φ matters; **pure clay (φ = 0)** → only c matters.
- **Most common mistake:** Forgetting to divide by area and plotting forces instead of stresses — **deducts 1–2 marks**.
